title: 01-Introduction
content: Perkins&Will consists of hundreds of individual professionals and more than 25 Studios throughout the world. These Studios and individuals are linked by the Perkins&Will tradition of Design Excellence. Excellence takes many forms: Excellence in Design (form, narrative, culture, inspiration, planning) Excellence in Strategic Planning (organizational behavior and strategy) Excellence in Programming (methodology and program efficiencies) Excellence in Management (internal organization and client relationships) Excellence in Building Systems (technologies, performance, assemblies) Excellence in Delivery (methods and models) Excellence in Integrated Services (true integration of all of our disciplines) Excellence in Finance (profitability, methods of procurement) This Project Delivery Manual (PDM) is intended to facilitate the consistent achievement of Design Excellence throughout the firm. There are several interconnected purposes to the PDM: Define consistent documentation standards for all Perkins&Will efforts Facilitate collaboration among Perkins&Will Studios, teams, and individuals Define firm-wide processes to promote high quality design and project delivery Maximize the sustainable design opportunities for every Perkins&Will project Provide a reference manual and educational tool for all Perkins&Will staff Promote efficiency in the development and delivery of Perkins&Will work The Project Delivery Manual (PDM) defines the way that Perkins&Will designs and delivers projects. It is “The Perkins&Will Way”, documenting best practices learned throughout the firm’s history. It’s also a living document, allowing for additions and revisions in recognition of changing industry practices and our growth and evolution as a firm. It is not intended to stifle innovation; in fact, innovation is encouraged. A project team may find that an alternative approach is best for a particular project, or that a new method or tool should be tested. These alternative approaches are welcome and can lead to benefits to our projects, our Studios and the Firm, but they must be reviewed and discussed with your Studio’s Technical Director, to manage our risks while maintaining standards and quality. Suggestions for changes in the PDM, including those based on alternative processes, should be addressed to the Technical Design Community (TDC) Leadership, using the General channel on the TDC MS Team. Project teams may encounter situations where project delivery requirements from a client or Authority Having Jurisdiction (AHJ) vary from the PDM. Regulatory, legal, and client requirements always take priority over those in this PDM. In those cases, it is the responsibility of the project team to identify the variances from the PDM, review them with your Studio’s Technical Director, document them, and distribute the document to the entire project team.

title: Contracts/agreements and Proposals
content: The terms “contract” and “agreement,” as they are used in the following sections, are defined to mean a document, signed by a representative of Perkins&Will and another party (client or consultant), that establishes a legal and financial framework for Perkins&Will to provide services to a client or for a consultant to provide services to Perkins&Will. Policy StatementIt is Perkins&Will policy—and the law in many states—not to perform services without a signed (“executed“) contract with the client. The firm’s template agreements, based on standard AIA agreements, can be found as templates in Microsoft Word in the pwAIA template folder. A Letter of Intent or signed proposal may be acceptable as an interim measure, or even an acceptable form of agreement, under certain conditions (see the Letters of Intent section below). Consult with the Legal Department to confirm acceptability.Perkins&Will’s Legal Department will review contracts or other forms of final agreement for insurability issues. Additionally, contracts must be forwarded to our Legal Department for assistance with development and/or review. Contracts should be routed for review by using the Legal Review Request Form.Executing Contracts and AgreementsPerkins&Will’s bylaws authorize only officers of the company to sign documents that bind Perkins&Will. The bylaws state that Principals are officers of the company, therefore Principals are authorized to sign contracts and other legal instruments binding the organization. However, since the public can reasonably believe that Associate Principals also have the power to act on behalf of Perkins&Will, Perkins&Will takes the position that Associate Principals are also authorized to sign contracts and other legal documents on behalf of Perkins&Will. Therefore, only Principals and Associate Principals may sign:· Project Contracts (including subconsultant contracts)· Proposals (a non-P/AP may co-sign with a P/AP)· Nondisclosure / Confidentiality Agreements (NDAs)· Contract Amendments (including additional services amendments and MSA service orders)· Consents to Assignment· Lender Consents· Lien Waivers· EFTAs· Any other legal documents binding Perkins&WillLetters Of IntentIf a contract has not yet been signed by Perkins&Will and the client, it is the responsibility of the managing principal and the project manager to obtain a signed Letter of Intent (LOI) from the client before the commencement of services. The LOI must incorporate or reference the written proposal, or include an agreed-upon definition of fees, schedule, and scope of services, as well as one or more of the following:1. Authorization to proceed2. Phases of service3. Anticipated deliverables4. Direction to seek (or not to seek) LEED® Certification5. Definition and rates of reimbursable expenses6. Terms of compensation7. Payment terms8. Hourly rate scheduleIf the full scope of the project or services is not known, the letter should address the specific preliminary services to be provided to determine a preliminary scope that will allow work on the project to commence (e.g., feasibility studies or programming). Additionally, the letter must be signed by a Perkins&Will principal or associate principal and an authorized representative of the client.Work may proceed under the terms outlined in the letter of intent provided that negotiations on the final agreement continue in good faith at a reasonable pace and the client remains current with the agreed-upon payments.Should any client be unwilling to provide the agreements and authorization described above, work should not proceed without the express approval of the office’s managing director.Forms of AgreementContracts should define the scope of services, schedule, fees, and other conditions. Once drafted, the contract is to be reviewed and approved by the project’s managing principal or the Studio’s Director of Operations and the Legal Department. Like proposals, contracts should also be reviewed by the Design and Technical Directors to ensure that the project fees and schedule allow for a successful project design and delivery.All supporting documents (such as the initial proposal) should be formally referenced as exhibits and attached to the contract to avoid misunderstandings of scope or deliverables.If there are original hard copies of executed contracts, they are to be filed with the Studio's accounting department. Electronic copies of the contracts should be filed in the Contracts area of PMTK in the Project App, and be accessible to any Perkins&Will team member.It is Perkins&Will’s practice to use our Legal Department to negotiate contracts. It is mandatory that the project’s managing principal, the Studio’s Managing Director or Director of Operations review all contracts with the Legal Department. The Legal Department is available to assist with the review, interpretation, and negotiations of all contracts. An attorney outside the firm should not be engaged unless approved by the Legal Department.It is important to execute all consultant contracts as early as possible, preferably before any services begin. In the case where the client has authorized services to begin in writing and we need to get the consultant started before an owner-architect contract has been executed, the Consultant Interim Letter of Agreement should be used. As soon as the prime agreement has been executed, it is important to execute the Perkins&Will standard subconsultant agreement (PW Template AIA C401) right away. You can attach an unsigned copy of the consultant’s proposal as the scope exhibit as indicated in the template. It is Perkins&Will’s policy to never sign a consultant’s proposal, no matter how small the scope. Signing a consultant’s proposal forms a binding contract and it will be on the consultant’s terms which will heavily favor the consultant and give them no reason to sign our subconsultant agreement. If you have questions about your consultant’s special circumstances, ask one of the Perkins&Will attorneys for guidance. The Owner-Architect Agreement (the “Prime Agreement”) is to be attached as an exhibit to the subconsultant agreement so that the consultant is legally bound by the same terms and conditions as Perkins&Will. Before attaching the Prime Agreement as an exhibit, confidential information (such as fees and hourly rates) should be redacted.”For information on how to handle amendments to our agreements, see Requests for Additional Services section.All proposals must be reviewed by a principal or associate principal before submission to the client or consultant. Principals or associate principals must execute contracts or letters of intent for projects with an NSR of $1,000,000 or less. Only principals can sign proposals or contracts exceeding a NSR (net service revenue) of $1,000,000.Contracts with NSR in excess of $3,000,000 or that incorporate a non-traditional scope, service, or business relationship require additional approval by the Risk Committee. Certain contracts will require approval by a vote of the Board of Directors, at their discretion.Allow 5 to 10 business days for contract review and approval by in-house Legal Department. Allow at least 10 to 15 business days for contracts requiring approval by the Risk Management Committee. You can track progress and scheduling of contract reviews using the Legal Department Contract Calendar.Contract Review1. Use the Perkins&Will version of the standard AIA C401-2007 subconsultant agreement for every subconsultant. It contains terms and conditions that protect us in relation to insurance maintained by our subconsultants. The most current version can be found as a template in Microsoft Word in the pwAIA template folder.2. Send client-generated prime contracts (or subcontracts when we’re a subconsultant) to the Legal Department for review with sufficient time to prepare a substantive response. Expect comments to the insurance section and understand that those comments are important and must be resolved before signing the contract.3. Don’t make contractual promises that may complicate coverage, such as (a) warranties or guarantees; (b) standards of care beyond negligence; (c) obligations to defend others; or (d) absolute duties or obligations which may result in liability without proof of negligence (e.g., stating that the Contract Documents will be “without errors”).4. Don’t enter into joint ventures or other legal arrangements that may make us responsible for the acts of others, or which differ from standard subconsultant agreements, without the written approval of the Legal Department.No one in Perkins&Will is authorized to approve or enter into contract terms which are uninsurable, or may result in uninsurable risk, without the prior written consent of the Risk Committee.Electronic File Transfer Agreements / EFTAAn Electronic File Transfer Agreement (EFTA) must be executed before sending electronic design documents (AutoCAD files, Revit models) to an outside party who is not under contract with Perkins&Will.Four EFTA forms are available for use in transferring electronic design documents, depending upon the party to whom the transfer is being made. EFTA forms are available on the Legal Department page and are described below.· The EFTA (Contractor or Subcontractor) is to be used when sending files directly to the prime contractor (or multiple prime contractors where applicable.) Note that this form is to be executed by the prime contractor (general contractor, multiple prime contractors or construction manager) and then applies to all subcontractors. Individual EFTA forms are not required for subcontractors.· The EFTA (Designer) is to be used when sending files to design professionals to whom we are not contracted.· The EFTA (Former Client) is to be used when sending files of a previously completed project to the client who originally hired Perkins&Will for that project.· The EFTA (Third Party Recipient) is to be used when sending files to a third party that does not fall into one of the specific categories above.A PDF-type file of the executed Electronic File Transfer Agreement, should be filed in the Contracts area of PMTK in the Project App before delivery of the data. When the forms are transmitted electronically, the project manager is required to verify that the executed agreement has not been altered.It is important to understand that unless Perkins&Will has a legal relationship with the contractor (as in, for example, a design-build project), we are not required to provide information to the contractor other than as specifically required by the Owner-Architect Agreement. Nonetheless, it is our usual practice to provide electronic drawings or models for use as backgrounds for coordination purposes, provided that the conditions of the EFTA are met.AIA Standard Agreement Forms and DocumentsThe standard agreements for use on Perkins&Will projects can be found as templates in Microsoft Word in the pwAIA template folder. For agreements with clients, use the B-101 or B-103 templates. For agreements with consultants, use the C-401 template. Others are available for non-standard projects.The full set of PDF copies of the Perkins&Will standard AIA agreements are available on the Legal Department page. It is Perkins&Will’s policy to use our in-house edited versions of the AIA documents included in Microsoft Word unless clients insist on using their own form of agreement.Note: For projects in Canada, refer to the Canadian Construction Documentation Council for applicable agreement forms and documents (www.ccdc.org).

title: Financial Performance of Projects
content: Requests for Additional ServicesThis is an area that requires the focus and constant attention of the project manager and managing principal, as it directly impacts profitability and client relationships. Often, requests for additional services may be overlooked due to the fear of irritating a client, or because of a loosely defined Scope of Services.Techniques available to mitigate these problems include:1. Clearly defining the scope of services: This should be reviewed in detail with the client before beginning work. Renderings, models, and production of 3-D imagery should be clearly defined as basic or additional services.2. Presenting the need for additional services to a client before undertaking the work: Saying, “Perkins&Will would be happy to perform those services for you. Would you like us to submit a proposal?” is easier and usually more successful than requesting compensation after the work is done.What constitutes additional services depends on the actual terms of the Owner-Architect Agreement for the project. According to the AIA B101 or B103 Standard Form of Agreement Between Owner and Architect, any of the following might be considered as an additional service:1. Programming2. Existing facilities surveys3. Building information modeling4. Detailed cost estimating5. Expanded on-site project representation6. Conformed construction documents7. As-designed record drawings8. As-constructed record drawings9. Pre- and/or post-occupancy evaluation10. Coordination of owner’s consultants11. Commissioning12. LEED® (or similar) certification documentation13. Fast-track design services14. Furniture, furnishings, and equipment (FFE) design15. Reviewing a submittal out of sequence from the approved submittal schedule16. Responding to RFIs that are not prepared per the Contract Documents (i.e., “frivolous RFIs”)17. Preparing Change Orders or CCDs that require evaluation of contractor proposals or changes to the documents18. Evaluating an extensive number of claims19. Evaluating substitution requests from the contractor and changing the documents accordingly20. Providing construction phase services 60 days after either Substantial Completion or the anticipated date of Substantial Completion as defined in the contractIf the agreement is not based on a standard AIA agreement form, make sure that basic and additional services are clearly defined.For additional services on a project that is already under contract with the client, a proposal letter should be sent to the client before the start of the additional service work. The proposal letter should specify the scope, fee, and schedule of the additional services; refer to the terms and conditions of the base contract; and be signed by an authorized Perkins&Will individual. When we receive the copy signed by the client, we can begin the work.It is the policy of Perkins&Will that the design team does not proceed with additional services work, during any phase, until authorized in writing by the owner.Financial Procedures TerminologyVisit the PMTK QuickStart Guide for explanations and definitions of the Perkins&Will financial procedures terminology.

title: Project Startup and Project Planning
content: Goals StatementFor Perkins&Will to design a project that meets the client’s goals, those goals must be understood by the design team. A goal-setting or visioning session should be scheduled as early as possible, once a project has started. All major stakeholders, including members of the owner’s team (project management, facilities management and users), along with key members of the Perkins&Will team, should participate. If there is a construction manager on the project team, they should also be represented. The session should be led by Perkins&Will, typically by the project manager or the managing principal. After all stakeholders have spoken, well-defined goals should be agreed to and documented. The project team can then use these goals as guides for making decisions throughout the project.The determination of a project's Architect of Record (AOR) must be made as early in the project as possible, preferably at the pursuit stage, but no later than project kickoff. See Chapter 3 Quality Program, Quality Program Roles and Responsibilities for team member responsibilities. Also see the Sealing Documents section in Chapter 5 for more information.Kickoff MeetingsProject KickoffThe project manager is responsible for scheduling and leading a kickoff meeting with the managing director, managing principal, design principal, director of operations, and the technical director to review the work plan material.Phase KickoffAt the start of each project phase, the project manager is responsible for scheduling and leading a meeting with the Perkins&Will design team, including consultants under contract to us. The project goals and the work plan material should be shared with the team so that the individuals understand their roles and responsibilities, project milestones and the team organization. A template of an Internal Team Kickoff Meeting Agenda is available in Microsoft Word in the pwGeneral template folder.Internal Design Team Construction Phase KickoffFollowing execution of the Owner-Contractor Agreement, the project manager will schedule a CCA kickoff meeting with the Perkins&Will team and our consultants to establish the procedures that will be followed by all members of the design team during construction. In addition, checklists are available to help the team perform CCA responsibilities at the start of construction, during construction, and at the end of construction (see the Construction Contract Administration chapter.)Project Schedule and BudgetVisit the PMTK QuickStart Guide for explanations and definitions related to building, monitoring and maintaining project schedules and budgets..Construction Contract Administration (CCA) Phase BudgetingIt is not possible to have a single rule of thumb to determine the appropriate construction phase budget. For some projects, 20% of the fee will be adequate; on others, 30% may not be enough. Rather than base the CCA budget on a percentage of the architectural fee, the project manager needs to consider the amount of effort that will be required over the duration of the construction schedule. For instance, if it is anticipated that a full-time project architect will be needed over a two-year construction period, then the budget should be 2 years x 2,080 hours/year = 4,160 PA hours. It doesn’t matter if that is 20%, 25%, or 30% (or more) of the architectural fee; as long as it is the best estimate for what it will actually take to perform our contractual responsibilities.It is important that the duration of construction be established in our agreement with the owner, since that is the basis of our construction phase budgeting. It is also important that the project manager and the team be diligent in identifying potential additional services. See the Requests for Additional Services section for more information.CCA Project Team OrganizationIt is Perkins&Will’s goal to have the core design team be consistent from the design phases through the construction phase. In most cases, this means the project architect and one or more junior team members will perform the CCA services. The project manager will continue to take care of contractual issues and will remain the primary client contact, but the project architect will have the most interactions with the client, contractors and consultants. The advantage to this approach is that, even if the team has limited CCA experience, they can use their knowledge of the project’s design and the guidance provided in CHAPTER 7: CONSTRUCTION CONTRACT ADMINISTRATION, to provide the required CCA services.

title: Project Document Management
content:
Active Project Filing ProtocolsDocuments That Must Be FiledHard copiesAssemble any hard copies of certified or signed documents, scan and save in the project files. Originals should be discarded.All project-related original hard-copy documents to and from clients, consultants, contractors, and authorities having jurisdiction (AHJs). (Note: “Original” means the actual document that was sent to Perkins&Will, which could be a duplicate of an original that was not sent to us.)Submittal samples that were returned to the contractor with a review action (Note: Samples will be discarded at project closeout, see CHAPTER 8: PROJECT CLOSE-OUT.)Electronic FilesElectronic documents sent to or received from client, consultants, contractors, and AHJs through email.Contract Documents or correspondence that refer to Contract Documents—i.e., all contracts and contract amendments and addenda, whether executed or not, including email, letter, and fax correspondence related to contracts.All deliverables which document fulfillment of contract duties or obligations, such as final deliverables (drawings and specifications) called for in the contract for SD / DD / CD phases, reports required to be prepared and delivered, submittals to be reviewed and responded to, RFIs presented and answered, etc.All documents and correspondence (including email) that document or refer to issues with performance and our response to that correspondence. Carefully consider whether to keep interim or intermediate steps or positions, since they may conflict with, and may not be relevant to, our final professional opinion and documentation.An electronic copy of each submittal returned electronically to the contractor with a review action.Field observation reports and associated construction site photographs.Documents That Should Not Be FiledDuplicate copies of original hard-copy documents to and from client, consultants, contractor, and AHJs, when the original is filed electronically. The exception to this is if there are important notations made on a duplicate that supplement information on the original.In-house Quality Assurance or Quality Control marked-up sets of drawings or project manuals (unless they are required to be retained in our Owner-Architect Agreement)Communication (hard copy and email) internal to Perkins&Will that has not been sent or copied to anyone outside the firm.Draft versions of documents sent to client, consultants, contractor, and AHJs (unless the draft version was sent to them for review and comment).Marked-up “scratch” copies of submittals.Personal email.Personal notes.Documents on Local Computer Hard Drives or in Microsoft Outlook FoldersProject-related documents or emails that meet the criteria identified as Documents That Must Be Filed for filing are not to be saved on your local computer hard drive. Documents must be filed in the project’s SharePoint and P drive folder structures; emails should be saved in Microsoft Outlook in a project-specific folder.Design PortfolioThe design principal and project leadership (PM and PA) are to coordinate photography and project process documentation to create a ‘design database’ within Project App. This ‘database’ of photographs (in progress and final, professional), diagrams and other project-appropriate graphics is to be utilized as material for marketing, publications, DEAR submissions, awards submissions, and internal/external project collateral.Project sheets for marketing purposes are to be reviewed and updated with the appropriate final narratives and graphic collateral.The Project App design portfolio is to consist of files along with other project information and is to be utilized as a resource.The final design drawings or record drawings are to be uploaded to Artifact. Coordinate this process with the local Digital Practice team. Archiving of Project DocumentsAt the end of the project, when all of our contractual services have been completed, and all project-related invoices (for both payments to us and payments from us to our consultants) have been paid, project files must be archived, following procedures described in CHAPTER 8: PROJECT CLOSE-OUT.Project Folder StructureThe Project Folder Structure is the framework within which individual project documents are filed. While many index structures are possible, this is the structure Perkins&Will has chosen to use across the entire firm. This structure establishes a common language for us to communicate and share critical project information, regardless of the office, discipline or market sector leading the project. It is based on our collective experience of the types of information required over the course of a project. It is understood that not every folder may be used on every project; it is reasonable to assume that some may remain empty. Use this structure to file your project’s documents, so that others can readily find information. Do not create filing structures different from these standards; this can lead to frustration and wasted time as others struggle to understand your logic in creating them.The current Project Folder Structure is an evolution of our file storage that leverages our Cloud-based platforms for file sharing and collaboration within our teams and with external Clients and Consultants. Project files will be stored in 3 distinct locations:· P: Drive - DESIGN, containing all modeling, graphics and presentation files, except Revit models (which now reside in BIM360.)· MS Teams - DOCS, divided into 3 broad categories: Deliverables, Design Team and PW Internal Team. Deliverables: files that are for external transfer. Design Team: files that are for both internal use and that can be shared with our consultants. PW Internal Team: private files restricted to Perkins&Will access, See below for the updated structure and a description of the suggested contents for each folder.· BIM360, contains Revit filesAt the beginning of every project, after a project number has been assigned, the IT manager will create project folders on the P:drive of the Perkins&Will Studio holding the prime contract for the project, on a project-specific MS Team and in BIM360. All project files are based on the base six-digit project number assigned by the accounting staff; this base project number is to remain unchanged throughout the course of the project. Sub-numbers may become necessary as the project progresses; the project manager needs to contact Accounting for these sub-numbers to be recorded in Deltek Vision. The project team is responsible for deleting folders once all recipients have downloaded the files, or when the team-defined expiration date arrives.**Create individual folders for file transfers. Obsolete links should be discarded. Except as noted below, subfolders are not
recommended.**If warranted, create individual folders for linked file type (e.g. diagrams, facades,
photos, plans, sections, visualizations, etc.) Publish Clarity **Create individual folders for publish events: yyyy-mm-dd_Publish Event In-Progress **Create individual folders for in-progress publish events: yyyy-mm-dd_In-Progress Event Record Sets **Create individual folders for record deliverables: yyyy-mm-dd_Issue Title Renders **Create individual folders for milestone renderings: yyyy-mm-dd_Milestone Title Study All files related to individual team member design studies, regardless of application**Create individual folders for team members: FIRSTNAME_LASTNAME Electronic File and Document NamingPerkins&Will has adopted a standard format for the naming of all electronic files stored in the folder structure noted above. This format is intended to ease searching for files through consistent organization of information. It also includes keywords as a “shorthand” version of content to alert people to a file’s contents. Use of other file naming formats will make it harder for people to find documents within the project’s files. Do not invent new file naming formats for your projects.File Naming ConventionAll files in the Consultants and Teams folder should be given document names that contain the following four fields: Field 1 Field 2 Field 3 Field 4 Identifier (with # where applicable) YYYY-MM- DD Note/Keyword Program extension (automatic) For clarity, within a document name use an underscore (_) between fields 1 to 3, and a period (.) before field 4. Use a hyphen (-) to connect information within a field (see examples below).Example 1: ltr_2025-03-19_file naming.docxExample 2: asi01_2025-03-19_RFI 001 response.docxField 1 of a document name is a file (document) Identifier indicating the type of document. See below for Perkins&Will standard identifiers. Note that some identifiers can include a number, as shown in Example 2.Example 1: ltr_Example 2: asi01_Field 2 is the Date the document is issued, in the format YYYY-MM-DD (year, month, day) to allow chronological sorting. Always use a preceding “0” for single-digit numbers.Example 1: ltr_2025-03-19_Example 2: asi01_2025-03-19_Field 3 is a brief Descriptive Note or Key Word(s) used to describe the document content.Example 1: ltr_2025-03-19_file namingExample 2: asi01_2025-03-19_RFI 001 responseField 4, the Program Extension, must be separated from the preceding portion of the document name by a period (part of the extension), and is usually automatically affixed to the end of the document name by the program used to create the document. The program extension is necessary to enable the document to be opened in the future. Examples of the Program Extensions are: for Microsoft Word documents, .docx; or for Microsoft Excel spreadsheets, .xlsx. The program extension should not be changed manually.Example 1: ltr_2023-07-29_file naming.docxExample 2: asi01_2025-03-19_RFI 001 response.docxAll documents are to be saved in the appropriate Project File Index Folder (see above Project Folder Structure) before printing to ensure the automatic attachment of the document name and path into the footer of the printed document. Perkins&Will Standard Identifiers (for use in document naming) Description Identifier Addendum add (with # in Field 1) Agenda agn Architect’s Supplemental Instructions asi (with # in Field 1) Certificate of Substantial Completion csc Checklist chk Construction Change Directive ccd (with # in Field 1) Contract, Agreement, Letter of Intent cont Electronic File Transfer Agreement efta Field Observation Report for (with # in Field 1) Letter ltr Meeting Minutes mm Memorandum memo Notice of Noncompliance nnc Photograph or Video ph Presentation pres Program prog Proposal prop Proposal Request pr (with # in Field 1) Report rpt Schedule sch Sketch sk Work Authorization wa Email ProtocolsEmail is considered legal and official project correspondence and should be written with the same level of care as any other professional correspondence. That includes correct spelling, grammar, and punctuation. Every email that is sent is a representation of Perkins&Will and should be treated as such. The following list of “dos and don’ts” should be used to help make your emails communicate what you intend, without annoying or offending the recipients or placing Perkins&Will at unnecessary risk.Email correspondence should be managed and stored by creating project folders in Microsoft Outlook and dragged/dropped into the appropriate folders. Email Dos and Don’ts1. Proof read your emails before you send them.2. If you aren’t prepared to see your email printed on the front page of the New York Times (or Chicago Tribune, London Times, etc.), don’t send it.3. Be concise and to the point. (Many of your emails will be read only on a small hand-held device screen.)4. Use proper spelling, grammar and punctuation.5. Address the recipient by name in the greeting.6. Include a closing (e.g. Regards, Thanks, Sincerely) and your official Perkins&Will Signature7. Respond in a timely manner.8. Do not attach unnecessary files (again, consider the hand-held devices).9. Do not overuse the high priority option.10. Do not write in CAPITALS unless you really need to emphasize something.11. Don’t leave out the string of previous email messages.12. Do not overuse “Reply to All.”13. Take care with abbreviations and don’t use emojis.14. Do not forward chain letters.15. Do not request delivery receipts.16. Do not use email to discuss confidential information.17. Include a meaningful subject in the subject line, starting with the project name and number, then the topic.18. Avoid using “Urgent” or “Important” in your subject line unless it really is urgent or important.19. Avoid long sentences. And long paragraphs. And long emails. (again, consider the hand-held devices.)20. Don’t send or forward emails containing libelous, offensive, racist or obscene remarks. (And remember, it is the recipient who is the judge of what is libelous, offensive, racist or obscene.)21. Don’t reply to spam.22. Use the cc: field sparingly.

title: Construction Cost Management
content: In order to understand Perkins&Will’s typical role relative to construction costs, it is important to clarify two distinct activities—cost estimating and cost management—and Perkins&Will’s role in each.· Cost Estimating: The process of identifying the estimated construction cost of the project at the time of bid. The cost estimate should include all the work documented by the design team at the time of the estimate, as well as reasonable assumptions to include work intended but not yet shown. The estimate should include contractor general conditions costs, overhead and profit. It should include contingencies for design (appropriate to the project phase) and construction. It should be based on current market costs and include an escalation factor to a specific point in the future (typically the time of bid or mid-point of construction.)· Cost Management: The process of managing the owner’s team, the design team, and the cost estimators to ensure that the construction cost at the time of bid is within the owner’s budget. This encompasses a broad range of activities: working with the cost experts to develop a realistic cost model, producing a work plan that identifies costing milestones, designing the project to the budget, evaluating the cost estimates and reporting to the owner, and establishing value engineering procedures that maintain the desired level of quality in the project.While neither the architect nor the owner has control over the contractor’s method of determining bid prices, the design must typically be within the owner’s budget and should satisfy the owner’s functional, programmatic, and operational requirements. The best means of delivering a cost-appropriate design is through accurate estimating.Estimates are a means of establishing a value for the “Cost of the Work.” This includes “…the total cost to the Owner to construct all elements of the Project designed and specified by the Architect and shall include contractor’s general conditions costs, overhead and profit. The Cost of the Work does not include the compensation of the Architect, the costs of the land, rights-of-way, financing, contingencies for changes in the work or other costs that are the responsibility of the Owner.” (AIA Document B101-2017, Standard Form of Agreement Between Owner and Architect, Section 6.1)The standard AIA B101 Owner-Architect Agreement includes requirements for cost estimates provided by the architect for each design phase. Since Perkins&Will has no internal cost estimators, we rely on cost consultants, under contract to us, to provide this service. However, the responsibility to the owner for the timeliness and accuracy of the estimate is Perkins&Will’s. In some cases, the owner may opt to shift the responsibility for cost estimates to another party, such as a construction manager providing preconstruction services.AIA Document B101-2017, section 6.3, defines the kind of cost estimate that is included in basic services: “The Architect’s estimate of the Cost of the Work shall be based on current area, volume or similar conceptual estimating techniques. If the Owner requests detailed cost estimating services, the Architect shall provide these services as an Additional Service…”B101 also states that “the Architect shall be permitted to include contingencies for design, bidding and price escalation; to determine what materials, equipment, component systems and types of construction are to be included in the Contract Documents; to make reasonable adjustments in the program scope of the Project; and to include in the Contract Documents alternate bids as may be necessary to adjust the estimate of the Cost of the Work to meet the Owner’s budget…”

title: Introduction
content: This program defines the Perkins&Will Quality Assurance (QA) and Quality Control (QC) processes. The program differentiates QA, which is the project team’s ongoing responsibility, from QC, which is the responsibility of an outside reviewer and is intended to be a check that QA has been done. The program described in the following sections is the ‘baseline’ that every Studio is expected to implement; Studios may provide enhancements that exceed the requirements here, if they so choose.Note that these same processes and tools apply to ALL projects, including small and stand-alone interior design projects. For those projects, which tend to have smaller scopes, smaller teams and abridged schedules, the activities will be shorter and more focused but retain the same basic character and format. Reduced time, scope and team does not make any of these processes less applicable or less valuable in ensuring Quality in our work. https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/Hk6xZdcovhTvl0O auto false 14px

title: Quality Assurance
content:
QA Empirical DefinitionThe planned and systematic set of procedures necessary to ensure that the services and documents meet industry-established performance and technical requirements and conform to applicable codes and regulations and the requirements of the professional service agreement and the client program.QA Goals1. Process:a. Engage in a consistent process where the design intent is clearly conveyed within a logically structured set of documents that contains drawings, narratives, and specifications.b. Reinforce the fact that QA is the responsibility of all team members, not one individual, particularly not the QC reviewer.c. Ensure technical accuracy and coordination within the documents and among all disciplines.d. Perform periodic technical reviews/pinups (at least once during SD and DD) involving the office technical director, specifications writer, and senior technical staff not directly involved with the project.e. Increase team efficiency and productivity, reduce time spent by the team and increase profit.2. Coordination:a. Ensure that design and coordination issues are discovered and resolved to prevent delays inthe delivery of the documents.b. Coordinate with interdisciplinary team members within Perkins&Will and with external consultants.3. Documentation:a. Produce clear and coordinated documents that meet contractual requirements and Perkins&Will quality standards, thus minimizing addenda, revisions and Change Orders.b. Use a consistent method of conveying information (sheet notes, keynotes, graphic conventions, etc.)c. title: Quality Control
content: QC Empirical DefinitionQuality Control is the systematic examination of documents to ensure that we have performed our Quality Assurance processes and that we meet client, contractual, and regulatory requirements, as well as Perkins&Will’s standards published in this PDM. Quality Control uses Perkins&Will’s senior technical expertise to verify and document that the Quality Assurance has been performed.QC Program OverviewNote that the Quality Control process applies to all Perkins&Will deliverables, regardless of project type, practice or scope. If we are releasing a deliverable externally, a knowledgeable person outside the project team must review it for quality.This is equally true for traditional architectural and interior design projects and for projects in Landscape Architecture, Urban Design, Branded Environments and Advisory Services.From the smallest test fit to the largest master plan, every deliverable must have a QC review. For projects that do not fit the specific process outlined here, see your Studio Technical Director to determine the correct process and timeline for Quality Control.Perkins&Will Quality Control (QC) is the process by which individuals outside the design team confirm that the project documents are in conformance with the Perkins&Will quality standards, as documented in the PDM. Quality Control is not intended to replace the day-to-day quality assurance efforts of the project team. Instead, Quality Control is intended to be a focused, limited review.Quality Control includes reviewing the drawings and project manual to ensure that project requirements have been met.Review comments from the Quality Control reviewer are in the form of questions (“Why did you do this?”), suggestions (“You should consider this.”), or requirements (“Do this, not that.”). They should be clearly stated to enable the design team to readily respond. They are not intended to be comprehensive redlines to address every instance of issues identified. QC comments should be focused on technical issues related to code and accessibility compliance, constructability, continuity of systems, and technical soundness, rather than on graphic, typographic, or layout issues that should be addressed as part of Quality Assurance. The design team has the responsibility to analyze QC comments, respond with answers or corrections, and incorporate them throughout the entire set of documents. The design team is also responsible for discussing with the Quality Control reviewer any comments that they disagree with or that require clarification.Reviews are required prior to the issuance of all project deliverables, at each phase. Key points to consider when scheduling these reviews are: the project’s complexity, time required for the QC Review and the time needed for the project team to understand comments and incorporate corrections before documents are issued.The following QC tools are templates in Word and can be accessed by selecting ‘New’ from the File tab:1. QA/QC Tools Checklist2. Quality Control Review Topics ListQC Process Narrative1. The Technical Director uses the PMTK to identify projects nearing a deliverable milestone. The Technical Director assigns a QC reviewer to each project. (In some cases, the technical director may be the QC reviewer.)2. The Project Architect coordinates delivery of documents to the QC reviewer. The preferred method of review is the Bluebeam Studio session that allows the reviewer and the project team to collaborate effectively.3. QC reviewer reviews documents, makes comments and, when deemed valuable, meets with the project architect and team members to give an overview of the comments and to review specific areas that require particular attention.4. Project architect meets with the project team to review remaining comments and assign responsibilities for correction or further development.5. Project team members make corrections and highlight comments as they are addressed. If comments are not addressed, the team member notes the reason on the QC set for Project Architect review.6. Project team discards the QC review set once all comments have been addressed.

title: Quality Program Roles and Responsibilities
content:
Role Quality Assurance Responsibilities Quality Control Responsibilities Technical Director Manages office wide scheduling and staffing of Pull Planning, Mockup/Cartoon Set Reviews and Mid-Phase Reviews. Monitors project staffing to ensure each team’s ability to perform QA tasks. Monitors office wide performance of the Quality Program. Manages scheduling of QC reviewers. Reviews unresolved QC issues with the PA. Design Director Monitors project staffing to ensure each team's ability to perform QA tasks and advance the design appropriately.Monitors project development for appropriate development of the design and advancement of the concept. Managing Principal Includes consultants’ QA/QC requirements in architect-consultant contracts.
Determines level of client (and CM, where applicable) involvement in the QA process.Determine, in conjunction with the Project Manager and Project Architect, who will serve as Architect of Record (AOR) for the project. Has the authority and responsibility to withhold the issuance of documents pending the QC process's satisfactory completion. Project Manager Team assignments (ensure adequate staffing), including QA responsibilities. Integrates QA activities into the project schedule. Manages client’s deliverable expectations. Select and contract with consultants; ongoing management of consultant scope of services - incorporating QA expectations of each. Ensures consultants perform their own quality assurance process.Identify the AOR in the Project AppConfirm that AOR is engaged in the project and exercising appropriate control and oversight as required by local laws and rules. Ensures adequate budget and schedule for QC reviews. Reviews QC assignment with the Technical Director to ensure availability of the reviewer. Informs the QC reviewer about upcoming deliverables and schedule changes. Project Designer Oversees design quality and adherence to design intent. Responsible for reviewing the QC comments to verify that the development of the project and direction suggested by the QC reviewer is consistent with the project design intent. Project Architect Has primary responsibility for ensuring that QA is performed by team members. Assists PM in scheduling QA activities. Schedules Mid-Phase Reviews. Responsible for the technical quality of the documents (using checklists as a guide for issues/topics). Assists the PM in customizing QA processes for each project. Management of internal project team’s work assignments. Develops of mockup sets/drawing organization/ deliverables list and tracks development. Leads coordination among consultants and between drawings and specifications. Leads implementation of the Specifications Preparation Procedures. Reviews and marks up documents and oversees corrections before QC review. Assigns presentation assignments for Mid-Phase Reviews. Distributes QA Checklists to team members. Develops and maintains the BIM Execution Plan.Ensures regular engagement of the AOR throughout the design phases and coordinates signing and sealing of documents prior to permit or other official issuance. Communicates with the Technical Director and QC reviewer about any unresolved issues. Resolves issues prior to release of documents. Provides the QC Reviewers with all the necessary documents for quality reviews. Responds to the comments from the Quality Control Reviewer and oversees corrections before issuing the documents. SpecificationsWriter Production of specifications.Coordination of drawings and specifications with Project Architect. Addresses QC comments on the specifications. Each Project Team Member Each team member checks his or her own work. BIM Management. Presentation at Mid-Phase Reviews. Completion of QA Checklists. Addresses QC comments. Alerts the PA and PM to QC comments that require additional discussion and/or resolution. QC reviewer Advisory role Triages documents to verify that the Design Team performed Quality Assurance. Reviews the project deliverables on the schedule established by the project Manager, using the QC topics list as a guide. Meets with the PA before the QC review to understand the project scope, schedule, goals, and document structure. Meets with the managing principal, PM, PA, and technical director if issues or concerns are raised that would suggest delaying the document issuance.

title: Introduction
content: The Project Manual and Construction Drawings together comprise Perkins&Will’s Contract Documents, which are to be coordinated with the owner’s construction contract with the contractor. The Project Manual, which includes the specifications, is the responsibility of the project team and the Specifications Writer. The Project Manual is the part of the Contract Documents that defines:1. How the project will be bid/procured.a. Instructions to the bidders as to the procedures for preparing and submitting bids. (These Procurement and Contracting Requirements are not Contract Documents but are included in the Project Manual).2. How the Contract will be administered.a. The Contracting requirements include Instructions to the Contractor on procedures to be followed during Construction, including the processes for submittals, review, and recordkeeping, and the information to be transferred to the owner upon completion of construction.3. The physical qualities and characteristics of construction products, components, equipment, and materials, including fabrication, installation, testing, cleaning, and protection.Specifications and Construction Drawings are legally binding Contract Documents, so it is imperative that we produce them so they are unambiguous in defining our design intent. Specification contents, particularly terminology, products, or materials, must be tightly coordinated with the Contract Drawings to avoid conflicts or a lack of clarity. Conflicts in our Contract Documents will result in Requests for Interpretation (RFIs) and Change Orders. This chapter of the Project Delivery Manual describes Perkins&Will’s approach to developing Project Manuals for our projects.See the Signing and Sealing Documents section in Chapter 5 for information and responsibilities related to the project's Architect of Record. Hub (perkinswill.com) https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/lvdZEv0ElhCM3A9 auto false 14px Link to full size Specifications Process Map - Executive Summary https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/MSgUjZiErEz0e7z auto false 14px Link to Full Size Specifications Process Map - Detailed

title: Project Manual Components
content: The Project Manual is composed of Procurement Requirements, Contracting Requirements, and Specifications.Procurement (Bidding) RequirementsThis section generally includes the Invitation to Bid (or Advertisement for Bids), Instructions to Bidders, the bid form, other sample forms, and portions of Addenda pertaining to the procurement (bidding)requirements. By definition, the procurement (bidding) requirements address matters pertaining to the procurement process and should not contain Contract Document related provisions (such as thoseaddressed in the Contracting Requirements portion of Division 00 and Division 01). Unless specifically directed by the owner and its legal counsel, or defined in the Owner-Architect Agreement, the procurement (bidding) requirements are not Contract Documents. Coordination with Division 00The Procurement Requirements are not typically a part of the Contract Documents, since they pertain to and govern the Procurement process only, not the construction process. As such, Procurement and Contracting requirements should not be discussed in the Specifications. Do not include references to submission of bids, site visits, bonds and insurance, or pre-bid approvals of substitutions in the Specifications.Contracting Requirements Owner-Contractor AgreementThis document is the agreement (legally binding contract) between the owner and the contractor for construction. AIA’s A-101-2017 (CCDC-2-2020 in Canada) is often used for this agreement, but owners may prefer their own form of agreement.Perkins&Will may assist the owner’s legal counsel in the preparation of this document. At a minimum, we should be aware of any provisions in this agreement that impose an obligation upon Perkins&Will that may not be consistent with the Scope of Services in the Owner-Architect Agreement for the project.Conditions of the ContractThe conditions of the contract detail contractual provisions describing the rights, responsibilities, and relationships of the parties to the Owner-Contractor Agreement.General Conditions of the Contract for ConstructionAn integral part of the Owner-Contractor Agreement setting forth the rights, responsibilities, and relationships of the owner, contractor, and architect. AIA’s A201-2017 (CCDC-2-2020 General Conditions of the Stipulated Price Contract for Canada) is the commonly used standard. The architect is not a party to the Owner-Contractor Agreement. The architect’s duties are defined in the Owner-Architect Agreement and are provided as information in the General Conditions.Supplementary ConditionsProject-specific modifications or additions to the General Conditions of the Contract for Construction. AIA’s A503-2017 (CCDC 20A Guide to Supplementary Conditions or owner Supplementary Conditions for Canada) provides guidance to developing the Supplementary Conditions. This document may be prepared by the architect, the owner, the program manager, or the construction manager but the final supplementary conditions of the contract must be reviewed by the Owner and their legal counsel.Coordination with Division 01The Project Manager, Project Architect,and Consultants should read Division 01 carefully, as it defines Perkins&Will’s work during the Construction Contract Administration phase of the project. Division 01 “General Requirements” specify the administrative and procedural requirements for the following items:1. Allowances, alternates, and unit prices2. Payment and modification procedures3. Substitutions4. Scheduling5. Project management and coordination, including meeting and RFI procedures6. Actionable submittals including shop drawings, product data, and samples7. Mockups and testing requirements8. Temporary facilities, including utility services and facilities to be provided by the contactor on site, as well as who pays for those utilities and facilities9. Product requirements10. Project closeout11. Project record documents, operations and maintenance manuals, warranties, and spare parts (“attic stock”)12. Additional sections required to address sustainability requirements during construction, including construction waste management and disposal, protection, and commissioningSpecificationsThe specifications are written descriptions of products, materials, and systems, defining the quality, performance, installation, and procedural requirements of the work to be incorporated into the project.MasterFormat 2010 defines 50 major divisions (Divisions 00–49), with 35 divisions populated by Construction Specifications Institute (CSI) at this time. Within each division, a specification section is generally identified by three pairs of numbers, with the first pair being the division number. Procurement and Contracting Requirements GroupDivision 00Procurement and Contracting Requirements Specifications GroupGeneral Requirements SubgroupDivision 01 General RequirementsFacility Construction SubgroupDivision 02Existing ConditionsDivision 03 ConcreteDivision 04 MasonryDivision 05 MetalsDivision 06 Wood, Plastics, and CompositesDivision 07 Thermal and Moisture ProtectionDivision 08 OpeningsDivision 09 FinishesDivision 10 SpecialtiesDivision 11 EquipmentDivision 12 FurnishingsDivision 13 Special ConstructionDivision 14 Conveying EquipmentDivision 15 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 16 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 17 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 18 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 19 Reserved for Future ExpansionFacility Services SubgroupDivision 20 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 21 Fire SuppressionDivision 22 PlumbingDivision 23 HVACDivision 24 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 25 Integrated AutomationDivision 26 ElectricalDivision 27 CommunicationsDivision 28 Electronic Safety and SecurityDivision 29 Reserved for Future ExpansionSite and Infrastructure SubgroupDivision 30 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 31 EarthworkDivision 32 Exterior ImprovementsDivision 33 UtilitiesDivision 34 TransportationDivision 35 Waterway and MarineDivision 36 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 37 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 38 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 39 Reserved for Future ExpansionProcess Equipment SubgroupDivision 40 Process IntegrationDivision 41 Material Processing and Handling EquipmentDivision 42 Process Heating, Cooling, and Drying EquipmentDivision 43 Process Gas and Liquid Handling, Purification and Storage EquipmentDivision 44 Pollution Control EquipmentDivision 45 Industry-specific Manufacturing EquipmentDivision 46 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 47 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 48 Electrical Power GenerationDivision 49 Reserved for FutureExpansionMasterFormat is the standard six-digit organizational structure for construction-related specifications in the US and Canada. It is used by AIA MasterSpec, SpecLink, SpecText, the US Department of Defense, Unified Facility Guide Specifications, and most major private and government master guide specification providers in the US and Canada. It is the basis for Perkins&Will’s Master Specifications.Every specification section is typically arranged according to SectionFormat and is divided into three parts:Part 1 GENERALPart 1 describes administrative, procedural, and temporary requirements unique to the section. Part 1 is an extension of subjects covered in Division 01 “General Requirements” to address information specific to the section.Part 2 PRODUCTSPart 2 describes products, materials, equipment, fabrications, mixes, systems, and assemblies that are required for incorporation into the project. Materials and products are included with their quality requirements.Part 3 EXECUTIONPart 3 describes installation or application procedures, including preparatory actions and post- installation cleaning and protection. Site-built assemblies and site-manufactured products or systems are included.Furniture SpecificationsWhen included in Perkins&Will’s Scope of Services, furniture specifications, in conjunction with furniture plans, are intended to describe specified products and their locations for procurement by the owner or contractor. The furniture specifications may be bound into the Project Manual or be a separate, stand-alone document, depending upon the Owner-Contractor Agreement. Information that should appear in thefurniture specifications includes:1. Item Description (Generally select from the following categories: chair, table, workstation, private office, storage, miscellaneous)2. Manufacturer3. Model4. Project Name5. Perkins&Will Project Number6. Code (Generally select from the following codes: C-xx (chair), T-xx (table), WK-xx (workstation), OF-xx (private office), ST-xx (storage), MS-xx (miscellaneous))7. Issue Date and Issue Name8. Contact Information9. Style Number10. Description11. Sustainable Criteria12. Fire-resistance Criteria13. Dimensions14. Finish15. Image of Item16. Image of Finish(es)Determining quantities of each product is the responsibility of the contractor, furniture dealer, or other party contracted to procure furniture for the project. Furniture quantities are not to be included on any furniture specification sheets or summaries prepared by Perkins&Will.Room numbers are not to be included on any furniture specification sheets or summaries prepared by Perkins&Will. Bidders or procurement organizations should be referred to the Contract Drawings for locations of furniture items.When manufacturers’ or dealers’ drawings are included as part of the furniture specification, refer the bidders or procurement organizations to these drawings for dimensions and additional required components.Design and Engineering Consultant Specification RequirementsPerkins&Will’s Project Manager will schedule delivery of design consultants’ specification sections for review and coordination prior to their incorporation into the Project Manual. Consultants must comply with the design team’s schedule and Perkins&Will’s Quality Program. Consultants are required to deliver to Perkins&Will either Microsoft Word files or PDF files, or both, based on project and owner requirements. This requirement should be stipulated in the agreement between Perkins&Will and our consultants.Table of Contents: Within two weeks of the start of each design phase, each consultant shall prepare and deliver to Perkins&Will an electronic file listing those specification sections they intend to prepare for that phase of the project. Perkins&Will’s Specifications Writer will then prepare a consolidated Table of Contents for all disciplines and distribute it to all consultants for coordination purposes.When required by the authorities having jurisdiction (AHJ) to secure project approvals and/or permits, each consultant shall print out its table of contents sheet; sign, seal, and date that sheet only; and return it to Perkins&Will as a PDF file or original hard copy. In the case of jurisdictions that require embossed seals, return only the original; the PDF file will not be required. Otherwise, consultants are not to provide a signed, sealed copy of a table of contents for their sections.If there are any questions or uncertainty regarding the above requirements, the consultant shall seek clarification from the Perkins&Will Project Manager.

title: General Specifications Information
content: Specifications ProcessPerkins&Will’s policy is to use internal specification writers for all projects. A Specifications Writer will be assigned by the Spec Team based on individual Specification Writers’ expertise and availability in coordination with the project’s schedule. Use of an external specifications writer rather than an internal specifications writer must be approved by the Technical Director and Operations Director of the office.Coordinate with your Project Manager, Operations Director, and the Specifications Writer to identify the budget and schedule for the specifications.Design Team ResponsibilitiesThe Perkins&Will design team is responsible for the development of the Project Manual and is accountable for the content. This policy will accomplish the following:1. Having the team members most familiar with the project’s design intent actively participate in the preparation of the written specifications.2. Distributing responsibility for the production of the Project Manual at each phase of the project among the appropriate team members.3. Increasing the coordination of the drawings with the specifications.4. Increasing the familiarity of the project team with project requirements in preparation for the construction contract administration phase.5. Providing the opportunity for team members to improve their knowledge and skills as design professionals.Naming Document IssuancesDocument issuances during the design phase should match the contractual language in the Owner-Architect Agreement (e.g. Design Development, Stage 4 Tender). Percentage complete should not be used in issuance names (e.g. 50% Design Development, 100% Construction Documents) unless specifically required by the contract. The use of percentages can be misleading and arbitrary, as it is impossible to accurately determine percentage completion of a document set, and it gives the false impression that a "100%" set is perfectly complete. Instead, issuances should include terms like "Mid-Phase", "In Progress" or similar.Specification Deliverables by Project PhaseSchematic Design Phase: Based upon information contained in the Owner-Architect Agreement,the project team, including the Specifications Writer, will prepare a Narrative based on information in the Basis of Design or Conceptual Design documents.The SpecificationsWriter will prepare a preliminary Table of Contents (using Master Format) or an outline specification (by editing Master Guide Outline Specifications sections).Design Development Phase: The Specifications Writer will provide a preliminary edit of the Perkins&Will master specifications based on Schematic Design documents.Construction Documents Phase: Based upon information contained in the Design Development specifications and the Basis of Design, the Specifications Writer and the project team will prepare, review, and edit Perkins&Will master specifications.Specification Writers’Roles and Responsibilities in Preparing Specifications1. Becoming familiar with the project’s design intent.2. Asking questions when the design intent is not clear in the drawings.3. Offering advice and guidance to the project team.4. Reviewing and advising the project team regarding materials and products selected by the project team.5. Preparing the Project Manual (including integration of consultant-prepared divisions when required by the Owner–Architect agreement).6. Assisting the project team in establishing performance requirements for project elements.7. Leading the design team in preparing the Narrative during Schematic Design before its release as a deliverable.8. Leading the team in preparing Design Development outline specifications before their release as a deliverable.9. Leading the team in preparing the Contract Document Phase specifications.10. Assembling the Project Manual for each design phase.Design team members’ roles and responsibilities in preparing specifications:1. Establishing requirements for materials, products, components, systems, and assemblies.2. Providing the Specifications Writer with a copy of the project’s master schedule including all revisions.3. Selecting specific Basis of Design or Conceptual Design products, materials, and systems, along with acceptable alternative selections, based on history of performance in similar applications, compatibility with other selected products, contribution to sustainable design, and life cycle value.4. Identifying project regulatory, functional, sustainability, aesthetic, constructability, budgetary, client- specific, and other requirements.5. Evaluating material, product, component, system, and assembly performance attributes based upon requirements, criteria, and tests.6. Selecting acceptable Basis of Design or Conceptual Design alternate materials, products, components, systems, and assemblies.7. Obtaining product information from product representatives.8. Documenting material and product selections by assembling manufacturers’ product data sheets (cut sheets) for standard and custom products and for proprietary products/systems.9. Ensuring the terminology used on drawings matches terminology used in the specifications, and vice versa.10. Delivering information to Specifications Writer according to agreed-upon interim deadlines.11. Reviewing drafts of specifications for content and providing comments to the Specifications Writer.Language and TerminologyThe design team and Specifications Writer are to coordinate terms used in the Contract Documents to ensure that contractually defined terms used throughout the drawings are the same as those used in the Project Manual. The following terms shall be capitalized when used in Contract Documents:1. Owner (do not use “Owner’s Representative”),2. Architect (do not use “Consultant”),3. Contractor (do not use “General Contractor” or “Construction Manager”)4. Contract Documents (do not use “Construction Documents”),5. Drawings (do not use “Contract Drawings”),6. Specifications,7. Site,8. Project,9. Project Manual,10. Work,11. Contract Sum,12. Contract Time, and13. Change OrderFurnish/Install/ProvideThe design team, including the Specifications Writer, is to ensure proper use of the terms “furnish,” “install,” and “provide.” These terms are contractually defined in the supplementary conditions as follows:1. The term “furnish” means to purchase, supply, and deliver to the project site the specified products or items, which may then be consigned to another party.2. The term “install” is used to describe operations at the project site including receiving, unloading, storing, protecting, assembling, erecting, placing, anchoring, applying, finishing, and curing such products or items. Installation shall also include all final mechanical and electrical connections, unless such work is specifically excluded.3. The term “provide” or “providing” as used in the Project Manual means to furnish and install, complete and ready for the intended use.Improper use of these terms could cause confusion by contractor and owner’s vendors, and may result in disputes and potential additional costs.Definition of the Term “Architect”The term “Architect,” as used in the specifications, is the designated entity with whom the owner has contracted to prepare Contract Documents for the project. The Architect delegates parts of the work to others, such as civilengineer, landscape architect, structural engineer, mechanical engineer, electrical engineer, etc., but the Architect is the entity under contract with, and responsible to, the owner.Contractor TerminologyRefrain from using terms such as “Mechanical Contractor,” “Electrical Contractor,” Plumbing Contractor,” “Controls Contractor,” “Roofing Contractor,” “Kitchen Equipment Contractor,” “Security Contractor,” “each contractor,” “this contractor,” etc., unless the project is specifically being awarded to separate prime contractors with those specific designations. Otherwise, these entities are only subcontractors or installers, performing work under a subcontractor agreement with the “Contractor” (always capitalized, and omitting the word “General”).Unless the project has been specifically designated to be awarded under separate multiple prime contracts, there is only one Contractor under contract with the Owner.Since the Contract Documents are addressed to the contractor, designating or mandating that a particular portion or item of work is to be performed by a specific subcontractor or trade is not appropriate, may be in direct conflict with the terms of the general conditions, and is to be avoided. Similarly, do not use the phrase “Contractor shall…” as it is already inherent in the documents.

title: Drawing Organization
content: Drawing Organization-GeneralSheet Numbering Use the following pattern for numbering sheets: A 11 - 01 A Discipline CodeSee Discipline Codes below Group NumberSee Drawing Order and Types below Dash Sheet Number Sheet Number Suffix (Optional) Create a master sheet index at the start of each phase, including all consultants’ sheets. Disciplines not needed for the project can be removed. Keep sets of drawings organized and well planned to avoid rework in subsequent phases.Where sheets are bound into multiple volumes, the sheet index for each volume should include all the sheets of all volumes.Building Level TerminologyAll building levels, including mezzanines, basements, penthouses and roofs, should be called “Level,” not “Floor” or “Story”, unless this is a jurisdictional requirement.Levels are to be designated with a number rather than words (e.g., “Level 01 Floor Plan” not “Level One Floor Plan” “First Floor Plan” or “Ground Floor Plan”).Level 01 is the lowest level that provides fire department access to the building. Levels below the lowest level of fire department access should be called “Lower Level 01,” “Lower Level 02,” etc. with the lower level numberincreasing as the levels go down.Coordinate with the consultants for consistency in terminology. See below Sheet Organization-Architectural and Interiors Sheets for sheet numbering standards for building level plans.Consultants’ SheetsSheets prepared by consultants under contract to Perkins&Will are to be included with our sheets in the drawing set. Sheets by consultants not under contract to Perkins&Will can be bound into our set (and sometimes they need to be for AHJ review); if they are, the Perkins&Willlogo and any other reference to Perkins&Will must be removed from those sheets. Consultant sheets must follow the Perkins&Will sheet numbering system, as defined in below Sheet Numbering. Discipline CodesThe following is a list of the standard discipline codes, in order of appearance in a drawing set: Code Discipline G General C Civil L Landscape A Architectural (and interiors, if prepared by Perkins&Will) I Interiors (if not prepared by Perkins&Will) Q Specialty Equipment (Medical or Lab) (if not prepared by Perkins&Will) S Structural F Fire Protection P Plumbing M Mechanical E Electrical T Telecommunications W Wayfinding, Signage, and Graphics (if not prepared by Perkins&Will) K Kitchen/Food Service V Vertical Transportation X Other Disciplines Sheet Organization-Architectural and Interiors SheetsSheet OrganizationThe following is the standard framework for Perkins&Will sheet numbering.This sheet organization is intended to be applicable to most Perkins&Will projects, unless a different system is required by the Client or AHJ. For very large projects, multi-building projects, and very small projects, modifications may be appropriate. The decision to vary from this sheet organization must be approved by your office’s Technical Director.Please note that the intent is not that every project uses every category of sheet identified below. Sheets used in a project will vary by project phase and scope. See below for typical minimum expectations by phase. Deliverables Checklist by Phase(Example drawing types can also be found in SharePoint: LINK)The 'x' marks in the charts below identify the minimum drawing expectations for a 'typical' project.Acknowledging that all projects are unique and have unique requirements, project teams can include more than the minimum, or combine or split drawing information, to convey the project-specific information necessary at each phase. Please review deviations from this list with your Technical Director prior to proceeding. Series # Series Description SD DD CD Example Drawings N/A Specifications X X N/A Code Review X X X Link to Code Review Templates N/A Design/Project Narrative X Example N/A Cover X X X Example N/A 3D Views and Renderings X X X Example G00-xx Drawing Index X X X G00-xx Code Compliance Data X X G01-xx Code Compliance Plans X X X Example G02-xx Accessibility Diagrams X Example G02-xx Zoning Diagrams X X X Example G03-xx Phasing Diagrams X X A00-xx General Information X Example A01-xx Architectural Site Plan X X A02-xx Architectural Site Details X A03-xx Site Parking Structures X X A04-xx Demolition Plans X X Example A05-xx Demolition Reflected Ceiling Plans X X A06-xx Demolition Elevations and Photographs X X Example A07-xx Demolition Wall Sections X A08-xx Demolition Details X Example A10-xx Overall Floor Plans X X X Example A11-xx Floor Plans X X X Example A11-xx Roof Plan X X X A12-xx Reflected Ceiling Plans X X Example A13-xx Finish Plans X X Example A14-xx Floor Pattern Plans X X A15-xx Furniture Plans X X A16-xx Utility Coordination Plans X X A17-xx Equipment Plans X X A20-xx Exterior Elevations X X X Example Series # Series Description SD DD CD Example Drawings A21-xx Building Sections X X Example A30-xx Enlarged Plans, Exterior Elevations and Wall Sections X X Example A31-xx Wall Sections X X Example A32-xx Typical Exterior Assemblies x X X Example A32-xx Building Enclosure Details X X Example A33-xx Exterior Glazing System Types X X Example A40-xx Enlarged Core Plans X X X A41-xx Stair Plans and Sections X X Example A41-50 Stair Details X A42-xx Elevator Plans and Sections X X Example A42-5x Elevator Details X A43-xx Enlarged Toilet Room Plans and Elevations X X Example A44-xx Miscellaneous Enlarged Plans, Enlarged RCPs X X Example A45-xx Interior Elevations X X Example A50-xx Casework Details X Example A51-xx Ceiling Details X A52-xx Interior Details X A61-xx Partition Type Charts X X Example A61-5x Partition Details X A62-xx Door Schedule X X Example A62-5x Door and Frame Details X A63-xx Interior Borrowed Lite Types X X A63-xx Interior Borrowed Lite Details X A64-xx Finish Schedule X X X Example A65-xx Furniture Schedule X X A66-xx Equipment Schedule X X A67-xx Prefabricated Casework Schedule X Drawing Order and Types (Example drawings can be found in SharePoint: LINK)The drawing order is based on national standards with refinements intended to respond to our extended practice areas. The basic sheet groups are:1. General Reference (All-Project Data Only) includes reference information that applies to the entire project. Indexes, code compliance and zoning data, andphasing.2. Architectural Reference includes abbreviations, symbols and general notes that pertain to the architectural drawings only.3. Architectural Site includes plans and details of architecturally designed items outside of the building.4. Architectural Demolition includes plans, elevation, sections, and details necessary to describe architectural demolition.5. Architectural Plans typically include floor plans, reflected ceiling plans, finish plans and may include Furniture and Equipment plans when done in-house.6. Exterior Elevations and Building Sections include exterior building elevations, and overall building sections. Interior elevations are in Group 8 with enlarged interior plans.7. Wall Sections and Related Details include exterior wall sections, enlarged partial elevations and exterior envelope details.8. Large Scale Views and Related Details include 1/4"=1’-0” (or larger) enlarged plans of cores, stairs, elevators, toilet rooms, and other areas, along with related interior RCP’s and elevations (optional), as well as stair and elevator plans, sections and details and other details specific to core construction.9.
