If the product is not highlighted, the submittal should be returned with a “D” action.Submittals that are not specifically required in the Contract Documents shall be returned without review.Also see the Canadian Handbook of Practice for Architects for additional information on submittals for projects in Canada.Stamping of Consultant-Reviewed SubmittalsWhen the primary review of a submittal is performed by a consultant who is directly contracted to Perkins&Will (or when the consultant is contracted to the owner, but Perkins&Will is contractually obligated to receive submittals and coordinate responses with those of the owner’s consultant for submittal reviews (including submittals required by Specification Divisions 21 (Fire Suppression), 22 (Plumbing), 23 (HVAC), 26 (Electrical), 27 (Communications), 28 (Security), 31 (Earthwork) and 33 (Utilities), as well as some specification sections in Divisions 03 (Concrete), 05 (Metals), 32 (Exterior Improvements) and others), the submittal is to receive the Perkins&Will submittal stamp in addition to the consultant’s submittal stamp.The action marked in the Perkins&Will stamp should be as follows:1. If the consultant submittal action is equivalent to Perkins&Will’s “Not Reviewed,” “For Information Only,” or “Rejected” actions, the action marked on Perkins&Will’s stamp should be the same.2. If the consultant submittal action is equivalent to Perkins&Will’s “Revise and Resubmit” or “Exceptions as Noted” actions, and Perkins&Will has no further comments on the submittal, then the action marked on Perkins&Will’s stamp should bethe same.3. If the consultant submittal action is equivalent to Perkins&Will’s “Exceptions as Noted” or “No Exceptions” actions, and Perkins&Will has added comments significant enough that resubmittal is required, the action marked on Perkins&Will’s stamp should be “Revise and Resubmit.”4. If the consultant submittal action is equivalent to Perkins&Will’s “Exceptions as Noted” or “No Exceptions” actions, and Perkins&Will has added comments that are not significant enough to require a resubmittal, the action marked on Perkins&Will’s stamp should be “Exceptions as Noted.”5. If the consultant submittal action is equivalent to Perkins&Will’s “No Exceptions” action, and Perkins&Will has no additional comments, the action marked on Perkins&Will’s stamp should be “No Exceptions.”When the consultant is not directly contracted to Perkins&Will, and Perkins&Will is not contractually obligated to receive submittals and coordinate its response with that of the owner’s consultant for submittal reviews, the submittal should not be submitted to Perkins&Will. If it is, then the action marked in the Perkins&Will stamp should be “Not Reviewed.”Filing SubmittalsSubmittals are to be filed in the Consultants/07-CCA-Mgmnt/Submittals folder. Each submittal should be filed in a subfolder named using the Contractor-generated submittal name. Only file the submittal that was sent to the contractor with Perkins&Will’s action on it.Physical samples should be filed in a central location in the Studio. When the project is closed out, discard the samples.Also see CCA Automation using PowerAutomate for information on setting up automatic processes that will streamline submittal intake and management.

title: Site Visits and Observations
content: Site VisitsFrom AIA Document B101-2017:"Article 3.6.2.1 The Architect shall visit the site at intervals appropriate to the stage of construction, or as otherwise required in Section 4.2.3, to become generally familiar with the progress and quality of the portion of the Work completed, and to determine, in general, if the Work observed is being performed in a manner indicating that the Work, when fully completed, will be in accordance with the Contract Documents. However, the Architect shall not be required to make exhaustive or continuous on-site inspections to check the quality or quantity of the Work. On the basis of the site visits, the Architect shall keep the Owner reasonably informed about the progress and quality of the portion of the Work completed, and promptly report to the Owner (1) known deviations from the Contract Documents, (2) known deviations from the most recent construction schedule submitted by the Contractor, and (3) defects and deficiencies observed in the Work."Additionally, from AIA Document A201-2017:“Article 4.2.2 The Architect will visit the site at intervals appropriate to the stage of construction, or as otherwise agreed with the Owner, to become generally familiar with the progress and quality of the portion of the Work completed, and to determine in general if the Work is being performed in a manner indicating that the Work, when fully completed, will be in accordance with the Contract Documents. However, the Architect will not be required to make exhaustive or continuous on-site inspections to check the quality or quantity of the Work. The Architect will not have control over, charge of, or responsibility for, the construction means, methods, techniques, sequences or procedures, or for the safety precautions and programs in connection with the Work, since these are solely the Contractor’s rights and responsibilities under the Contract Documents, except as provided in Section 3.3.1.”The number or frequency of site visits should be defined in the Owner-Architect Agreement. It is best if a precise frequency can be agreed upon.The A/E team may confer with the contractor to provide clarifications to the Contract Documents. If the contractor requests an interpretation, it can be discussed, but the formal question and response must be done via the RFI process. However, the main purpose of the site visit is to determine the progress and quality of the work.Field Observation Reports: The “Field Observation Report” is an instrument of service to fulfill the architect’s responsibilities defined in the Owner-Architect Agreement. The A/E team shall observe the work during each site visit and document the progress and quality of the work as indicated in the General Conditions. See Record Keeping and Reporting below for more information.Other than at Substantial and Final Completion, the architect is not required to make inspections of the work. The architect is not responsible for construction means, methods, site safety precautions or procedures.Also see the Canadian Handbook of Practice for Architects for additional information on projects in Canada.Non-Conforming WorkArticle 4.2.2 of the AIA Document A201-2017, General Conditions of the Contract for Construction, requires the architect to determine if the work is being performed in accordance with the Contract Documents.In the performance of this duty the architect has the responsibility to notify the owner and contractor of all nonconforming activities and installations in the work.The instrument of service for reporting all nonconforming work is the Notice of Noncompliance, which is available as a template in Microsoft Word.The Notice of Noncompliance will specifically address the drawing or project manual section that is the subject of noncompliance.On-Site ConductWhen on site, Perkins&Will staff must comply with all contractor safety procedures and must wear personal protective equipment (PPE), at a minimum, a construction helmet, safety glasses, and a safety vest. Staff must also comply with any additional PPE rules and procedures required by the Contractor.While on site, Perkins&Will staff should maintain a professional demeanor, be polite and courteous, show respect, follow the proper lines of communication, and follow the contractor’s protocols for site visitors.Record Keeping and ReportingIn the Field Observation Report, the architect documents the work performed and items observed during the site visit. Reports are to be distributed to the owner, contractor, and consultants, and to the Perkins&Will project team. Reports are to be completed and distributed promptly after the site visit. They are to be objective and factual.The preferred method for creating Field Observation Reports is to use BIM360 on a tablet, or other mobile device, while on the project site. Instructions on setting up the template, creating field observations and exporting a report can be found in the CCA Modernization QuickStart Guide and this Core eXchange Presentation.Field Observation Reports created using BIM360 should be exported as PDFs and then transmitted to the Owner, Contractor, Consultants and the Perkins&Will project team via e-mail, the Contractor's PIMS or whatever method the broader project team has agreed upon. The open Issues can also be exported to an .xlsx (MS Excel) file and transmitted to the Contractor for input into the PIMS. See the CCA Modernization QuickStart Guide for more information and instruction.Typically, we should not invite the Contractor to our BIM360 project for the purpose of transmitting FORs or responding to Issues contained in those reports.If requested by the Owner, it is acceptable for our teams to document field observations in the Contractor’s Project Information Management System (PIMS, e.g. Procore, eBuilder, CMIC), as long as:The PIMS allows the inclusion of all information noted as required for FORs belowThe PIMS allows exporting a report that includes only current. Perkins&Will-generated open items (both new and rolled over from previous observations)We export and retain a copy in our project fileWe have a record of transmitting the report to the Owner and ContractorNote that when working int the Contractor's PIMS, in-progress items we create are viewable by the Contractor, so we need to be careful and judicious in what we write, even in draft form. It is recommended that the following hardware be purchased (as a project cost) for each project entering CCA:iPad Air (LINK)Apple Pencil - Generation 2 (LINK)Case with Shoulder Strap (Example)Project team leaders should coordinate with local IT for this purchase.The reports are to include, at a minimum, the following information (input in the "Message" field in the BIM360 template):1.	Project name and number2. Date of visit3. Weather and approximate temperature4. Others present during the visit5. Approximate construction completion percentageIssues:1. Focus on identifying deficiencies and non-conforming work.2. If an Issue includes a 'dropped pin', BIM360 will automatically include a cropped plan showing the area in question. Note that the user cannot control the size or extent of the plan area.3. BIM360 will automatically assign a sequential ID number to each Issue. This numbering is sufficient and does not need to be manually altered with prefixes or suffixes, as it will create a unique identifier for each Issue that can be tracked through the life of the project. Note that a BIM360-generated ID number cannot be deleted once created. Depending on how Issues are created, BIM360 may create gaps in the Issue numbering sequence. These gaps are acceptable and need not be corrected. See the CCA Modernization QuickStart Guide for information on creating Issues.4. Include the location of each Issue.*5. Issues can be used to document general site progress for purposes of recording the percentage of construction completion, certifying applications for payment and demonstrating the contractor's adherence to the published project schedule.**6. Unresolved (Open) Issues from previous Field Observation Reports should appear on successive reports until the Issue has been resolved. Updates to the Issue, including resolution, should be recorded in the Issue's "Description" field.* When setting a project up in BIM360 for use in Field Observation Reporting, teams should take the time to 'draw' regions in BIM360 and assign them to rooms in the project floor plans. While there is setup time required for this step, it will allow BIM360 to automatically correlate the location of each Issue with a room or space in the floor plan, ultimately saving time and increasing the accuracy of the FORs. See the CCA Modernization QuickStart Guide for information and instructions on this process.** General progress Issues should be closed immediately after generating the FOR so that they do not carry over to the subsequent report. See the CCA Modernization QuickStart Guide for instruction on closing Issues.Photographs:General guidance for taking on-site photographs1. Site photographs should not be taken separate from the FOR process.2. Perkins&Will representatives on construction sites or related out-of-office locations will take photographs of only the following:a. Illustration of narratives in a Field Observation Reportb. General site conditions, but only to document overall progress for the purpose of recording percentage of completion.c. The general state of a specific scope of work, or discipline, for the purpose of recording percentage of completion.d. Conditions that do not comply with the Contract Documents.e. Conditions that require follow-up action by owner/client, constructor, or design team.3. All photographs transferred to and saved in Perkins&Will servers and devices are to be used to illustrate comments in Field Observation Reports or Notices of Noncompliance.4. All photographs transferred to and saved on Perkins&Will servers will be thoroughly reviewed for content prior to completion of the associated field report.5. All photographs transferred to Perkins&Will servers will be named according to Project Delivery Manual Section: Project Document Management.6. Photographs associated with Field Observation Reports created in BIM360 do not need to be downloaded to the project file.Content of PhotographsPhotographs of the construction work in progress can be valuable aids to Perkins&Will’s CCA staff. However, each photograph should be taken only to document one or more of the following:When a representative of Perkins&Will takes a photograph on a construction site or related out-of-office location and saves it on a mobile device (whether company provided or personal) or transfers it to our servers, it can be reasonably assumed that the photographer knows the content and purpose of the image. It is therefore critical that when we deliberately and knowingly photograph site or other construction-related conditions, rather than capture images indiscriminately.Photographs shouldn’t be taken “just in case they might come in handy later.”Review of Image ContentRegardless why a photograph is taken, the photographer must examine the image content. By taking a photograph and saving it on a company-provided device or our server, we make a tacit representation that we are familiar with the content and that we will alert appropriate parties when the recorded conditions require action. If we indiscriminately photograph site conditions and save them on our devices or servers without knowing their content, we risk being accused of negligence if we have inadvertently captured a noncompliant condition without identifying an action on someone’s part.Our having photographed a noncompliant conditioning without realizing it is there leaves us vulnerable to claims of negligence and liability for costs of correction due to a lack of timely action.Photographic File NamingIf photographs are taken outside of BIM360 (discouraged) it is important that they be filed and named according to the protocol described in PDM Chapter 2 section Project Document Management. Project construction can span years, during which we can accumulate hundreds, even thousands, of purposeful photographs. If these photos are filed by date only and with device-default file names (e.g., IMG_1333.jpg, DSCN0026.jpg, etc.), it will be enormously time consuming at best, and nearly impossible at worst, to find an image of a specific condition in the future. Tools are available from our Digital Practice group to batch-change file names to speed the process of renaming, if required. This requirement for naming photographs will also deter excessive site photographing and will help ensure that only deliberate and meaningful images are captured.Use of On-Site Photographs for Marketing and Posting to Social Media Platforms1. When a representative of Perkins&Will takes a photograph on a construction site or related out-of-office location, use of that image for personal social media posting is prohibited.2. The use of on-site photographs for Perkins&Will marketing material (Proposals, Qualifications Statements and similar) and social media (whether for an individual Studio or Firmwide account) is generally discouraged. On-site photographs may be used for these purposes only under the following conditions:a. The image documents a construction milestone (e.g. topping-out ceremony), important client visit (e.g. client's Board site visit) or other significant eventb. The image is first reviewed and approved by Firmwide Marketing and the Global Director of Technical Design.What do we do if the Contractor regularly copies us on their construction progress photos?If we are on a distribution list for Contractor progress photographs, whether we are the sole recipient or one of many, our receipt of these images creates a responsibility to review the content. If you are in this position, please follow one of the following 3 possible courses of action:1. Continue to receive the photographs without defining our role relative to them (discouraged) - If we continue to receive progress photos, and do not define what we will do with them, by default we must review each one critically and carefully and notify the Client and Contractor of anything we have concerns with or that we believe to be nonconforming with the Contract Documents. This is likely to be work we do not get paid for, as it is not a basic service.2. Ask to be removed from the distribution (preferred) - If we aren’t going to look at them critically, we should ask to be removed from the distribution. This may be viewed unfavorably by the Client, so we have to make a careful and considered decision here.3. Continue to receive the photographs, but communicate our role to the Client and Contractor - If we don't want to risk negative perception by asking to be removed from the distribution, we will need to go on record to both the Client and Contractor stating the following (though not necessarily in these words):a. Perkins&Will will not review the photographs for conformance with the contract documents, project schedule or payment applications and will not comment on their content, unless asked to do so on a specific issue or condition.b. Perkins&Will will not retain the email or the images in Outlook or in our project files.What do we do if we have access to a live webcam?It is permissible to view a live webcam for general interest and to keep appraised of progress on site. Do not, however, take screenshots or other stills of the webcam images, or otherwise save webcam information to the project file.What is not to be included in the Field Observation Report:1. Extraneous information2. Sarcastic remarks3. Suppositions, hearsay or conjecture4. Nonfactual judgments or feelings5. Information about individuals or events not related to the project.6. Contractor safety requirements or procedures7. The BIM360 Field Observation Report template includes several unwanted fields in each Issue that cannot be deleted: Due Date, Assigned To, Root Cause. These fields should not be filled in and should be left blank on all Field Observation Reports.

title: Interpretations Modifications and Substitutions
content: Requests For Interpretation (RFIs)RFIs clarify the intent of the Contract Documents and should only be used when the contractor discovers one of the following:1. An unforeseeable condition during the course of construction2. A conflict between the various components of the Contract Documents3. The omission of necessary information within the Contract DocumentsThe design team should not accept RFIs if they are used as substitution requests, submittals, requests for corrective action, clarifications of subcontractor’s scope, or claims. Guidelines for RFI procedures should be defined in the Contract Documents to avoid any misunderstandings.The AIA estimates that as a project team, we spend an average of 17-man hours per RFI. It is therefore in the project team’s best interest to minimize the number of RFI’s to only those that are necessary and avoid the submission of open-ended RFI questions. Reviewing any current or pending RFI’s with the on-site project team during visits can help reduce the number of redundant RFI’s and limit the submission of post-visit RFI’s to those for confirmation only.If an RFI asks for information that is already in the Contract Documents, it can be answered directly on the RFI form and returned to the Contractor.For any other situation, each RFI must be answered individually in the form of one of the following: Architect’s Supplemental Instructions (ASI), Construction Change Directive (CCD) or Proposal Request (PR) in order to formally incorporate the response into the Contract Documents. See below Modifications and Changes to the Contract. Note, 'bundling' multiple RFI responses together under a single ASI, CCD or PR is not acceptable.RFI Management Perkins&Will’s standard specification for project management and coordination (Section 01 31 00) requires the Contractor to utilize a Project Information Management System (PIMS) for the submission, management and receipt of RFIs and their responses. The PIMS should be the primary means of managing and tracking RFIs. Perkins&Will teams should also work with their local Digital Practice team to set up automations to receive, file and manage RFIs internally. See the CCA Modernization QuickStart Guide for more information.Response TimeThe allowable response time is typically defined in specification section 01 31 00 Project Management and Coordination. If the response time is not defined in the specifications, it should be discussed, agreed and documented at the initial meeting with the Owner and Contractor.RFI TriageThe project architect should triage, or preview, the RFI immediately upon its arrival. The purpose of the triage is to determine the following:1. Is the RFI written clearly enough to understand what the question or issue is so you can respond to it?If not, return the RFI to the contractor, requesting clarification.2. Is the question or issue the RFI addressed within Perkins&Will’s contractual scope?If not, return the RFI without response.3. Is it immediately evident that the RFI is requesting information that is addressed in the Contract Documents?If so, include in your response that this was an inappropriate RFI. (These are often called, by architects at least) “frivolous RFIs.” Keep a log of all such RFIs; you’ll be grateful you did when the contractor says that there are too many RFIs on the project.)4. Is the contractor requesting an expedited review, and is it justified by the project schedule?Requests for expedited reviews should be accompanied by the reasons for the request. Expedited review of RFIs must be agreed upon by Perkins&Will before they are issued. If that agreement is not in place, notify the contractor that the RFI will be returned as quickly as possible, but may not be returned before the standards response time limit.5. Does the RFI include a recommendation from the Contractor/Subcontractor?If not, return as an incomplete requesting the Contractor’s recommended solution.Consultant-Reviewed RFI Triage Immediately upon receiving a response from the consultant, check the response to determine the following:1. Is the consultant’s response clear, understandable and complete?If not, call the consultant to find out what was intended.2. Does the consultant’s response answer the contractor’s question?If not, return the RFI to the consultant for a better response.3. Has the consultant made comments that are inconsistent with the Contract Documents?If so, return the RFI to the consultant for a better response.Filing RFIsRFIs are to be filed in the project file: Consultants/07-CCA-Mgmnt/RFIs Only file the version of the RFI that was sent to the contractor with Perkins&Will’s response.Also see CCA Automation using PowerAutomate for information on setting up automatic processes that will streamline RFI intake and management.Modifications and Changes to the ContractArchitect’s Supplemental InstructionsArchitect’s Supplemental Instructions (ASI) are minor changes not affecting contract cost or schedule. If the contractor believes that the ASI will involve changes in cost or time, a change order request may be submitted.The work described in theASI is to proceed immediately. If the Contractor proceeds with the work without notifying the Owner and Architect that they believe it constitutes a change to the cost or schedule, they waive the right to request additional time or fee later. TheASI is available as a template in Microsoft Word in the pwConstructionAdmin folder.Construction Change DirectivesConstruction Change Directives (CCD) direct the contractor to make a change with the understanding that an agreement on cost and/or schedule impact will be made later. Under a CCD, the contractor performs the work whether or not an agreement has been reached before the start of work incorporating the proposed changes.Construction change directive process:1. Architect prepares CCD and reviews it with the owner2. Owner signs CCD3. Architect issues the CCD to the contractor4. Contractor proceeds with the changes to the work.5. Time and cost changes are negotiated and agreed upon by all parties before becoming a Change OrderBecause the contractor is at risk when performing work under a CCD, the owner and architect need to make every effort to fairly evaluate the contractor’s compensation for the work. CCDs should be used only when required by the project schedule, not routinely for every change.The CCD is available as a template in Microsoft Word in the pwConstructionAdmin folder.The signatory on a CCD is signing on behalf of Perkins&Will, and the Architect of Record as a designee and would not generally be considered to be acting as a licensed architect. As such, most jurisdictions would permit any employee to sign CCDs. Be sure to review the laws and rules of the project's jurisdiction to confirm.Proposal RequestsProposal Requests are written documents prepared by the architect describing a proposed change to the project. Based on the Proposal Request, the contractor will evaluate how the proposed change will affect the project cost and schedule and will submit a change proposal for Owner and Architect review. The work described is not an official change to the Contract Documents until it is accepted by the Owner and incorporated into a Change Order. If the Contractor proceeds with the work before that occurs, they are proceeding at their own risk. The Proposal Request is available as a template in Microsoft Word in the pwConstructionAdmin folder.The signatory on a Proposal Request is signing on behalf of Perkins&Will, and the Architect of Record as a designee and would not generally be considered to be acting as a licensed architect. As such, most jurisdictions would permit any employee to sign Proposal Requests. Be sure to review the laws and rules of the project's jurisdiction to confirm.Change OrdersA Change Order provides direction to the contractor to make a change with an agreed upon adjustment to the project cost and/or schedule.Roles in the change order process:1. The contractor starts the process by submitting a change order request (COR) to the owner and architect for review. The COR can be limited to a single issue or combine multiple cost and schedule issues.2. Perkins&Will analyzes the contractor’s proposed changes to contract sum and/or time and documents reasons for recommendation for acceptance, modification or rejection of the COR.3. If we recommend modifications or rejection of the COR, negotiations among the owner, contractor and Perkins&Will will result in a COR that can be approved.4. If the owner accepts the proposed changes, the contractor prepares the change order form, stipulating the approved changes in cost and/or schedule.5. Perkins&Will signs the change order and forwards to the contractor for signature.a. The Change Order must be signed by an architect (or landscape architect, or registered interior designer of record, as appropriate) licensed in the project's jurisdiction.b. The signatory does not need to be the AOR for the project. The signatory also does not need to be an Associate Principal or Principal. Any staff member with appropriate licensure and project involvement may certify/sign.c. Architects licensed in states or jurisdictions other than the one the project is located in may not certify/sign payment applications. Doing so could be considered unlicensed practice by the jurisdiction.d. Be sure to review and understand the local laws and rules regarding practice.e. Perkins&Will's policy is that Change Orders are signed by the project's Senior Project Architect/Designer/Manager or Project Architect/Designer/Manager.6 The contractor reviews and signs all copies of accepted change orders7. The owner signs and executes all copies of the change order; it then becomes part of the contract betweenthe owner and contractorSubstitutionsSubstitution requests are proposals from the contractor to use a manufacturer or product different from that required in the Contract Documents. The project requirements governing the submission of substitutions are defined in specification section 01 25 00 Substitution Procedures. Substitutions must be submitted independently of the submittal or RFI processes, and as a stand-alone process. Perkins&Will’s services to evaluate substitutions and to make revisions to the Contract Documents for accepted substitutions should be considered additional services.The burden of proof to demonstrate that the proposed substitution meets the requirements of the Contract Documents rests with the contractor.However, once a substitution is accepted, Perkins&Will (as the Architect of Record) assumes all responsibility for the suitability, performance and compatibility of the substituted product.There are two basic types of substitution request:Substitutions for Convenience: The contractor may submit as a substitution request a product or system that potentially could benefit the project or the owner. The Project Manual will define under which conditions a Substitution for Convenience may be allowed. Substitutions for Convenience typically may be submitted within specified time periods during bidding and following the signing of the Owner-Contractor Agreement. In some cases, the specifications may stipulate that no Substitutions for Convenience will be considered at any time.In reviewing Substitutions for Convenience, the architect must be assured that the proposed substitution demonstrably gives the owner greater value, in cost, time, or quality, than the specified product or system. This could mean that the proposed product will perform better at equal cost, or perform equally well at a lower cost, or perform better at a lower cost. In evaluating potential cost savings, any collateral costs, such as the architect’s additional service fee for reviewing the substitution and modifying the Contract Documents, need to be considered.Substitutions for Cause: If a specified product is no longer manufactured or available, or cannot reasonably be brought to the site in the time required by the project schedule, or is not appropriate for its intended use or location on the project, the contractor may request a Substitution for Cause. Unlike Substitutions for Convenience, there are usually no time restrictions for when Substitutions for Cause can be submitted.In reviewing Substitutions for Cause, the architect and owner have to be assured of the validity of the cause. As part of the substitution review, the architect needs to determine if the real cause of the substitution is the result of the contractor’s failure to perform in accordance with the Contract Documents. If this is the case, the contractor is to bear all additional costs for providing products or systems that are equal to or better than the specified products or systems.Evaluation of Substitution RequestsItems to consider in evaluating proposed substitutions:1. Reason for unavailability of the specified item2. Benefit to the Owner in terms of cost savings or schedule reduction3. Total cost impact, including owner costs, such as architect’s additional services resulting from the substitution4. Potential maintenance issues/long term impacts to the Owner5. Performance of the proposed product6. Consistency with project criteria7. Impact on project schedule8. Impact on project coordination9. Impact on other building systems, components, and materials10. Acceptance of manufacturer11. Compliance with codes12. Warranties13. Operational characteristics14. Finishes consistent with design intent15. Qualifications of fabricator and installer16. Installation requirements consistent with project conditionsThe substitution request must contain the following information:1. Name, address, and phone number of person making request2. Name and location of project3. Specification section in which the product is specified4. Generic title of product5. Manufacturer, brand, and model number of proposed product6. List of projects where product has been used7. List of references familiar with the product8. Product data and (where appropriate) samples9. Test data establishing performance equality10. Side-by-side comparisons of original and proposed products11. List of ways in which proposed product deviates from the Contract Documents12. Anticipated cost impact, with $0 being explicitly listed if no impact. Do not accept a blank line for cost impact.13. Anticipated schedule impact, with 0 days being explicitly listed if no impact. Do not accept a blank line for schedule impact.Acceptance or Rejection of Substitution RequestsSubstitution requests must be submitted by the Contractor on the Substitution Request Form issued by Perkins&Will as part of the project manual. Substitutions submitted on any other form should be returned to the Contractor without review.All substitution requests should be reviewed with the Studio's Technical Director and the project's Managing Principal. Following the substitution request review, the architect will recommend that the owner either accept or reject the request by completing and signing the "Architect's Review and Action" section of the substitution request form.Legally, the signatory on a Substitution Request is signing on behalf of Perkins&Will, and the Architect of Record as a designee and would not generally be considered to be acting as a licensed architect. As such, most jurisdictions would permit any employee to sign Substitution Requests. Be sure to review the laws and rules of the project's jurisdiction to confirm.Perkins&Will's Policy is that only the project's Senior Project Architect/Designer or Project Architect/Designer may sign the substitution request.When the substitution request form is completed and signed, it is sent to the Owner for their review and action. Only the owner can accept or reject a substitution.The standard AIA Owner-Architect Agreements include substitution review as a “pre-approved” additional service. However, it is advisable to alert the owner before starting the review that it will be an additional service.

title: Project Information Retained for Marketing/Firm Historical Significance
content: At project closeout, project team leadership is responsible for consulting with the Studio MOD (Managing/Operations/Design Directors) and Marketing team to determine if the project has historical or marketing value. If it does, it is the project team’s responsibility to coordinate with the MOD/Marketing to identify the material to keep and to ensure that the information is in the appropriate physical, HUB and server locations. Generally, it is assumed that this information will be kept in perpetuity.Original Physical Design CollateralAssemble significant original sketches and images, scan and save in the project file.Generally, study and in-progress models should be recycled or discarded. If, however, a project has been deemed significant (as noted above) it may be valuable to retain in-progress models. Coordinate the disposition of significant in-progress models and presentation-quality models with your Studio’s MOD and Office Management team.Other physical design collateral (handmade drawings, sketches or renderings) that is deemed significant may be retained but should be housed locally in the Studio and in appropriate conditions to prevent degradation. The quantity of this material will be very limited and must be able to be housed within each Studio.Portfolio (app on HUB)The design principal and project leadership (PM and PA) are to coordinate photography and project process documentation to populate the Portfolio app. This ‘database’ of photographs (in progress and final, professional), diagrams and other project-appropriate graphics is to be utilized for marketing, publications, DEAR submissions, awards submissions, and internal/external project collateral.Project information for marketing purposes are to be reviewed and updated with the appropriate final narratives and graphic collateral.If the project has been built, the final design drawings or record drawings are to be uploaded to Artifact. Coordinate this process with the local Digital Practice team.

title: Internal Project Retention and Closeout
content: For projects that have been built, internal archiving can be initiated at Project Closeout but is more appropriately done after any applicable post-construction correction period has expired.For unbuilt projects, internal archiving should be initiated at the termination of our contract with the Client.Files and material from completed projects are retained for one of 2 broad purposes: Marketing/Firm Historical Significance or Legal/Contractual Obligation.Planning for, and scheduling, eventual document destruction is an integral part of project closeout. Before deleting electronic files or destroying physical material, confirm legal retention requirements for both the project and the Studio jurisdictions (see below).At project closeout, dispose of all redlines, check sets, coordination sets, and document drafts unless we have an active First Incident Report (FIR), a notice of a claim or expected litigation or have a contractual duty to notify the client before disposing of documents. In the former case we need to keep everything until the matter is resolved. In the latter case, we need to keep everything until we receive written notice that it’s acceptable to dispose of the material or send it to the Client, whichever they direct.

title: BIM Process Management
content: Digital Execution PlanAll projects benefit by creating a Digital Execution Plan which reflects the contract, goals, and effort for that project . It covers all workflow, documents, software, and expectations for an entire project, not only Revit.The contract tells you what you need to do, and the Digital execution plan tells you how to do it.Templates for Digital Execution Plan are available when creating a new Word document. They are flexible and can easily adjust to the size and complexity of any project across the firm.When starting a new project, make sure contracts are reviewed and we understand the effort and risk.Create a Digital Execution Plan for your project and update it as needed. See How it WorksPlanning for Design PhasesThe Digital Execution Plan is a living document. It is recommended to review Digital Execution Plan between project design phases. While writing/adjusting the Digital Execution Plan, consider following: Project setup Folder StructureSince March 2020, the firm migrated almost all Revit projects to BIM360 cloud.Example of BIM360 folder structure for simple projects: https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/C3EQ77DB0XbDckf auto false 14px The BIM360 Folder structure should suit the project, but the root folders names are Plans and Project Files. It is not recommended to change them. PW automation processes depend on these folders namesThe working Revit files are located in the Project Files folders. Each company/trade works in their own folders.The extended team should discuss the data exchange workflow and permissions, and document it in Digital Execution Plan (DxP). Permissions to folders are set up by the project BIM manager on desired file sharing workflow._Shared Docs folder is a container for shared documents of any kind, including possibly Revit files to be used as links. The sub folders there should reflect agreed upon method of file sharing/collaboration.Plans is a root folder with additional built-in functionality for document control. For example, it splits any Revit file or PDF posted there into individual sheets. Do not place work-in progress files there.sub folders should not be more than 4 levels deep, keep folder and file names as short as possible. Use only alphanumerical characters, dash, underscore and spaceAt the time of writing , some projects are still residing on P drive. These are mostly old projects in Revit Version 2017 and earlier, inactive, and minimally active projects slated to be completed within 1-3months. Additionally, for the BIM360 migrated projects where Revit models were all migrated to BIM360, other documents might have stayed on the P drive. Below is the description of the file structure pre-BIM360 on the P driveEvery project folder is created from a standard template and consists of 3 top-level folders:DESIGNDOCSSPECSThe DOCS and SPECS folders are outside the scope of this discussion.Design FolderThe Design folder is the home to all project specific graphic files. NO FILE should ever be saved in the root of the Design folder, but instead in the corresponding subfolders below it as indicated in the following subsections. below is an example from the on-going project https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/byYdLcoLaPme3E7 auto false 14px Pre-Design Phase Please coordinate with your local studio’s design director or design community to understand the requirements of each phase of your project for how Perkins&Will accomplishes excellence in design. Also refer to your project’s contract to understand the deliverables required at each phase to direct you as to what to do with technology to form your Digital Execution Plan.Contract reviewSite analysisTopographical studiesClimatic studiesSustainable goalsEstablish sustainable target goals for projectIdentify roles and responsibilitiesEnvelope investigations/ massing studiesBuilding orientationEnvelope performanceVentilationThermal massingPreliminary programmingInvestigate conceptual relationshipsInvestigate building and site flow Schematic Design Phase At the beginning of the schematic design phase it is crucial that the entire team identifies and understands the goals of the project. Responsibilities and milestones must be set in order to facilitate the schedule. Utilize the Digital Execution Plan to document levels of modeling and establish modeling responsibilities.PREPARATIONBIM Kick-off Meeting (All project team members – Owner Team, Design Team, Contractor Team)Develop Digital Execution Plan1. Identify BIM goals, deliverables, milestones and responsibilities for the following items:VisualizationsProgramming StudiesCost EstimatingTrade CoordinationActively Linked SpecificationsEnergy ModelingOccupancy SimulationsCode ComplianceConstruction Sequencing2. Identify and formalize the responsibilities of each entity in the overall design teamSet collaboration protocols, revisit schedule periodicallyArchitect/ Consultant BIM kick-off meeting (Design Team)Utilize the Digital Execution Plan to establish communication and responsibility of members of the extendeddesign team.Note: all projects are to start on BIM360. Discuss the scope of services the team needs to activate.Internal BIM Organization Meeting (Perkins&Will).1. Identify basic internal team structure2. Identify primary team member(s) responsible for model management and digital workflows3. Identify and schedule any internal training needs4. What additional design applications will be used –Enscape, 3-D Max, Rhino?5. Discuss if/when and how separate design application processes will be used and outline major steps.6. Discuss importing/ exporting of model info7. Discuss workset management guidelines (Refer to Links and Worksets section)8. Set-up preliminary cartoon set9. Set milestone dates for exporting the model for energy modeling exercises, rendering packages, animations10. Set-up model maintenance plan. Reference team members from Step 211. Define how the team is to calculate building area13. Discuss use of Copy Monitor – what model elements will/will not be copy monitored14. Develop plan for controlling file size by model, Example include:Separate shell/core from interiorsSeparate tower elements from base elementsSeparate Projects Standard FileSeparate along expansion jointsSeparate by documentation sets deliveryNOTE: During the schematic design phase some projects may require numerous skin variations for pricing studies. If the project also requires accurate visualizations of those options it is advisable to generate separate model files. For smaller variations without the need for visualizations, it may be advisable to provide multiple 2-D views to facilitate pricing studies.Sustainable plan updateEvaluate options for sustainable designIf pursuing LEED – monitor LEED credits, update LEED checklistEstablish export requirements for energy model softwareStart preliminary energy model studiesComputational design goalsEvaluate and document in Digital Execution Plan possible workflows and efficiencies for computational design use. Examples: geometry, data, code compliance, etc.Code complianceStart code review and analysis using templates where applicable: LINK TO TEMPLATES in SharePointNote that Code Compliance drawings are not typically issued at SD, but major code compliance issues (occupant load, travel distance) must be studied and consideredSITEDevelop/ link 3-D site modelEstablish ground floor levelSTRUCTUREStructural gridEstablish basic column gridSet GRID workset controlled by Project Architect (Refer to Links and Worksets section)Use basic dimensions to lock column linesIMPORTANT: Verify graphical dimensions are accurate (flex dimensions)Generate generic slabsSet level heights, locked and controlled by Project ArchitectSet Level designations (Refer to Views section)Locate all major shaftsSHELL1. Locate generic exterior walls Establish major exterior wall types: masonry, curtainwall, etc…2. Preliminary Roof Establish roof heights. Base footprint on exterior walls below, manually adjust perimeter as required3. Major roof features4. Mass out basic roof elementsVERTICAL CIRCULATIONLocate approximate vertical circulation elementsAREA PLANS AND SCHEDULESSet-up area plan views, manually place area boundaries on complex footprintsSet up area schedules, Perkins&Will standard colors, monitor Gross Square Footage as the projectevolvesINTERIOR ARCHITECTURENote: During the schematic design phase some projects may require numerous program, finish, and phasing studies for estimating purposes. Simple small variations may be studied utilizing the design options feature. If large portions or entire buildings are affected it is advisable to use separate models to complete the investigation.1. Interior partitionsStart with PW standard wall types when possible A40, A41, etc. (minimizes re-work later)Initial wall placement to have levels set as constraints2. Room objectsDefine and label roomsDevelop project specific departmental and area parameters for room typesAvoid nesting rooms or separation lines inside groupsDevelop schedules. It is recommended to start using schedules as early as possible; primarily as program and model management tools1. Associate departmental/ program information with PW color schemes2. Develop and maintain departmental colored plans3. Set-up preliminary door schedule4. Set-up preliminary finish schedule5. Place 3-D furniture as required for planning studiesREVIEW1. Owner review and comments2. In-house QC reviews according to studio procedure Design Development Phase In addition to routine model maintenance to be done in All Design Phases, BelowFurther division of model should be discussed by the project team. Options should include separation of shell and core from interior elements, and splitting FFE modelAlso investigate division by levels, along expansion joints or where primary building features meet – for example a tower can be separated from its base.Update cartoon set and delete extraneous viewsSUSTAINABILITY UPDATESustainability goals and strategies should be discussed with stakeholders and documented in Digital Execution Plan. Refer to sustainability chapter of PDM Steps for Design Development phase may include:Engage Commissioning AgentDevelop detailed strategies for MEPUpdate LEED checklist (if required)Upload letter templates (if required)Upload LEED submittal documentation (if required)Update any and all analytic models. Toolset for these tasks is not limited to Revit, many of them may be performed in other softwarePeriodically re-test the energy model as the project evolves.Update renderings, animations as requiredPrimary modeling efforts to be in Revit for the remainder of the project. However as new tools evolve, the modeling can be done in other software if it can be brought to Revit and updated seamlessly, without losing information.Alternate design applications can still be utilized to investigate complex design elementsAssign correct materials to expedite rendering process for external programs.STRUCTUREGRIDSFinalize column grid locationsDimension and constrain gridsPin GridsLink structural filesNote: Grids can be maintained using the copy monitor feature.Important: Update view templates to reflect linked items, especially crucial for section viewsSLABSModel slabs with appropriate assemblies and materialsShow all specific slab edge conditionsLocate all shaft openings, coordinate slab edge conditions at risers, escalatorsLocate expansion jointsModel all curbs, pads, and slab depressions.Coordinate changes with engineers, use copy monitorSHELL/COREExterior wallsModel all walls to show accurate materials and assembliesShow all material changesLocate major reveals and jointsCoordinate wall relationships to adjacent slab edgesExterior soffitsModel all soffits to show accurate assembliesModel revealsWall penetrationsLocate all windows, doors, and louversUse Perkins+Will standard doors and windowsTag all windows, doors and louvers – use P+W standard tagsCurtain wall mullionsModel curtain wall mullion profiles, establish grid spacingWhen possible use panel systems with integrated mullions to speed up revisionsCurtain wall panelsModel all specialized curtain wall panel conditions, shadow boxes, spandrelGenerate special system high panels complete with mullions and spandrel panelsRoof componentsFinalize the heights of all roof levels.Model all roofs complete with accurate assemblies and perimetersAvoid using automatic roof selections when placing roof elements on complex footprints.Locate roof drains, model roof slopes in 3-D.Awnings/ Misc exterior elementsModel with accurate assembliesMinimize use of in place families for repetitive componentsColumns, column coversModel with accurate assembliesDo not use in place families for column coversElevatorsModify external elevator families to match specified, save to local families folder. (Refer to Section 11.7 Family Library)Use openings tool to align shaft wallsEscalatorsModify external escalator families to match specified, save to local family’s folder.Coordinate slab edge conditionsStairsAll stairs should be correctly modeled, complete with basic handrails and adequate landing clearancesUse Multi level stairs for uniform level heights with a max. of 2 runs per floorFor floor to floor conditions with more than 2 runs – use two separate stairs.Ok to draft certain problematic handrails, treads, stringers, etc.After stair modeling is complete, form a group of all components to minimize possibility of errant manipulationsRampsModel all ramps and associated handrailsShaftsCoordinate and lock down shaftsThe appropriate slab edge conditions should be coordinated with adjacent shaft wall assemblies.Loading docks (if needed)Locate railings, compactors, dock levelers, vehicle restraints, rolling security gates etc…Model all 3-D components in loading dock area.INTERIOR ARCHITECTURERoom objectsFinalize tagging of all roomsFill out all departmental and area parameters for room typesMinimize use of room separation linesDo not nest rooms or separation lines inside groupsRoom/ Area schedules (as required)Associate departmental/ program information with P+W color schemesContinue maintaining departmental colored plans (as required)Partition typesAll interior partition types should be correctly located and justifiedAll interior partitions should be P+W standard partition typesAll floor plan work should be done with line weights turned off, this will help with proper justification of partition assembliesSet up partition types sheetInterior wall penetrationsAll windows, doors and interior louvers should be locatedUse P+W standard door types for all doorsAny project specific door families should be saved in the local family’s folder. (Refer to Section 11.7 Family Library)Door scheduleUse door schedule to monitor door information in modelPopulate all parameters of door information as information becomes availablePlumbing fixturesLocate all plumbing fixturesCoordinate placement of fixtures with adjacent assemblies…toilet compartments, urinal screens, countertops…etc.)MillworkAll millwork to be inserted, non-hosted, with embedded keynote informationStart tagging all millwork componentsInsert 2-D section details or sheets from PW Revit libraryGuardrailsModel all decorative guardrail assembliesFinishesContinue locating finishes, embed information in room parametersContinue to develop finish schedule (schedule can be a powerful model management tool)Furniture/ EquipmentAll furniture and equipment should be located, non-hostedDevelop furniture specifications (If part of scope)Locate and coordinate all equipmentCeilingsAll ceilings should be modeled, complete with appropriate heights, assemblies and patterns.Model all light coves and soffits that will be seen in views other than RCPS.Light fixturesLocate all light fixturesDiscuss hosting options for lighting fixtures, planes vs. ceilingsHVAC coordinationCoordinate locations of lighting fixtures and MRPFPNOTE: all items not described here, reference LOD matrix in Digital Execution Plan for your projectREVIEWOwner review and commentsIn-house QC reviews: refer to your office Technical Director and or Technical Committee Construction Docs Phase The primary efforts during the construction documents phase involve detailing specific conditions, and further refining assemblies. All materials, equipment, walls, windows, and doors are tagged and their associated schedules are refined and completed. During this phase a higher level of model coordination should be achieved. It is recommended to review and resolve internal clashes of the model.NOTE: Fast track projects will require separate early release bid packages. To document the process, a detailed archival plan needs to be established and agreed upon by the entire design team , and should be documented in the project Digital Execution PlanLEED™ PLAN UPDATERefer to sustainability chapter of PDMsome things in sustainability checklists may impact the model (make table in future)STRUCTURESlabsAll slab edge conditions are documented and coordinated with associated wall section and details.Locate, dimension all slab depressions, curbs and shaftsSet up slab edge plans, coordinate with structural engineerSHELL/CORENOTE: Many exterior design elements may have been developed in earlier stages of the project due to visualization requirements. It is important during the CD phase to revisit these elements and embed associated information and properly tag these elements.Exterior revealsModel all exterior wall reveals, and sweeps.Dimension revealsExpansion and control jointsModel all building expansion joints, coordinate with structural engineerModel all control and masonry jointsScuppers, gutters, downspoutsModel all scuppers, gutters & downspouts, preferably 3-DRoof detailsDevelop misc. roof details in 2-D, such as roof drains, vent stacks, equipment curbs, roof hatches, walk pads etc.Exterior signageModel exterior signage, show attachment details in 2-D (if part of scope)Head, sill, and jamb detailsDevelop details for all exterior windows and doorsDevelop detail edenlarged elevations of complex exterior enclosure conditionsMiscellaneous section and plan detailsUse model sections to develop section details. Overlay 2-D drafting information on model sections, use underlay method when possibleUse “Floor Plan” callouts to establish plan details, overlay 2-D detail components over the model views. When possible use the line work tool and profile edits to maintain model integrity to the detail level.***All reused or existing details regardless of the source brought into this project need to be reviewed and edited to make them applicable to this project.INTERIOR ARCHITECTUREInterior partitionsFinalize tagging of all interior partitionsVerify all partitions are justified correctlyMonitor rated partition conditions, coordinate with life safety plansInterior wall openingsFinalize locations for all interior doors, windows, louvers, etc.Apply all tags, coordinate numbering with roomsOverlay 2-d information on all previously developed section detailsDocumenting FinishesThere are two acceptable methods of documenting finishes: using room parameters or by modeling. Consider the benefits of either method to your project and the impact on the documentation process.Wall finishesUtilize room tags to show wall finishes, information should be nested in room parameters.Locate all sweeps and bases, 3 acceptable methods, either embedded in wall types, placed as a familywith instance parameters, or 2-D drafting lines (only in non-rendered views)Floor finishesUse room tags to establish floor finishes, information should be nested in room parametersShow patterns in finish plans, seeming diagrams and rendered views as required.Furniture/ Equipment (if part of scope)﻿Tag all furniture and equipmentDesignate Owner Provided Contractor installed FFE via dashed line graphicsFinalize furniture specificationsArchitectural Woodwork (Casework)Finalize tagging of all caseworkFinalize all project specific casework sections, 2-D unless part of renderingFinalize linking and modifications to all casework section details from PW libraryInterior expansion and control jointsDetail interior expansion and control joints in 2-DDoor and window legendGenerate elevation legends for all doors and windows typesCeiling detailsDevelop ceiling details further by overlaying 2D information on all previously developed ceilingExit signs (per project requirements)Coordinate with life safety plans and MEP, non hostedHVAC layoutCoordinate HVAC locations with feature lighting elements. Optional: Use 2-D components, hosted to planes (not ceiling grids) to avoid accidental deletion.Floor drainsPlace all floor drains, sinks, etc.; coordinate with MEPToilet accessoriesPlace all toilet accessories, non-hostedAll accessories to have embedded parameters, keynote informationToilet accessories scheduleDevelop toilet accessory scheduleUse Revit drafting view or legend show typical mounting heights for fixtures and accessoriesStart schedule with initial placement of accessories, process leads to minimal re-workDo NOT Reference CAD detailsDo NOT use non-Revit content linked into model as detailsUse Revit 2D tools and families for detailingREVIEWOwner review and commentsIn-house QC reviews: refer to your Technical Director and/or Technical Committee Construction Admin Phase NOTE: Before any CA activity begins, all documents including Revit files are to be archived as a milestone document set . Other than project closeout, this is the most important milestone archive, DO NOT SKIP.SUPPLEMENTAL INFORMATIONSet-upThe level of “3-D model” work done to facilitate responses to RFI’s should be discussed internally with the team at the point of issuance of construction.The sharing of the model with the contractor team for coordination purposes shall be discussed with the design team during the project set-up and documented/updated in Digital Execution PlanThe design team’s participation on a “Final Model” for use by the owner in facility management shall be discussed during the project set-up. That should be a part of Digital Execution Plan and a contract much earlier.****Update Digital Execution Plan to reflect the above if change is agreed uponARCHITECT’S SUPPLEMENTAL INSTRUCTIONS – ASIsASI’s will be maintained inside the model directory.ASI’s should have multiple parameters that enable them to be associated with separate ASI’sAll revision bubbles and tags will be placed inside the views within view boundaries.When revisions are combined and issued together all changes shall be documented and archived.CloseoutFollow standard closeout procedures for archiving entire project, and deactivate all automation and project servicesReview and submit project specific Revit families for vetting processDocument processes, submit lessons learned All Design Phases Following tasks span all phases, and should be performed at every phaseSustainability.For each design phase, the team is to establish/analyze/verify sustainability goals.Depending on the established goals, each phase may require specific modeling, analysis and filing paperwork for LEED or other appropriate certification.File maintenance.Each Revit user for BIM360 projects needs to periodically clear their machine Revit Cache Link to MK cardRegardless of design phases, Revit file maintenance should be performed regularly (weekly or by-weekly). Each project should assign a team member(s) responsible for maintenance and other coordination tasks – Project Model ManagerDaily tasks:In BIM360, open daily with audit, then sync. This task can be performed by any team member, once a day.Weekly/biweekly tasks:Monitor and maintain minimal Revit file sizes. BIM360 works with files up to 2 to 5 (max) GB in size (including links), but consider team’s connectivity. Make sure the workset use is optimized. Model files that become difficult to work with due to size should be split into multiple files and linked together. This can vastly improve coordination and productivity. The local Design Application Manager or Design Technology Leader will collaborate with the project team on how to split the project file. Refer to Links and Worksets section for more information.Selective Purge. Recommended to batch extract all loadable families for a backup before the purgeAudit and sync with compactMinimize the amount of non-Revit content linked into the fileRegularly delete un-needed viewsReview and resolve warnings.Some warnings are more toxic then others. Some warnings indicate a QC issues, and other tend to multiply quickly.Addressing these errors and warnings timely and properly will improve performance and ensure model and data integrity. for more information, refer to guide to Revit warnings MODEL MANAGEMENTBIM360: verify that files are being published (as a backup)Adjust view templates as new files are linked inArchival:Archive at milestones and before significant design changes per Perkins&Will current standards protocol.Archive and save secondary options into a separate file before accepting design options

title: Revit Guidelines
content:
This section addresses workflows and resources specific to Revit. Revit Standards and Templates Text Styles Included with Revit templatesPerkins&Will maintains both Imperial and Metric templates. Below is US standards for Imperial templates. Metric templates follow the same principles with variations for specific localities. The text styles provided as part of the Imperial Revit templates cover the vast majority of the text requirements during the documentation process. The default PW Standard text style for Imperial templates is *PW Note 3/32?. An asterisks is used to demonstrate the default text style.If/when additional text styles are needed, create a new style by duplicating and modifying one of the existing styles and change the PW prefix to a PRJ prefix so that they sort separately and are readily identifiable. ?Do not use dashes, underscores, or other characters in style names. https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/s7FZuXLp55pCmpX auto false https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/qCEKt2dp0Pzh9h7 auto false Imperial Example 14px Metric Example 14px Text Styles Naming Conventions:PW Note 3/32? TransparentPrefix Type Size AttributePrefix: For all standard text styles this is PW or QA PW. If additional styles are needed, Use prefix PRJType: The text type is one of the following; Note, Presentation, Header, Title, Instructions, Workflows and Schedule.Size: The text size is the text height in inches or in millimeters.Attribute: The attribute is a description of the text styles that differs from other text types.There are two types of text styles used within the PW Revit toolkit; text for the documentation process and text for the setup and maintenance of the Revit model. The text styles with the PW prefix are for the documentation process. The text styles with the QA PW prefix are for the setup and maintenance of the Revit model and is not to be used for the documentation process. Dimension Styles Included with Revit templatesPerkins&Will maintains both Imperial and Metric templates. Below is US standards for Imperial templates. Metric templates follow the same principles with variations for specific localities.The dimension styles provided as part of the PW Revit templates cover the vast majority of the dimensioning requirements during the documentation process. The default PW Standard dimension is *PW Linear “. An asterisks is used to demonstrate the default dimension style.If/when additional dimension styles are needed, create a new style by duplicating one of the existing styles instead of editing existing style. ?Do not use dashes, underscores, or other characters in style names.Refer to the PDM Chapter 5.4.6 – Dimensioning Standards for the Perkins&Will standards in which dimensions are to be used in the documentation process. https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/CEwYuyG6cyneeWn auto false https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/3Fo4oQhTfGDLxIL auto false Imperial Example 14px Metric Example 14px Dimension Styles Naming Conventions:PW Linear Name Dimension Prefix For all standard dimension styles this is PW or QA PW.
Do not use prefix “PW” for project sp Type The dimension type is one of the following; Linear, Arc Length, Diameter, Radial, or Angular Size The dimension size is the text height in inches. The PW standard dimension size is 3/32?. Attribute The attribute is a description of the dimension styles that differs from other dimension types There are two types of dimension styles used within the PW Revit toolkit; dimensions for the documentation process and dimensions for the setup and maintenance of the Revit model. The dimension styles with the PW prefix are for the documentation process. The dimension styles with the QA PW prefix are for the setup and maintenance of the Revit model and is not to be used for the documentation process. Object Styles Object Styles can control graphics and visibility of categories and custom created sub-categories at the project level or view level so you don’t need to control those things at the element level. https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/V7sqjiMrXPWUCW7 auto false 14px https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/VSIXVhvDCgD96RO auto false 14px PW templates come with object styles, and PW content libraries are compatible with the templates. Revit subcategories and other extraneous resources can be brought in when adding content to the file.Subcategory Creation GuidelineWhen creating sub-categories in custom loadable families, always check in the project model to see if your subcategory already exists. If it does, follow the exact naming convention that is already in the project when creating the custom familyIf creating generic model family, always add sub-categories for all solids.More information about PW standard categories:Material in object styleThe material column can be used to assign a material to families whose material definition is set to “<By Category>”. When desired, this is a very quick and effective way to ensure that objects within the same category or, more commonly, subcategory, have a consistent material. Revit Filled Regions Filled Regions:Commonly used filled regions and filled patterns are contained in our templates. There are more patterns available from our content distribution system (as drafting views)The filled regions provided as part of the PW Revit toolkit cover the vast majority of the hatching requirements during the documentation process. Within Revit, Filled Regions are made up by their Fill Pattern. There are two types of Fill Patterns, each with their different types of settings and uses; Drafting and Model.PW has broken down Drafting Fill Patterns into two categories:1. _Materials: Sectional Representation of a Material, ie: Steel2. Graphics: Pattern for non-material specific graphics, ie: CrosshatchPW has broken down Model Fill Patterns into two categories:1. _Materials: Sectional Representation of a Material, ie: Plywood2. Surface: Surface Patterns, ie: Brick CoursingFilled Region & Fill Pattern Naming Conventions for Materials:_Material – Soil – CompactedType Prefix – Material Type – Pattern TypeType Prefix: For all filled regions and fill patterns representing a material will have a _Material prefix. Please note the underscored used.Material Type: The material being represented.Pattern Type: The subcategory of material being represented.Filled Region & Fill Pattern Naming Conventions for Graphics:Graphic – Crosshatch – 1/16”Type Prefix – Pattern – Type Size/AttributeType Prefix: For all filled regions and fill patterns representing a graphic will have a Graphic prefix.Pattern Type: A description of the pattern typeSize/ Attribute: The size represents the line spacing used to create the pattern will. The attribute is a modifier that differentiates graphics of similar types.Filled Region & Fill Pattern Naming Conventions for SurfacesSurface – Masonry – Brick – Modular – Running Bond – FineType Prefix – Material Type – Sub-Type – Size – Pattern Style – AttributeType Prefix: For all filled regions and fill patterns representing a surface pattern will have a Surface prefix.Material Type: The category of material being represented.Sub-Type: The subcategory of material being represented.Size: The size of the material being represented.Pattern Style: The description of how the pattern is repeated.Attribute: Additional description of the surface pattern. In this case, indicating a fine level brick pattern.There are times on every project customization of a filled region and fill pattern is needed. Please change the prefix to a ‘project specific’ prefix so that they sort separately and are readily identifiable. Title Blocks Title blocks are part of the templates, additional title blocks are available in content library.Firm wide template (starting point for office templates) contains some title blocks 30×42. https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/IATRDd9gG46Rouc auto false PW_Titleblock-30×42-Landscape 14px https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/ai0HbfmA7sizRXG auto false PW_Titleblock-CoverSheet-30×42 14px https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/jt4A1FV9PXrehOj auto false PW_Titleblock-PRES-30×42-Landscape 14px https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/OO29ALWbvDVkX1G auto false PW_Titleblock-IndexSheet-30×42-Landscape 14px Each office adds and modifies title blocks to their templates as they see fit. Additional title block sizes and styles are available from HIVERegular title blocks have contain following blocks https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/YetzewPYm9ZrNoi auto false 14px 1. ArchitectName/LogoOffice Address.Phone #/Web SiteSelect your office location from type properties of the title block family, it will populate everything https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/9VqEZgbKVeA2sBJ auto false 14px 2. ConsultantsName & Address OnlyDo not include logo for consultants. Their logos will be on the index front cover sheet.Only include consultants under contract to Perkins&WillUse project information parameters to populate consultant info https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/LL80cyxgFuFQkms 400px false 14px 3. Architects Seal / Disclaimer SpaceNote: Below are General Recommendations. Where local guidelines requirements vary, follow the local requirements.The date on the seal should match the issue date in the issue chart in 7 below.Disclaimer should be included on all sheets without an architects seal, i.e. GMP Pricing, SD, DD, Owner Options InformationDisclaimer should be exact wording of state law. Coordinate with spec writer. https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/AtvpA4qTJLoedoi auto false 14px 4. ProjectName, City, StateInclude project logo if availableDo not include telephone numberUse project information parameters to populate Project info5. ClientName / Logo, City, StateVerify with client / owner how much info they want on sheet titles. https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/IQW7aNTGY5vIthy auto false 14px 6. Key PlanPlan, North ArrowKey Plans should be included in the title block for:Sheets with plan parts, i.e. RCP, Equipment Overall Dimension Control Plans? Key Plans are not necessary for sheets with enlarged plans 7. Issue Chart Mark Issue Date 2 Design Development 20-Aug-21 1 Schematic Design 2-Jun-20 This chart is specific sheet issues only. If the issuance does not apply to the specific sheet, it should not be listed in the “issue chart”.It starts from the bottom and grows up, the most recent issue staying on topInclude pre-stamp issues. i.e. Schematic Design, Design Development, GMP Pricing, in the chart, during design phases we are not required to cloud and delta changes.After the design phase, the first issue at the bottom should be the 1st time the sheet is issued with a signed and dated architects seal on it. i.e.The 1st time a sheet that has been issued with a seal is changed, the “mark” column in the title block is “1”. The second time that same sheet is issued with the changes the mark should be “2”.“Mark” numbers and “description numbers” do not have to match up. For example, the first change on a sheet marked as “1” could be described as “Change Order #6”.During the bid phase, issues are described as an addendum.After the bid phase, changes are still marked with the next delta but the description can only be described as ASI, CCD, or CO.When a new sheet is created and issued after any package is sealed, it should be marked with “1” and described with the package it is issued in. Cloud the sheet number only.Dates shall follow the dd-Mon-yy (17-Sep-20 standard to eliminate confusion in how different countries read dates.Note: Use yyyy-mm-dd for file naming so that they sort properly, use Use dd-Mon-yy (17-Sep-20) for everything else. 8. Management Block https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/wTPTWbmlLWnzAyP 400px false 14px 1. Project Number,2. Drawn By, Checked By, Approved By (not in firm wide, can be in the used in office specific depending on local requirements)3. Optional Owner Project Number4. There is NO DATE in this block5. The job # is the same for all sheetsUse project information parameters to populate Management Block https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/odmVp3jMIOcXVVe auto false 14px 9. Sheet TitleShould follow the titles listed in our standard numbering system in Chapter 5 of PDM.10. Sheet NumberShould follow standard sheet numbering system in Chapter 5 of PDM 11. Copyright 31 https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/RvbsGByudtQ52bR 400px false 14px should be project and not sheet specific.Include the year (not the specific date) when the project’s design documentation began. 12. Optional Package Description and Date https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/mQp7bP4KJEvs7yy auto false 14px When necessary, Project teams may choose this option in lieu of using the issue box, however, the primary suggested tracking is using the issue box to ensure that each issuance is captured properly in Revit.May be used at the same time as the issue box to indicate sheet issue packages. Required at design phases if not keeping track of packages in the issue box. Revit symbols Included with Revit templatesAll symbols are located in the Revit Templates and the Revit Library. Revit ensures all text and symbols plot at a uniform, consistent size since the annotation is created at an actual size without regard to scale.Following is the list of most frequently used symbols. Always cross check symbols used in the project with the symbols legends on the sheets.The project team may create additional tags/symbols for the project when necessary, subject to approval by PA and studio Technical DirectorView Title:This is the drawing title that will appear when a drawing view is placed on the sheet. The type may be selected in the ‘Type Selector’.Schedule View Title:Use this view title for schedules or other views that require a title but are not referenced by a number reference in the drawings. This may be selected in the ‘Type Selector’. Content Library Perkins&Will content guidelines, library structure and content Management software are documented here Revit Phasing PhasesEvery studio template contains default Revit phases – Existing and New Construction.If your studio template does not contain any other phases, ignore the next paragraph and go straight to Phase Filters https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/khcUJ5oNJM7iLYm auto false 14px Many PW studios contain “Super Legend…” phases, which are the first three phase (before Existing)These phases that are used for creating the graphic representations of model objects for the purposes of graphical legends like Door Types, Frame types, Glazing Types, and other. This is done to overcome the limitations of standard legends in Revit. To ensure that none of these modeled objects interfere with the model or scheduling, they are all demolished in the “Super Legend – Demo” phase. Think of those phases as pre-historical, before the Existing phase.After the “Super Legend – Demo” phase begins the standard phases to use for modeling the building. These phases are associated with model objects to determine their timeline within the project. The phases are organized from past to future, a new phase can be inserted in any order, but should typically only be inserted after the template standard “Super Legend – Demo” phase. Phase Filters https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/YNMNxgiYBrDd9B2 auto false 14px Phase filters are used to determine graphic overrides for objects that are associated with different phases. You can choose to set each of the phases to: By category, Overridden, or Not Displayed. The firm wide Revit template has eight standard filters to associate with a view. It should be noted that typically the “out of the box” Phase Filter names (first column) should not be changed.
