If they are, it can causes issues with linked models with different phase filter names to not display correctly in the host model.Phasing StatusEach model view has assigned Phase and Phase filter in properties. Phasing status is relative to the view’s assigned phase. The Existing Status occurs when an object was created in a phase previous to the current phase, and was not demolished up to the current view phase. The Demolished status occurs when an object was demolished in the current view phase. The New status occurs when an object is created in the current view phase. The Temporary status occurs when an object is created and demolished in the same phase. Phasing Graphic Overrides https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/hcMK0jZ9yNAS8Kc auto false 14px In Revit there are four possible of phase statuses: Existing, Demolished, New, and Temporary. In each status you can control line weights, patterns, tone, and materials. These graphic overrides are linked to the phase filters if overridden is selected. Depending on project/office needs, graphic overrides can be adjusted as needed. Model Files COLLABORATION ON AUTODESK BIM360GENERALMost of Perkins&Will Projects are utilising BIM360 Documents. Team should request setting up the project by a DA. After the BIM 360 Project is set up and the team members are invited to the projects, if the Revit files have already existed, they have to be collaborated up to BIM 360. Alternatively, the files can be created by using Perkins&Will Project Creator tool from the templates.IMPORTANT: To open model files ALWAYS open Revit first and then open from the home page only, by navigating through the 360 folder structure.FILE CREATION AND NAMING CONVENTIONS GUIDELINESThe design model file names will be formatted as follows:DISCIPLINE-ClientName-ModelDivision-ModelSubdivision.file extensionwhere ClientName, ModelDivision and ModelSubdivision are optional. If needed, the consultant name can be also used as modifier.(example: ARCH-ACC-CoreShell-Podium.rvt)If there is no need to subdivide a fully inclusive model for a single structure, then the team will utilize the suffix for that model name as “ALL”.DISCIPLINE-ClientName-ModelDivision. file extension(example: MECH-ACC-ALL.rvt)The team will utilize the following 4-character discipline designators as the prefix for all model names: Model Name Prefix Architecture ARCH Civil CIVL Structural STRU Mechanical MECH Electrical ELEC Plumbing PLUM Fire Protection FIRE Construction CNST Estimate COST Coordination CORD Food Service FOOD Energy ENRG Pneumatic PNEU Comm/Tech/IT COMM Med Equip MDEQ MEP Combined MEP File naming needs to be coordinated with the DxP. Document in DxP for project specific namingNaming convention link structure for large and complex projects has to be discussed and documented in Digital Execution Plan.File names should only include alphanumerical characters, dash and underscore.Additionally, discuss file naming, location and permissions for consultant files which should depend on your collaboration plan MANDATORY MAINTENANCE (AUDIT, PURGE)Autodesk recommends opening files from BIM360 with Audit once a day ( sync immediately upon opening). Purge should be used with caution in order not to remove Families and Groups that a user may require for future use.REVIEW OF ERRORS AND WARNINGSThe local Design Application Manager or Design Technology Leader should determine course of action as it pertains to the review of errors and warnings.IMPORTANT: some warnings are more toxic then others. Some warnings indicate a QC issues, and other tend to multiply quickly.Addressing these errors and warnings timely and properly will improve performance and ensure model and data integrity. for more information, refer to http://archilab.net/wpcontent/uploads/2017/01/S03_Handout_LAB_YouvebeenWarned_H_Gorina.pdf Sheets SHEET NAMING GUIDELINESFollow the sheet naming convention from your template. Some sheets will come pre-populated with views, and you most likely will need to create more sheets for multiple floors/segments.Example from Firm wide template https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/Sboym1noy1cSIqn auto false 14px Views VIEW NAME GUIDELINESThe view naming protocol is used to allow the automatic sorting of views.Revit will automatically provide information for the type of view such as plan, section, elevation, and 3D from the view element properties by the system family. However, it is necessary to note the type of view in relation to plan, section, or three dimensional view, interior or exterior in the name. Some of the project details may undergo a conversion process to a drafting view detail for inclusion into the detail library. Because of this conversion, the automatic information provided by the software is not carried over to the Revit Drafting View. The drafting views is an ideal view type for transferring of details views to other Revit projects or for building of a detail library.The view name should be in all capital letters. The Revit view element property for ‘Title on Sheet’ should be filled out only when necessary. (i.e. there are 4 views on the same sheet called JAMB DETAIL or INTERIOR ELEVATION)VIEW ORGANIZATIONOur templates are pre-set to use shared project parameter https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/OQY8CxZbp5F3Lbo 400px false 14px for view organisation. The view series correspond with the sheet series these view should be placed on.Following parameter values are pre-populated in the template and should be used for organization and grouping the views in the browser:Views which are preceded with * are NOT to be placed on sheets. These are the views for duplication and creating working views*Floor Plans -Levels*Reflected Ceiling Plans -LevelsViews to be placed on sheet series Series Plans A04-00s Demolition Plans A05-00s Demolition RCPs A10-00s Overall Plans A11-00s Floor Plans A13-00s Finish Plans A14-00s Floor Patterns Plans A15-00s Furniture Plans A16-00s Utility Plans A17-00s Equipment Plans A20-00s Exterior Elevations A21-00s Building Sections A31-00s Enlarged Exterior Walls A41-00s Stairs A42-00s Elevators A43-00s Toilet Room Elevations A43-00s Toilet Room Plans A45-00s Interior Elevations A50-00s Architectural Woodwork Details A61-00s Partition Types A61-10s Partition Details G01-00s Code Compliance Plans – Smoke Compartments G01-00s Code Compliance Plans – Suites G01-00s Code Compliance Plans – Occ. Load G02-00s Code Details and Diagrams Dependent viewsWhen dependent views are needed, duplicate a parent view as dependent view for each segment (scope box) and rename source view with suffix “Parent”. Parent views should never be placed on the sheets. Use automation tools for consistency.Special Views seriesSuper LegendsTeam Views_QADepending on complexity of the project, the view organisation can be modified to suit. For example, you may need coordinations views, views to be linked into another Revit file for reference (visibility graphics setting of linked model ‘By Linked View’)PLAN VIEWS CREATION GUIDELINESFor plan views, names start with the view type code, followed by LEVEL or SUBLEVEL (for below grade levels) and a 2 digit level indicator.INTERIOR ELEVATION VIEWS CREATION GUIDELINESWhen creating interior elevations, it is important to plan ahead whether or not all elevations of this room will fit on one sheet.Views Best Practice is to place newly created views immediately onto their appropriate sheets and group team views as needed. This habit facilitates sorting, and make it easier when it comes time to delete unneeded views from the project.To maximize model performance, minimize view extents and make use of view range, depth clipping, far clip off set, and scope boxes in views where these tools can be utilized.This will eliminate unnecessary computer processing to display information beyond what can be seen in the view. View Templates View templates are useful to quickly apply specific View Properties graphic settings to multiple views for consistency. An example of this would be creating a view template which turns off the annotations from linked in Revit models then applying the view template simultaneously to all elevation views.View Templates can control view parameters. Including: View Scale, Display Model, Detail Level, Visibility Graphic Overrides of the drawings and should be used when quickly replicating view views for sheet use. There are some view templates that have been provided in the Revit templates as a starting point and will likely need to be tweaked based on project needs.View template can be partial, where not all properties are being applied to the recipient view. https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/eVBDKzh5CYzOypQ auto false https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/QXPe5zswEa9zQ4b auto false https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/kggewKWmtWwTpuU auto false 14px 14px 14px Assign View Template to ViewIt is strongly encouraged to “Assign” view templates to views using the assign view template option in the properties of views. This helps to ensure that each plan series shows consistent graphics and also saves time by allowing the graphics of all views associated to that view template to be adjusted in a single operation, instead of view by view.Apply View Template Properties to ViewThis method is very useful when trying to apply properties of view templates without assigning the template to the view on a permanent basis. It is especially useful with “partial” view templates, where the “Include column is limited to only certain Parameters for specific purposes.It is farily common to have view templates assigned to views that don’t include every parameter. For example, many times the “V/G Overrides Import” will need to be different based on which level CAD backgrounds need to show for renovation projects.Temporarily Apply View Template Properties to ViewWhen possible, it is recommended to create “working” view templates in lieu of many working views. There is an option to temporarily apply view templates to active views for purposes of displaying objects that need to be modified and would typically be hidden, such as room separation lines. Also, you can temporarily remove template from the view, Eliminating additional working views in favor of working view templates can help keep model sizes more manageable. Do not temporarily change view template, use temp settings for troubleshooting. https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/cTiyBU8BhsBzj93 auto false 14px PW Project Browser Perkins&Will firmwide and office templates contains project browser organizations to facilitate easy navigation in even very large projects.Should there be a need tor more levels of organisation, project teams may add browser organizations as needed.PW default browser organizationViews which are placed in the sheet, are filtered out from the view list, they are only visible from the sheet hierarchy. This ensures that team is aware immediately aware which views are on the sheet. When using dependent views, parent view should never be placed on the sheet, only dependent views should be placed on the sheet.Views are sorted by a shared PW parameter VIEW_ViewType_PWProj, and then by view Family and Type. This allows teams to easily organize the views into groups for maintenance and cleanup.Sheets are grouped by a shared PW parameter SHT_Discipline SortOrder_PWProj, and then sorted by sheet numberWhen working with consultants, provide the shared parameter to be pushed in their models for sheet sortingThere are other browser organizations available in the templates, they are useful for cleanup and special tasks.On the large projects teams may discuss adding more granular configurations as needed Links and Worksets LinksThe following guidelines provide for consistent and optimal work process experience for the Revit project team for linking of Revit or DWG files.File and link structure for complex projects should be planned and documented in Digital Execution PlanSince migration to BIM360, when using live links it is especially important to set rules and expectations of consultant Revit files. Unless specifically discussed and documented, the entire extended team should use only “Overlay”. Only in special cases when nesting is desired, the team may consider using “Attachment”. Note that nesting does not work in BIM360 at the time of this writing.If live links are used in BIM360, it is critical to maintain Revit files sizes; if other method is used, it is a good practice to clean up and purge files prior to publishing into shared folder. see model maintenance guidelinesEach Revit link should live in its own workset. all CAD links cab live on one workset, except a survey(often has problems, need to be able to separate for troubleshooting)Please note the following for DWG files:DWG files should be audited and purged before linking.Flatten CAD file is 3D is not neededAlways link to DWG files instead of importing them.Please minimize the number of DWG links.If a situation arises that allows for importing of DWG files within a Revit file, remember to never explode DWG files.Delete hatches in DWG files if possible. Linking to DWG files with hatches will result in significant slow performance of the model.Remove all unnecessary linework, linetypes, layers, and elements.DWG details should be redrawn in Revit. In many instances, linetype issues and printing problems will take longer to resolve than to redraw the information in Revit.CAD file shoud not have any proxy elements when linked to Revit fileDelete all elements (origins, etc.) which are located more than 2 miles from your site.Project team should determine which elements in the Revit project file should become selected for the ‘Copy and Monitor’ process and document it in Digital Execution Plan. Coordination Reviews of those elements are necessary to keep models coordinated.When linked files are used, it is important to discuss and establish coordinate systems and methods of linking and document it in DxP to make sure all files will fall in place for coordination is Revit and when exported/imported. In general Revit can use three coordinate systems: Project Base, Origin, and Shared Coordinates. Additionally, True North and Project North have to be defined. If a coordinate system is not known or not yet established when the project is setup origin to origin should be used as a default and everything should be kept as close to the Revit internal original origin as possible.WorksetsWorksets can serve multiple functions for a team; they are organizational unit which has to be considered together with links when planning the project. document them in DxPHere are some typical worksets from studios’ templates:CoreThe ‘Core’ workset contains elements such as stairs, elevators, and restrooms.EquipmentThe ‘Equipment’ workset contains elements such as large equipment or hardware.FurnitureThis ‘Furniture’ workset contains interior elements created for interior space planning.Interior ArchitectureThe ‘Interior Architecture’ workset contains elements such as interior walls or items other than exterior shell of the building model.Links- (name link) Ex: Link-Structural, Link-Mechanical, Link-SiteThis ‘Links’ workset contains elements that are external Revit or DWG file links.PW Grids and LevelsThe ‘PW Grids and Levels’ workset contains grids, levels, and reference planes.Scope BoxThe ‘Scope Box’ workset contains scope boxes used in the model to control the display of datum elements.ShellThe ‘Shell’ workset contains building elements that define the exterior of the model such as exterior walls.(Unassigned)The ‘(Unassigned)’ workset contains is a temporary assignment of elements that do not yet fall into any of the previous worksets listed. The project team may further establish the use of this workset as needed.*The project team can create additional worksets for the project but should always consult with their local Design Applications Manager to limit the number of worksets in a project.Best PracticesPin all linked files.All Revit links should be placed on their own workset to allow for selective opening of worksets.Every Revit project should start with an internal Revit Kick-off meeting with your Design Applications Manager and Design Technology Leader to set the protocol for the project to consider the use of worksets and links in regards to project size, team size, team member roles, and potential strategies for splitting a model logically for large projects. Keynoting Keynote fileKeynote file is an external lookup file used to consistently annotate the drawings.Each project should use the project specific keynote file. All RVT files linked together in the same project should use the same keynote file.A keynote list must be made for each project and edited/updated in conjunction with the creation and editing of the specifications. The project architect and specification writer share responsibility for coordinating, editing, and maintaining the project keynote list, with the help of the rest of the project team.Perkins&Will has an additional tool – Keynote Manager for editing Keynote files and applying keynotes to the projects. For BIM360 projects, we recommend hosting keynote files on KM cloud to facilitate access from anylocation. Keynote file hosted on KM cloud can be shared with a collaboration company.Revit uses three types of keynote: Element, Material, and User keynotes.Element Keynotes can be applied to an entire family (such as a curtain wall), nested families, or detail components. The Element Keynote value lives as a parameter within each type of element. If the keynote is changed it will change globally for all “like” elements in the model. Element Keynotes shall be used only for identifying individual families or components. These keynotes can display in plan, section, elevation, or detail views. The project architect determines where it is appropriate to use an Element Keynote.Material Keynotes can be assigned to a material that has been applied to a surface, or to materials assigned to the component layer of an element. They should be used only when there is a direct link to a Material Legend or Schedule. Material Keynotes are for identifying the material properties of individual components. They will display in plan, section, elevation, or detail. The project architect determines where keynotes are appropriate.User Keynotes are for repeated instructional notes or phrases that address actions or information, rather than materials or components. The User Keynote value lives only with the instance that it is used. They do not propagate. Unlike Element and Material Keynotes, which are tied directly to the properties of a family or component, User Keynotes are not tied to a specific Revit family or component and therefore will not auto-update if its associated item is modified. Carefully consider their application and provide appropriate coordination.Specifics of the use, organization and formatting of the Keynoting System can be found on the General channel of the Technical Design Community MS Team: 2024 Keynoting Protocol Code Compliance This is general USA guidelines, always check with your local codes. Your local office template can have different resources.All code calcs are done in area plans NOT rooms in order to meet code requirement to have both net and gross calculations in the same planCode compliance process training can be found here:https://digitalpractice.perkinswill.com/portfolio-item/dfw-continuing-education-code-compliance/Resources in PW_TemplatePLUS_ImperialSchedules https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/GVLUozIBUUyw163 auto false 14px schedules named with asterisk indicates key schedule. IBC schedules are updated as needed.CODE COMPLIANCE PLAN SHEETThe example code compliance sheet already contains following resourcesSchedules https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/Qjxk6wtKyIEwd80 auto false 14px this schedule is nested within a group. Note: because of Revit limitations, round-off error can occur, verify as needed.LEGENDFiretapes are a method which does not use additional phases. It requires more work, because walls need to be traced, but some AHJ require the text in addition to the pattern. Only use if your local AHJ requires this. https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/wZQkTuLxVOi4Bh9 auto false 14px Many offices utilize the benefits of Revit automation, by establishing the rating of a wall as an instance parameter and then illustrate through the use of view specific filters. These offices use “Super Legend” view which is simply a view with examples of walls with various values of parameter WALL_RatedOrAcousticConstruction_PW. This view has view filters applied, the walls in it are created in Super Legend – New phase and demolished in Super Legend – Demo phase. Depending on the view template applied, the legend can show different graphics,Monochrome cut fills are applied automatically based on the parameter, but maybe harder to read https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/G0koqky1d6k917A auto false 14px Color cut fills are applied automatically based on the parameter, but may not be acceptable depending on local requirements https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/zqNwuUCLudtVd9H auto false 14px AREA SCHEMESAs needed for calculations https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/x9h0CBwAb5qxeHt auto false 14px RAILINGSUse special railings types to represent paths.Life Safety Designations – Additional Symbolsthese symbols are already in the sheet in most templates https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/cvkzCbvK6mg5ogq auto false 14px

title: Substitution Requests
content: The procedures for bidders to propose substitutions and for architects to evaluate and approve or reject them are defined in Divisions 00 and 01 of the project’s project manual. These procedures identify the information that the bidders need to provide that will allow the architect to evaluate the proposal. It is strongly preferred that Perkins&Will’s standard Substitution Request Form be used by bidders in requesting substitutions.As with RFIs, responses to substitution requests, either acceptance or rejection, must be distributed to all bidders and issued in an addendum to become part of the contract documents.

title: Addenda
content: Addenda are contract documents issued during the bid period (or, in some cases, up until the owner-contractor agreement is executed) that provide new or updated information to the bidders. Accepted substitution requests, RFI responses that impact the procurement documents, and revisions generated by the owner/architect team must be included in addenda to be considered contract documents. On the bid form, bidders must acknowledge receipt of all addenda in order to have a responsive bid.

title: Contractor Payments
content: The contractor depends on prompt and fair progress payments for capital to meet project payment obligations. Payment is the owner’s primary obligation under the contract with the contractor.Perkins&Will Contractual ResponsibilitiesThe architect certifies the amount of payment due to the contractor. It is important that the amount certified accurately reflects the actual amount of work completed; should the contractor default during construction, there needs to be sufficient funds available to complete the project. Only an architect licensed in the project’s jurisdiction is legally able to certify payment applications.Certification does not require that the architect:1. Make exhaustive on-site inspections2. Review construction means, methods, sequences, or procedures3. Review requisitions of subcontractors or suppliers4. Determine the disposition of previously certified paymentsThe Owner-Contractor Agreement addresses contract sum, contactor’s warranty of title to work, materials and equipment, interest on overdue payments, completion dates for project, liquidated damages, and retainage.The General Conditions address general principles of payments, rights and responsibilities of parties regarding obligations to make payments, conditions for withholding payments, and remedies for nonpayment.The Supplementary Conditions typically include the stipulation of the form of payment application, application for payment intervals, period of time between approved application for payment and the actual payment, procedure for reduction of retainage, period of time to make final payment, and requirements for payment for stored materials.Perkins&Will Review of Schedule of ValuesAs part of construction startup, the contractor is required to submit the schedule of values to Perkins&Will for review. We review the schedule of values to verify the following information:1. The level of detail is sufficient to establish the proportionate cost of each component of the work2. There is no inappropriate “front-loading” of project costs3. Separate labor and materials breakdown for line items as identified in Division 01 specifications are shown4. Line items are defined so that percent complete can be readily estimatedA well-prepared schedule of values will allow for easier and more accurate evaluations of payment applications.Perkins&Will Review of Payment ApplicationsThe architect should review the application for payment to determine the following:1. Payment for nonconforming work is not requested in the application2. For lump sum contracts, the percent complete for each line item appears reasonable and is in proportion with the work remaining to complete3. For Cost-Plus-Fee contracts, the invoices, payroll, and equipment rental or amortization documentation is in order and represents the actual progress of the work4. The arithmetic adds up5. Only approved change orders are included6. For stored materials, there is sufficient documentation that the materials have been purchased, insured, and stored in a bonded warehouse. The materials are available for observation by the architect (although the architect is not required under most Owner-Architect agreements to physically verify them).Inadvertent and typographical errors may be corrected by hand and initialed and dated.Certification/Signing of Payment ApplicationsCertification of payment applications must be signed by an architect (or landscape architect, or registered interior designer of record, as appropriate) licensed in the project's jurisdiction.The signatory does not need to be the AOR for the project. The signatory also does not need to be an Associate Principal or Principal. Any staff member with appropriate licensure and project involvement may certify/sign.Architects licensed in states or jurisdictions other than the one the project is located in may not certify/sign payment applications. Doing so could be considered unlicensed practice by the jurisdiction.Be sure to review and understand the local laws and rules regarding practice.

title: Construction Contract Closeout
content: Contract CloseoutContract Closeout comprises the activities to be performed by contractors, subcontractors, suppliers, manufacturers, architects, consultants, and owners related to completing the work as defined in the Owner- Contractor Agreement. Performing these activities is necessary for occupancy of the work by the owner, Final Completion, final payment to the contractor, and normal termination of the contract. On phased-occupancy projects, contract closeout occurs at different times as portions of the project are completed and occupied at different times.The requirements for Contract Closeout are primarily defined in Division 01 of the Project Manual. The most relevant sections are 01 77 00 Closeout Procedures, 01 78 23 Operation and Maintenance Data, 01 78 39 ProjectRecord Documents, 01 79 00 Demonstration and Training, 01 81 13 Sustainable Design Requirements, and 01 91 13 General Commissioning Requirements. Individual specification sections may have additional closeout requirements that apply to only the products specified in that section. The General Conditions and SupplementaryConditions contain information about fulfilling contract requirements.Closeout MeetingsWell before substantial completion, regular meetings should be held to specifically address closeout issues. Participants should include the owner, contractor, architect, consultants, and facility manager.Substantial CompletionSubstantial Completion is defined as the point where the project is sufficiently completed so that the owner can occupy and use the building/space/phase for its intended purpose. The time of Substantial Completion is established when the architect issues the Certificate of Substantial Completion (AIA G704) following an inspection of the work.The process for Substantial Completion begins when the contractor submits the initial list documenting items needing to be completed or corrected, to the architect (commonly, though not officially, known as a ‘punch list’). The architect then inspects the project to determine if the work is substantially complete and supplements the contractor’s ‘punch list’ with additional items that need to be completed or corrected. During the inspection, the architect should have available a list of deficiencies noted on observation reports and a copy of the Contract Documents.If it is determined that Substantial Completion has not been achieved, the inspection should be terminated. The architect may be entitled to additional fee for additional inspections.Following the inspection, the architect issues a Certificate of Substantial Completion. The certificate must identify the date of Substantial Completion. The ‘punch list’ for incomplete or incorrect items should be attached to the form, and a time period in which the punch list items need to be completed or corrected should be identified. The certificate is to be signed by the architect and then distributed to the owner, contractor for their signatures.The Certificate of Substantial Completion must be signed by an architect (or landscape architect, or registered interior designer of record, as appropriate) licensed in the project's jurisdiction.The signatory does not need to be the AOR for the project. The signatory also does not need to be an Associate Principal or Principal. Any staff member with appropriate licensure and project involvement may certify/sign.Architects licensed in states or jurisdictions other than the one the project is located in may not certify/sign Certificates of Substantial Completion. Doing so could be considered unlicensed practice by the jurisdiction.Be sure to review and understand the local laws and rules regarding practice.The date of Substantial Completion has long been accepted as the base date for all time-related considerations of the contract, including the owner’s occupancy, payment for fuels and services, and responsibility for operating building systems, insurance changeover, starting date for warranties and the start of the owner’s responsibility for security and maintenance.Certificate of OccupancyThe contractor must submit a Certificate of Occupancy or other documents issued by the appropriate building official confirming that the required inspections have been completed and that the project meets the requirements of the authorities. Note that the Certificate of Substantial Completion (a contractual process) and the Certificate of Occupancy (a regulatory process) are separate processes and are not co-dependent. Perkins&Will is not typically involved in the Certificate of Occupancy process.Final Inspection and Final PaymentFor Final Completion, as for Substantial Completion, the contractor requests the architect to make an inspection to determine if the work is “acceptable under the Contract Documents and the contract fully performed.” If it is, the architect issues a final Certificate for Payment. Again, many items must be submitted to establish that obligations have been satisfied before the contractor can request the inspection for Final Completion, including evidence that the Substantial Completion inspection list (‘punch list’) of items has been completed or corrected.Final payment by the owner represents final acceptance of the work. The owner waives all claims except liens, claims, security interests or encumbrances arising out of the contract and unsettled; failure of the work to comply with the Contract Documents; and terms of special warranties required by the Contract Documents. In accepting the final payment, the contractor waives all claims except those previously made in writing and identified as unsettled.Closeout SubmittalsThe specifications define the required closeout submittals due from the Contractor. These often include:1. O&M data for systems/equipment2. Contractor-produced record documents3. Spare parts and extra stock materials4. Special warranties and bonds5. Certificates and affidavits6. KeyingUnless a project's Contract Documents state otherwise, closeout submittals should be treated like all other submittals, reviewed for conformance with the Contract Documents and stamped with one of the following actions:A - NO EXCEPTIONS: Work included in the submittal may proceed. The work generally is in compliance with the Contract Documents.B - EXCEPTIONS AS NOTED: Work included in the submittal may proceed provided the modifications noted are properly incorporated. Resubmission is not required.C – REVISE AND RESUBMIT: The submission is partially, but not completely, in conformance with the contract documents and must be resubmitted to the A/E team for review.D – REJECTED: The submittal is not complete or does not conform to the Contract Documents. A new submittal is required.E – NOT REVIEWED: The submittal is not required by the Contract Documents.Perkins&Will is also typically responsible for verifying that all required closeout documents have been submitted by the Contractor. Once all required closeout submittals have been submitted and have received A or B action, Perkins&Will should inform the Contractor that they can be transmitted to the Owner.Note that submission of closeout submittals is a requirement of the Contract Documents. If they have not been submitted, reviewed and transmitted to the Owner at the time of Substantial Completion (see above) that should be included on the 'punch list' of incomplete items that accompanies the Certificate of Substantial Completion and their value should be included in the cost estimate of work to be completed or corrected.Perkins&Will-produced Record DocumentsThe architect’s responsibility with regards to record documents can vary from project to project; make sure you know what your Owner-Architect Agreement says. If you are using the AIA B101-2017 Standard Form of Agreement Between Owner and Architect, the architect’s role in producing record documents is an additional service. However, when we are working under an owner-written contract, or a modified B101, it is not unusual for record document preparation to be included under basic services. In that case, our responsibility, as well as our consultants’ responsibilities, are usually limited to incorporating the Contractor-produced record drawing information into the Contract Documents. Because we are basing our record documents on information provided by the Contractor, we should not certify or otherwise state that our record documents represent the as-built conditions.If we are required to prepare record documents, we should use the most recent version of the Contract Documents as their basis. All previous revision clouds and deltas should be removed, and all new changes based on Contractor-provided information should be identified by clouds and deltas. The deltas should be tied to the revision block of each sheet, and identified as “Record Documents.” If editable electronic copies (e.g. .dwg or .rvt files) of these documents are given to the owner, the Perkins&Will name and logo, and the names of our consultants, should be removed from the title blocks of each sheet.It is recommended that each sheet of the record drawing set include the following statement:This record drawing has been prepared, in part, based upon information furnished by others. While this information is believed to be reliable, Perkins&Will cannot assure its accuracy, and thus is not responsible for the accuracy of this record drawing. Those relying on this record document are advised to obtain independent verification of its accuracy before applying it for any purpose.Record documents should not be called “as-built documents.” Neither the AIA nor CSI recognize the term and it implies a level of site verification that we are not responsible for.Record documents should not be sealed by Perkins&Will or our consultants unless it is specifically required by either the Owner-Architect Agreement or the Authority Having Jurisdiction. If a seal is required, the recommended statement above is required.Warranties and Correction PeriodTypically, within one year of the date of Substantial Completion, the contractor is required to correct any work not in conformance with the Contract Documents upon written notice of the owner. The Owner-Architect Agreement may require the architect to perform a site walk to identify any nonconforming work prior to the termination of the warranty period.Post-Occupancy Services:Post-occupancy services are typically additional services not specifically addressed in the Owner-Architect agreement. These services can include assisting the owner in the initial occupancy and ongoing operation of the project, or a post-occupancy evaluation.

title: Project Information Retained for Legal/Contractual Obligation
content: Criteria for Required RetentionRequired by regulation –Statutes of Repose and Limitations in each project’s location, and in the location of the Studio delivering the work, determine the required retention period for all project-related documents. Statutes of Repose bar actions against architects after a specified period of time following the completion of services or substantial completion. Statutes of Limitation bar actions against architects after a specified period of time following an injury or discovery of a deficiency.Retention requirements differ across jurisdictions. To simplify, the retention periods that follow are based on the longest requirement in each country or region.Retention periods by country are:Brazil – 10 years from the date of issuance of the Habite-se (Certificate of Occupancy)Canada – 15 years from the date of Substantial PerformanceChina – 3 years from the date of final acceptance of the workMexico – 10 years from the date of Substantial CompletionUnited Kingdom – 15 years from the date of Practical Completion (20 years in Scotland, 30 years for residential projects completed prior to 2023)United States - 14 years from the date of Substantial Completion**European countries are not included in the list because each of the many countries in the region is governed by their own laws.Consult with the Legal Department about retention requirements for countries in Europe or others that are not on the list above.Required by contract – Sometimes we enter into contracts with governmental or large institutional clients who require that we retain some or all business records related to a particular project for a specified period. Typically, this will be restricted to financial records, but not always. If these requirements exist, they will be enumerated in the Owner-Architect Agreement. These requirements almost always expire prior to the retention requirements noted above, so it can be assumed that they are not in force if documents pass the relevant regulatory retention period identified above.

title: Storage of Physical Material
content: In general, long-term storage of physical material is discouraged. Sending physical material to an off-site facility is rarely warranted or beneficial to Perkins&Will and creates ongoing costs for storage, retrieval and destruction. Nearly all material related to our business should be stored digitally, with the only exceptions being those noted above as significant for marketing or firm history, and these should be housed locally in each Studio.Written and Graphic DocumentsScan hard copies of certified or signed documents and graphic material that is important to the project and save in the project file and on the Portfolio app as appropriate. Originals should be discarded.Submittal Samples﻿Discard sample submittals. Disposal methods, in order of preference, are:1. Return to manufacturers or vendors2. Return to the General Contractor3. Recycle4. Discard to landfill. Note that disposal to landfill is a last resort.

title: Archival Storage of Digital Files
content: Except as noted above in section Project Information Retained for Marketing/Firm Historical Significance and below, digital project files should be maintained in the state they exist at project closeout for the legally required period and then deleted when that period ends. Coordinate long-term digital file storage and deletion with IT.Project DirectoryFor projects that have been built, download the final project directory from the Contractor PIMS and verify that the names and addresses of all parties associated with the project are included: Owners, Consultants, Contractors, and Subcontractors. Make necessary revisions to create an updated, final project directory. Transcribe the appropriate information to the Portfolio app and save the file to Team/01-Project Mgmnt.E-MailCoordinate retention of email with the Project Manager and Project Architect. Each team member’s project-related emails should be saved in a project-specific Outlook folder. Note that these files will be accessible to each individual indefinitely and can be accessed by project leadership if an employee leaves Perkins&Will. Saving project email to individual Outlook folders is suitable for long-term storage. Email should not be copied to the project folder.

title: Guidance for Physical Material Already in Off-Site Storage
content: Each Studio should endeavor to maintain as little off-site storage as possible and to reduce current holdings as much and as quickly as possible. The goal should be to dissolve physical holdings and terminate all contracts with off-site storage facilities as soon as practical.As soon as possible, each Studio’s MOD should review their off-site storage inventory and take the following actions.Items that cannot be identified or that do not have a project completion date: Schedule for destruction as soon as possible.Items that have passed the retention period noted above: Schedule for destruction as soon as possible.Items that have not passed the retention period noted above:Review the stored material inventory once a year, preferably near the beginning of Q1.Identify material that reached its retention limit in the previous year.Schedule destruction with the storage facility for the earliest possible date.If the project is in a jurisdiction that the project team believes has a shorter retention period than noted above, consult with the Legal Department to confirm. If the Legal Department concurs, the material can be scheduled for destruction.Note: The cost for destruction of stored material can be considerable. Studios with a large volume of stored material that can be destroyed may have to coordinate a phased approach to spread the cost over a number of months.Starting in 2025 no additional material should be sent to off-site storage.Special instructions for projects with an active claim or First Incident Report (FIR).All material related to projects with active claims or FIRs must be retained until the claim has been resolved or the reported incident has been concluded without a claim.Special instructions for material being retained for historic reasons or Firm significance.Retrieve material from the storage facility.The MOD and Marketing will assess the value of the material.If it is deemed important to retain the physical material, house locally in the Studio. If the material cannot be stored in the Studio, suitable archival storage must be arranged.If the content is deemed important, but the physical artifact is not, scan, file in the Portfolio app and destroy the original.If it is deemed insufficiently important, destroy.

title: Area Sync - Revit01 - Installation
content: Installation AreaSync Revit pushes model information to a cloud database where it can be easily accessed by reports from PowerBi to track important design data. Information on this page will guide you through the process. https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/OsBMeZzEi3iUR4Z auto false 14px After successful installation Area Sync 2 should appear on PW tab. Every time there is a new release AreaSync will send a notification for an update. https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/KMlWnIHcJe7akyU auto false Caption 14px First time login Visit https://areasync.perkinswill.com/ and log in with your Microsoft Account

title: Sealing Documents
content: ***The first and most important step in understanding the requirements for signing and sealing professional documents is to check the rules of professional practice in the project's jurisdiction.***Note that in legal language, the term "seal" is generally understood to include a stamp and dated signature. Stamping and applying one's signature with a date, therefore, creates a "seal." Many of us say "sign and seal" but it is more accurate to simply say "seal." This section, then, uses the term "seal" to identify both the stamp and dated signature that is required by a project's jurisdiction. Most jurisdictions require that the Architect of Record (AOR) seal drawings and project manuals (specifications) that are issued for official approval and/or permit and that the AOR exercise responsible control over the development of the work. As such, the AOR should be a regular and integrated member of the project team, but in any event the AOR must comply with the requirements of the project's jurisdiction. The AOR does NOT have to be a Principal or Associate Principal. A project's AOR should be an experienced team member who is licensed in the project's jurisdiction and meets the requirements for AOR. The project Managing Principal, in conjunction with the Project Manager and Project Architect, will determine who meets the above criteria and will serve as the AOR. The AOR should be engaged early in the project and have meaningful input and participation throughout its development. We are a professional services organization and expect our employees to be professionally responsible for their services when they act as the AOR (or landscape architect, or registered interior designer of record, as appropriate) and our insurance policies provide coverage for the performance of professional services by all our registered design professionals. It is shared responsibility of the designated AOR, project leadership and the lead Perkins&Will Studio to ensure the foregoing.Therefore, only the designated AOR (or landscape architect, or registered interior designer of record, as appropriate) may seal:· Drawings· Project ManualsSome jurisdictions require firm licensure in addition to individual architectural licensure. Confirm such requirements and use the proper entity name (and firm stamp where required) for Perkins&Will on sealed documents. Firm licensed entity names can be found in the Legal Toolkit on Hub.Documents should only be sealed when required by the jurisdiction (See note above about local laws and rules!) In most cases, this is limited to issuance for permit review, but will vary by jurisdiction.

title: Contracts/Agreements and Proposals UK
content: The terms “contract” and “agreement,” as they are used in the following sections, are defined to mean a document, signed by a representative of Perkins&Will and another party (client or consultant), that establishes a legal and financial framework for Perkins&Will to provide services to a client or for a consultant to provide services to Perkins&Will. Policy StatementIt is Perkins&Will policy—and the law in many states—not to perform services without a signed (“executed“) contract with the client. The firm’s template agreements, based on standard AIA agreements, can be found as templates in Microsoft Word in the pwAIA template folder. A Letter of Intent or signed proposal may be acceptable as an interim measure, or even an acceptable form of agreement, under certain conditions (see the Letters of Intent section below). Consult with the Legal Department to confirm acceptability.Perkins&Will’s Legal Department will review contracts or other forms of final agreement for insurability issues. Additionally, contracts must be forwarded to our Legal Department for assistance with development and/or review. Contracts should be routed for review by using the Legal Review Request Form.Executing Contracts and AgreementsAll Contracts and Agreements that bind Perkins&Will (Proposals, Interim Letters of Agreement, Agreements/Contracts with Owner (AIA B101/103), Agreements/Contracts with Consultants (AIA C401/C421/C422), EFTAs) must be executed (signed) by a Principal or Associate Principal.**CEL may co-sign if not a Principal or Associate Principal.Letters Of IntentIf a contract has not yet been signed by Perkins&Will and the client, it is the responsibility of the managing principal and the project manager to obtain a signed Letter of Intent (LOI) from the client before the commencement of services. The LOI must incorporate or reference the written proposal, or include an agreed-upon definition of fees, schedule, and scope of services, as well as one or more of the following:1. Authorization to proceed2. Phases of service3. Anticipated deliverables4. Direction to seek (or not to seek) LEED® Certification5. Definition and rates of reimbursable expenses6. Terms of compensation7. Payment terms8. Hourly rate scheduleIf the full scope of the project or services is not known, the letter should address the specific preliminary services to be provided to determine a preliminary scope that will allow work on the project to commence (e.g., feasibility studies or programming). Additionally, the letter must be signed by a Perkins&Will principal or associate principal and an authorized representative of the client.Work may proceed under the terms outlined in the letter of intent provided that negotiations on the final agreement continue in good faith at a reasonable pace and the client remains current with the agreed-upon payments.Should any client be unwilling to provide the agreements and authorization described above, work should not proceed without the express approval of the office’s managing director.Forms of AgreementContracts should define the scope of services, schedule, fees, and other conditions. Once drafted, the contract is to be reviewed and approved by the project’s managing principal or the Studio’s Director of Operations and the Legal Department. Like proposals, contracts should also be reviewed by the Design and Technical Directors to ensure that the project fees and schedule allow for a successful project design and delivery.All supporting documents (such as the initial proposal) should be formally referenced as exhibits and attached to the contract to avoid misunderstandings of scope or deliverables.If there are original hard copies of executed contracts, they are to be filed with the Studio's accounting department. Electronic copies of the contracts should be filed in the Contracts area of PMTK in the Project App, and be accessible to any Perkins&Will team member.It is Perkins&Will’s practice to use our Legal Department to negotiate contracts. It is mandatory that the project’s managing principal, the Studio’s Managing Director or Director of Operations review all contracts with the Legal Department. The Legal Department is available to assist with the review, interpretation, and negotiations of all contracts. An attorney outside the firm should not be engaged unless approved by the Legal Department.It is important to execute all consultant contracts as early as possible, preferably before any services begin. In the case where the client has authorized services to begin in writing and we need to get the consultant started before an owner-architect contract has been executed, the Consultant Interim Letter of Agreement should be used. As soon as the prime agreement has been executed, it is important to execute the Perkins&Will standard subconsultant agreement (PW Template AIA C401) right away. You can attach an unsigned copy of the consultant’s proposal as the scope exhibit as indicated in the template. It is Perkins&Will’s policy to never sign a consultant’s proposal, no matter how small the scope. Signing a consultant’s proposal forms a binding contract and it will be on the consultant’s terms which will heavily favor the consultant and give them no reason to sign our subconsultant agreement. If you have questions about your consultant’s special circumstances, ask one of the Perkins&Will attorneys for guidance. The Owner-Architect Agreement (the “Prime Agreement”) is to be attached as an exhibit to the subconsultant agreement so that the consultant is legally bound by the same terms and conditions as Perkins&Will. Before attaching the Prime Agreement as an exhibit, confidential information (such as fees and hourly rates) should be redacted.”For information on how to handle amendments to our agreements, see Requests for Additional Services section.All proposals must be reviewed by a principal or associate principal before submission to the client or consultant. Principals or associate principals must execute contracts or letters of intent for projects with an NSR of $1,000,000 or less. Only principals can sign proposals or contracts exceeding a NSR (net service revenue) of $1,000,000.Contracts with NSR in excess of $3,000,000 or that incorporate a non-traditional scope, service, or business relationship require additional approval by the Risk Committee. Certain contracts will require approval by a vote of the Board of Directors, at their discretion.Allow 5 to 10 business days for contract review and approval by in-house Legal Department. Allow at least 10 to 15 business days for contracts requiring approval by the Risk Management Committee. You can track progress and scheduling of contract reviews using the Legal Department Contract Calendar.Contract Review1. Use the Perkins&Will version of the standard AIA C401-2007 subconsultant agreement for every subconsultant. It contains terms and conditions that protect us in relation to insurance maintained by our subconsultants. The most current version can be found as a template in Microsoft Word in the pwAIA template folder.2. Send client-generated prime contracts (or subcontracts when we’re a subconsultant) to the Legal Department for review with sufficient time to prepare a substantive response. Expect comments to the insurance section and understand that those comments are important and must be resolved before signing the contract.3. Don’t make contractual promises that may complicate coverage, such as (a) warranties or guarantees; (b) standards of care beyond negligence; (c) obligations to defend others; or (d) absolute duties or obligations which may result in liability without proof of negligence (e.g., stating that the Contract Documents will be “without errors”).4. Don’t enter into joint ventures or other legal arrangements that may make us responsible for the acts of others, or which differ from standard subconsultant agreements, without the written approval of the Legal Department.No one in Perkins&Will is authorized to approve or enter into contract terms which are uninsurable, or may result in uninsurable risk, without the prior written consent of the Risk Committee.Electronic File Transfer Agreements / EFTAAn Electronic File Transfer Agreement (EFTA) must be executed before sending electronic design documents (AutoCAD files, Revit models) to an outside party who is not under contract with Perkins&Will.Four EFTA forms are available for use in transferring electronic design documents, depending upon the party to whom the transfer is being made. EFTA forms are available on the Legal Department page and are described below.· The EFTA (Contractor or Subcontractor) is to be used when sending files directly to the prime contractor (or multiple prime contractors where applicable.) Note that this form is to be executed by the prime contractor (general contractor, multiple prime contractors or construction manager) and then applies to all subcontractors. Individual EFTA forms are not required for subcontractors.· The EFTA (Designer) is to be used when sending files to design professionals to whom we are not contracted.· The EFTA (Former Client) is to be used when sending files of a previously completed project to the client who originally hired Perkins&Will for that project.· The EFTA (Third Party Recipient) is to be used when sending files to a third party that does not fall into one of the specific categories above.A PDF-type file of the executed Electronic File Transfer Agreement, should be filed in the Contracts area of PMTK in the Project App before delivery of the data. When the forms are transmitted electronically, the project manager is required to verify that the executed agreement has not been altered.It is important to understand that unless Perkins&Will has a legal relationship with the contractor (as in, for example, a design-build project), we are not required to provide information to the contractor other than as specifically required by the Owner-Architect Agreement. Nonetheless, it is our usual practice to provide electronic drawings or models for use as backgrounds for coordination purposes, provided that the conditions of the EFTA are met.AIA Standard Agreement Forms and DocumentsThe standard agreements for use on Perkins&Will projects can be found as templates in Microsoft Word in the pwAIA template folder. For agreements with clients, use the B-101 or B-103 templates. For agreements with consultants, use the C-401 template. Others are available for non-standard projects.The full set of PDF copies of the Perkins&Will standard AIA agreements are available on the Legal Department page. It is Perkins&Will’s policy to use our in-house edited versions of the AIA documents included in Microsoft Word unless clients insist on using their own form of agreement.Note: For projects in Canada, refer to the Canadian Construction Documentation Council for applicable agreement forms and documents (www.ccdc.org).

title: Financial Performance of Projects UK
content: Requests for Additional ServicesThis is an area that requires the focus and constant attention of the project manager and managing principal, as it directly impacts profitability and client relationships. Often, requests for additional services may be overlooked due to the fear of irritating a client, or because of a loosely defined Scope of Services.Techniques available to mitigate these problems include:1. Clearly defining the scope of services: This should be reviewed in detail with the client before beginning work. Renderings, models, and production of 3-D imagery should be clearly defined as basic or additional services.2. Presenting the need for additional services to a client before undertaking the work: Saying, “Perkins&Will would be happy to perform those services for you. Would you like us to submit a proposal?” is easier and usually more successful than requesting compensation after the work is done.What constitutes additional services depends on the actual terms of the Owner-Architect Agreement for the project. According to the AIA B101 or B103 Standard Form of Agreement Between Owner and Architect, any of the following might be considered as an additional service:1. Programming2. Existing facilities surveys3. Building information modeling4. Detailed cost estimating5. Expanded on-site project representation6. Conformed construction documents7. As-designed record drawings8. As-constructed record drawings9. Pre- and/or post-occupancy evaluation10. Coordination of owner’s consultants11. Commissioning12. LEED® (or similar) certification documentation13. Fast-track design services14. Furniture, furnishings, and equipment (FFE) design15. Reviewing a submittal out of sequence from the approved submittal schedule16. Responding to RFIs that are not prepared per the Contract Documents (i.e., “frivolous RFIs”)17. Preparing Change Orders or CCDs that require evaluation of contractor proposals or changes to the documents18. Evaluating an extensive number of claims19. Evaluating substitution requests from the contractor and changing the documents accordingly20. Providing construction phase services 60 days after either Substantial Completion or the anticipated date of Substantial Completion as defined in the contractIf the agreement is not based on a standard AIA agreement form, make sure that basic and additional services are clearly defined.For additional services on a project that is already under contract with the client, a proposal letter should be sent to the client before the start of the additional service work. The proposal letter should specify the scope, fee, and schedule of the additional services; refer to the terms and conditions of the base contract; and be signed by an authorized Perkins&Will individual. When we receive the copy signed by the client, we can begin the work.It is the policy of Perkins&Will that the design team does not proceed with additional services work, during any phase, until authorized in writing by the owner.Financial Procedures TerminologyVisit the PMTK QuickStart Guide for explanations and definitions of the Perkins&Will financial procedures terminology.

title: Project Startup and Project Planning UK
content: Goals StatementFor Perkins&Will to design a project that meets the client’s goals, those goals must be understood by the design team. A goal-setting or visioning session should be scheduled as early as possible, once a project has started. All major stakeholders, including members of the owner’s team (project management, facilities management and users), along with key members of the Perkins&Will team, should participate. If there is a construction manager on the project team, they should also be represented. The session should be led by Perkins&Will, typically by the project manager or the managing principal. After all stakeholders have spoken, well-defined goals should be agreed to and documented. The project team can then use these goals as guides for making decisions throughout the project.The determination of a project's Architect of Record (AOR) must be made as early in the project as possible, preferably at the pursuit stage, but no later than project kickoff. See Chapter 3 Quality Program, Quality Program Roles and Responsibilities for team member responsibilities. Also see the Sealing Documents section in Chapter 5 for more information.Kickoff MeetingsProject KickoffThe project manager is responsible for scheduling and leading a kickoff meeting with the managing director, managing principal, design principal, director of operations, and the technical director to review the work plan material.Phase KickoffAt the start of each project phase, the project manager is responsible for scheduling and leading a meeting with the Perkins&Will design team, including consultants under contract to us. The project goals and the work plan material should be shared with the team so that the individuals understand their roles and responsibilities, project milestones and the team organization. A template of an Internal Team Kickoff Meeting Agenda is available in Microsoft Word in the pwGeneral template folder.Internal Design Team Construction Phase KickoffFollowing execution of the Owner-Contractor Agreement, the project manager will schedule a CCA kickoff meeting with the Perkins&Will team and our consultants to establish the procedures that will be followed by all members of the design team during construction. In addition, checklists are available to help the team perform CCA responsibilities at the start of construction, during construction, and at the end of construction (see the Construction Contract Administration chapter.)Project Schedule and BudgetVisit the PMTK QuickStart Guide for explanations and definitions related to building, monitoring and maintaining project schedules and budgets..Construction Contract Administration (CCA) Phase BudgetingIt is not possible to have a single rule of thumb to determine the appropriate construction phase budget. For some projects, 20% of the fee will be adequate; on others, 30% may not be enough. Rather than base the CCA budget on a percentage of the architectural fee, the project manager needs to consider the amount of effort that will be required over the duration of the construction schedule. For instance, if it is anticipated that a full-time project architect will be needed over a two-year construction period, then the budget should be 2 years x 2,080 hours/year = 4,160 PA hours. It doesn’t matter if that is 20%, 25%, or 30% (or more) of the architectural fee; as long as it is the best estimate for what it will actually take to perform our contractual responsibilities.It is important that the duration of construction be established in our agreement with the owner, since that is the basis of our construction phase budgeting. It is also important that the project manager and the team be diligent in identifying potential additional services. See the Requests for Additional Services section for more information.CCA Project Team OrganizationIt is Perkins&Will’s goal to have the core design team be consistent from the design phases through the construction phase. In most cases, this means the project architect and one or more junior team members will perform the CCA services. The project manager will continue to take care of contractual issues and will remain the primary client contact, but the project architect will have the most interactions with the client, contractors and consultants. The advantage to this approach is that, even if the team has limited CCA experience, they can use their knowledge of the project’s design and the guidance provided in CHAPTER 7: CONSTRUCTION CONTRACT ADMINISTRATION, to provide the required CCA services.

title: Project Document Management UK
content:
Filing and Archiving ProtocolsThere are two basic reasons for retaining (filing and archiving) documents: (1) There are legal requirements for retaining them; (2) it is beneficial to Perkins&Will to retain them beyond their immediately useful life. We should dispose of all redlines, check sets, coordination sets, and draft copies unless we have notice of a claim or expected litigation or have a contractual duty to notify the client before disposing of documents. In the former case we need to keep everything until the matter is resolved. In the latter case of the client notice, we need to keep everything until we receive written notice that it’s acceptable to dispose of, or send the client, everything.Criteria for Required Retention· Required by regulation – There are federal and state laws stipulating mandated periods of retention; these typically apply to corporate, employment, ERISA, and tax records. There are few regulations which pertain to regulatory periods of retention for business records related to our professional services. Statutes of Repose and Limitations must also be considered in evaluating the retention of documents. Statutes of Repose bar actions against architects after a specified period of time following the completion of services or substantial completion. Statutes of Limitation bar actions against architects after a specified period of time following an injury or discovery of a deficiency. These statutes vary state by state; contact the Legal Department for a list of the current statutory time periods.· Required by contract – In many instances, we enter into contracts with governmental or large institutional clients who require that we retain some or all business records related to a particular project for some period of time. Typically, this will be restricted to financial records, but not always. If these requirements exist, they will be enumerated in the Owner-Architect Agreement.Criteria for Beneficial (though not required) Retention· Beneficial for business continuity – There are some records, like bank records, leases, and contracts with parties owing duties to the corporation, that are so valuable to the corporation that they should be retained permanently.· Beneficial for knowledge management – There are some records that form the basis of the valuable “intellectual property” of the company, which needs to be retained to ensure that there is no “reinvention of the wheel” on future projects and that the business can provide its services in the most efficient manner. These would include standard details, standard specifications, and checklists.· Beneficial for risk management – There are some records which should be kept because they demonstrate our performance of contract obligations and professional duties. These should be limited too Contract Documents or correspondence that refer to Contract Documents—i.e., all contracts and contract amendments and addenda, whether executed or not, including email, letter, and fax correspondence related to contracts.o All deliverables which document fulfillment of contract duties or obligations, such as final deliverables (plans and specifications) called for in the contract for SD / DD / CD phases, reports required to be prepared and delivered, submittals to be reviewed and responded to, RFIs presented and answered, final QA/QC sign-off sheets, etc.o All documents and correspondence (including email) that document or refer to issues with performance and our response to that correspondence. Carefully consider whether to keep interim or intermediate steps or positions, since they may conflict with, and may not be relevant to, our final professional opinion and documentation.Documents That Must Be FiledHard copiesNote: If documents only exist in hard copy, these should be filed. If documents are duplicated in electronic form (scanned), only the electronic version needs to be filed and the original hard copy can be discarded.1. All project-related original hard-copy documents to and from clients, consultants, contractors, and authorities having jurisdiction (AHJs). (Note: “Original” means the actual document that was sent to Perkins&Will, which could be a duplicate of an original that was not sent to us.)2. Perkins&Will accounting documentation (e.g., work authorization forms, invoices, etc.)3. All contractually required deliverables and all contracts, including those contractually required for interim milestones, must be filed as hard copies.4. One copy of hard-copy submittals (including samples) that were returned to the contractor with a review action (Note: Shop drawings are eventually archived, samples typically are not.)Electronic Copies1. Electronic documents sent to or received from client, consultants, contractor, and AHJs through email2. An electronic copy of each submittal returned electronically to the contractor with a review action3. Digital construction site photographs4. Incoming emails from clients, consultants, contractors, and AHJs.5. Outgoing emails sent or copied to clients, consultants, contractors, and AHJs.Documents That Should Not Be Filed1. Duplicate copies of original hard-copy documents to and from client, consultants, contractor, and AHJs, when the original is filed electronically. The exception to this is if there are important notations made on a duplicate that supplement information on the original.2. In-house Quality Assurance or Quality Control marked-up sets of drawings or project manuals (unless they are required to be retained in our Owner-Architect Agreement)3. Communication (hard copy and email) internal to Perkins&Will that has not been sent or copied to anyone outside the firm.4. Draft versions of documents sent to client, consultants, contractor, and AHJs (unless the draft version was sent to them for review and comment).5. Marked-up “scratch” copies of submittals.6. Personal email.7. Personal notebooks.Documents on Local Computer Hard Drives or in Microsoft Outlook Folders1. Project-related documents or emails that meet the criteria identified as Documents That Must Be Filed for filing are not to be saved on your local computer hard drive. Documents must be filed in the project’s SharePoint and P drive folder structures; emails should be saved in Microsoft Outlook in a project-specific folder.2. Project-related inter-office or intra-office emails once they are no longer needed should be deleted.3. Personal emails should be deleted.4. Drafts of documents, once the final document has been issued, should be deleted.Archiving of Project DocumentsIt is important to understand the distinction between active project files and archived project files. Active files are those that are maintained in a central file location (both physical and electronic) in a Perkins&Will office or a project-related field office. Archived files are those that are kept off-site at a remote storage facility, typically at an overhead cost to the office.Active files are not to be stored on individual computer hard drives.At the end of the project, when all of our contractual services have been completed, and all project-related invoices (for both payments to us and payments from us to our consultants) have been paid, all project files must be archived, following procedures described in CHAPTER 8: PROJECT CLOSE-OUT.Project Folder StructureThe Project Folder Structure is the framework within which individual project documents are filed. While many index structures are possible, this is the structure Perkins&Will has chosen to use across the entire firm. This structure establishes a common language for us to communicate and share critical project information, regardless of the office, discipline or market sector leading the project. It is based on our collective experience of the types of information required over the course of a project. It is understood that not every folder may be used on every project; it is reasonable to assume that some may remain empty. Use this structure to file your project’s documents, so that others can readily find information. Do not create filing structures different from these standards; this can lead to frustration and wasted time as others struggle to understand your logic in creating them.The current Project Folder Structure is an evolution of our file storage that leverages our Cloud-based platforms for file sharing and collaboration within our teams and with external Clients and Consultants. Project files will be stored in 3 distinct locations:· P: Drive - DESIGN, containing all modeling, graphics and presentation files, except Revit models (which now reside in BIM360.)· MS Teams - DOCS, divided into 3 broad categories: Deliverables, Design Team and PW Internal Team. Deliverables: files that are for external transfer. Design Team: files that are for both internal use and that can be shared with our consultants. PW Internal Team: private files restricted to Perkins&Will access, See below for the updated structure and a description of the suggested contents for each folder.· BIM360, contains Revit filesAt the beginning of every project, after a project number has been assigned, the IT manager will create project folders on the P:drive of the Perkins&Will Studio holding the prime contract for the project, on a project-specific MS Team and in BIM360. All project files are based on the base six-digit project number assigned by the accounting staff; this base project number is to remain unchanged throughout the course of the project. Sub-numbers may become necessary as the project progresses; the project manager needs to contact Accounting for these sub-numbers to be recorded in Deltek Vision. Deliverables Folder Contents yyyy-mm-dd_Issue_Name The Deliverables folder is intended strictly as a transfer location for distribution to external parties.All information in this folder should be a copy of a file permanently stored elsewhere in the permanent project file(either on P:/ or BIM360 or SharePoint). The project team is responsible for deleting folders once all recipients have downloaded the files, or when the team-defined expiration date arrives.**Create individual folders for file transfers. not copies, of permit issuance drawings, specifications and other deliverables.
These documents should remain in the DESIGN folder.)Copies of Issued Building and Other Permits Information and Product Data Related to Permit-Specific Materials and Systems (e.g. bird-safe glass, Knox box.) Archive Word files that are no longer relevant to the project, but may be useful at some point in the future.
