If not, files can be placed in
this root folder Dynamo Dynamo scripts PointCloud ReCap projects, Matterport downloads, other point cloud platform filesPoint cloud support files Rhino Active Rhino files (.3DM) only**Can be split into multiple models if the project warrants Archive Archived models Conveyor Data for Conveyor workflows, used to translate Rhino geometry to Revit Scripts Grasshopper, or other, scripts specific to the project Links PDFs, PNGs, JPGs and other files that
are linked in any design application software. Active, current files only.
Obsolete links should be discarded. Except as noted below, subfolders are not
recommended.**If warranted, create individual folders for linked file type (e.g. diagrams, facades,
photos, plans, sections, visualizations, etc.) Publish Clarity **Create individual folders for publish events: yyyy-mm-dd_Publish Event In-Progress **Create individual folders for in-progress publish events: yyyy-mm-dd_In-Progress Event Record Sets **Create individual folders for record deliverables: yyyy-mm-dd_Issue Title Renders **Create individual folders for milestone renderings: yyyy-mm-dd_Milestone Title Study All files related to individual team member design studies, regardless of application**Create individual folders for team members: FIRSTNAME_LASTNAME Electronic File and Document NamingPerkins&Will has adopted a standard format for the naming of all electronic files stored in the folder structure noted above. This format is intended to ease searching for files through consistent organization of information. It also includes keywords as a “shorthand” version of content to alert people to a file’s contents. Use of other file naming formats will make it harder for people to find documents within the project’s files. Do not invent new file naming formats for your projects.File Naming ConventionAll files in the Consultants and Teams folder should be given document names that contain the following five fields: Field 1 Field 2 Field 3 Field 4 Field 5 Identifier (with # where applicable) YYYY-MM- DD 9-Digit Project Number Note/Keyword Program extension (automatic) For clarity, within a document name use an underscore (_) between fields 1 to 4, and a period (.) before field 5. Use a hyphen (-) to connect information within a field (see examples below).Example: ltr_2023-07-29_123456.000_file naming.docxField 1 of a document name is a file (document) Identifier indicating the type of document.See below for Perkins&Will standard identifiers.Example: ltr_Field 2 is the Date the document is issued, in the format YYYY-MM-DD (year, month, day) to allow chronological sorting. Always use a preceding “0” for single-digit numbers.Example: ltr_2023-07-29_Field 3 is the 9-digit project number, with separating period (.).Example: ltr_2023-07-29_123456.000_Field 4 is a brief Descriptive Note or Key Word(s) used to describe the document content.Example: ltr_2023-07-29_123456.000_file namingField 5, the Program Extension, must be separated from the preceding portion of the document name by a period (part of the extension), and is usually automatically affixed to the end of the document name by the program used to create the document. The program extension is necessary to enable the document to be opened in the future. Examples of the Program Extensions are: for Microsoft Word documents, .docx; or for Microsoft Excel spreadsheets, .xlsx. The program extension should not be changed manually.Example: ltr_2023-07-29_123456.000_file naming.docxAll documents are to be saved in the appropriate Project File Index Folder (see above Project Folder Structure) before printing to ensure the automatic attachment of the document name and path into the footer of the printed document. Perkins&Will Standard Identifiers (for use in document naming) Description Identifier Addendum add (with # in Field 1) Agenda agn Architect’s Supplemental Instructions asi (with # in Field 1) Cert. of Substantial Completion csc Change Order co (with # in Field 1) Checklist chk Construction Change Directive ccd (with # in Field 1) Contract, Agreement, Letter of Intent cont Electronic File Transfer Agreement efta Email em Field Observation Report for (with # in Field 1) Letter ltr Meeting minutes mm Memorandum memo Notice of Noncompliance nnc Photograph or Video ph Program prog Project directory dir Proposal prop Report rpt Schedule sch Work Authorization wa Email ProtocolsEmail is considered legal and official project correspondence and should be written with the same level of care as any other professional correspondence. That includescorrect spelling, grammar, and punctuation. Every email that is sent is a representation of Perkins&Willand should be treated as such. The following list of “dos and don’ts” should be used to help make your emails communicate what you intend, without annoying or offending the recipients or placing Perkins&Will at unnecessary risk.Email correspondence should be managed and stored by creating project folders in Microsoft Outlook and dragged/dropped into the appropriate folders. Email Dos and Don’ts1. Proofread your emails before you send them.2. If you aren’t prepared to see your email printed on the front page of the New York Times (or Chicago Tribune, London Times, etc.), don’t send it.3. Be concise and to the point. (Many of your emails will be read only on a small hand-held device screen.)4. Use proper spelling, grammar and punctuation.5. Address the recipient by name in the greeting.6. Include a closing (e.g. Regards, Thanks, Sincerely) and your official Perkins&Will Signature7. Respond in a timely manner.8. Do not attach unnecessary files (again, consider the hand-held devices).9. Do not overuse the high priority option.10. Do not write in CAPITALS unless you really need to emphasize something.11. Don’t leave out the string of previous email messages.12. Do not overuse “Reply to All.”13. Take care with abbreviations and don’t use emojis.14. Do not forward chain letters.15. Do not request delivery receipts.16. Do not use email to discuss confidential information.17. Include a meaningful subject in the subject line, starting with the project name and number, then the topic.18. Avoid using “Urgent” or “Important” in your subject line unless it really is urgent or important.19. Avoid long sentences. And long paragraphs. And long emails. (again, consider the hand-held devices.)20. Don’t send or forward emails containing libelous, offensive, racist or obscene remarks. (And remember, it is the recipient who is the judge of what is libelous, offensive, racist or obscene.)21. Don’t reply to spam.22. Use the cc: field sparingly.

title: Construction Cost Management UK
content: In order to understand Perkins&Will’s typical role relative to construction costs, it is important to clarify two distinct activities—cost estimating and cost management—and Perkins&Will’s role in each.· Cost Estimating: The process of identifying the estimated construction cost of the project at the time of bid. The cost estimate should include all the work documented by the design team at the time of the estimate, as well as reasonable assumptions to include work intended but not yet shown. The estimate should include contractor general conditions costs, overhead and profit. It should include contingencies for design (appropriate to the project phase) and construction. It should be based on current market costs and include an escalation factor to a specific point in the future (typically the time of bid or mid-point of construction.)· Cost Management: The process of managing the owner’s team, the design team, and the cost estimators to ensure that the construction cost at the time of bid is within the owner’s budget. This encompasses a broad range of activities: working with the cost experts to develop a realistic cost model, producing a work plan that identifies costing milestones, designing the project to the budget, evaluating the cost estimates and reporting to the owner, and establishing value engineering procedures that maintain the desired level of quality in the project.While neither the architect nor the owner has control over the contractor’s method of determining bid prices, the design must typically be within the owner’s budget and should satisfy the owner’s functional, programmatic, and operational requirements. The best means of delivering a cost-appropriate design is through accurate estimating.Estimates are a means of establishing a value for the “Cost of the Work.” This includes “…the total cost to the Owner to construct all elements of the Project designed and specified by the Architect and shall include contractor’s general conditions costs, overhead and profit. The Cost of the Work does not include the compensation of the Architect, the costs of the land, rights-of-way, financing, contingencies for changes in the work or other costs that are the responsibility of the Owner.” (AIA Document B101-2017, Standard Form of Agreement Between Owner and Architect, Section 6.1)The standard AIA B101 Owner-Architect Agreement includes requirements for cost estimates provided by the architect for each design phase. Since Perkins&Will has no internal cost estimators, we rely on cost consultants, under contract to us, to provide this service. However, the responsibility to the owner for the timeliness and accuracy of the estimate is Perkins&Will’s. In some cases, the owner may opt to shift the responsibility for cost estimates to another party, such as a construction manager providing preconstruction services.AIA Document B101-2017, section 6.3, defines the kind of cost estimate that is included in basic services: “The Architect’s estimate of the Cost of the Work shall be based on current area, volume or similar conceptual estimating techniques. If the Owner requests detailed cost estimating services, the Architect shall provide these services as an Additional Service…”B101 also states that “the Architect shall be permitted to include contingencies for design, bidding and price escalation; to determine what materials, equipment, component systems and types of construction are to be included in the Contract Documents; to make reasonable adjustments in the program scope of the Project; and to include in the Contract Documents alternate bids as may be necessary to adjust the estimate of the Cost of the Work to meet the Owner’s budget…”

title: Quality Assurance UK
content:
QA Empirical DefinitionThe planned and systematic set of procedures necessary to ensure that the services and documents meet industry-established performance and technical requirements and conform to applicable codes and regulations and the requirements of the professional service agreement and the client program.QA Goals1. Process:a. Engage in a consistent process where the design intent is clearly conveyed within a logically structured set of documents that contains drawings, narratives, and specifications.b. Reinforce the fact that QA is the responsibility of all team members, not one individual, particularly not the QC reviewer.c. Ensure technical accuracy and coordination within the documents and among all disciplines.d. Perform periodic technical reviews/pinups (at least once during SD and DD) involving the office technical director, specifications writer, and senior technical staff not directly involved with the project.e. Increase team efficiency and productivity, reducing time spent by the team and increasing profit.2. Coordination:a. Ensure that design and coordination issues are discovered and resolved to prevent delays inthe delivery of the documents.b. Coordinate with interdisciplinary team members within Perkins&Will and with external consultants.3. Documentation:a. Produce clear and coordinated documents that meet contractual requirements and Perkins&Will quality standards, thus minimizing addenda, revisions and Change Orders.b. Use a consistent method of conveying information (sheet notes, keynotes, graphic conventions, etc.)c.	Eliminate redundancy in documentation (overdetailing, excessive notes/system descriptions, etc.) QA Project Milestones Milestone When it Occurs Who Participates Documents/Activities Project Kickoff Meeting Project Start-upStart of each phase Managing Principal

title: Quality Program Roles and Responsibilities UK
content:
Role Quality Assurance Responsibilities Quality Control Responsibilities Technical Director Manages office wide scheduling and staffing of Pull Planning, Mockup/Cartoon Set Reviews andMid-Phase Reviews. Participates in Project Technical Reviews/pinups. Monitors project staffing to ensure each team’s ability to perform QA tasks. Monitors office wide performance of the Quality Program. Manages scheduling of QC reviewers. Reviews unresolved QC issues with the PA. Design Director Monitors project staffing to ensure each team's ability to perform QA tasks and advance the design appropriately.Monitors project development for appropriate development of the design and advancement of the concept. Managing Principal Includes consultants’ QA/QC requirements in architect-consultant contracts.
Determines level of client (and CM, where applicable) involvement in the QA process.Determine, in conjunction with the Project Manager and Project Architect, who will serve as Architect of Record (AOR) for the project. Has the authority and responsibility to withhold the issuance of documents pending the QC process's satisfactory completion. Project Manager Team assignments (ensure adequate staffing), including QA responsibilities. Integrates QA activities into the project schedule. Manages client’s deliverable expectations. Select and contract with consultants; ongoing management of consultant scope of services - incorporating QA expectations of each. Ensures consultants perform their own quality assurance process.Confirm that AOR is engaged in the project and exercising appropriate control and oversight as required by local laws and rules. Ensures adequate budget and schedule for QC reviews. Reviews QC assignment with the Technical Director to ensure availability of the reviewer. Informs the QC reviewer about upcoming deliverables and schedule changes. Project Designer Oversees design quality and adherence to design intent. Responsible for reviewing the QC comments to verify that the development of the project and direction suggested by the QC reviewer is consistent with the project design intent. Project Architect Has primary responsibility for ensuring that QA is performed by team members. Assists PM in scheduling QA activities. Schedules Mid-Phase Reviews. Responsible for the technical quality of the documents (using checklists as a guide for issues/topics). Assists the PM in customizing QA processes for each project. Management of internal project team’s work assignments. Develops of mockup sets/drawing organization/ deliverables list and tracks development. Leads coordination among consultants and between drawings and specifications. Leads implementation of the Specifications Preparation Procedures. Reviews and marks up documents and oversees corrections before QC review. Assigns presentation assignments for Mid-Phase Reviews. Distributes QA Checklists to team members. Develops and maintains the BIM Execution Plan.Ensures regular engagement of the AOR throughout the design phases and coordinates signing and sealing of documents prior to permit or other official issuance. Communicates with the Technical Director and QC reviewer about any unresolved issues. Resolves issues prior to release of documents. Provides the QC Reviewers with all the necessary documents for quality reviews. Responds to the comments from the Quality Control Reviewer and oversees corrections before issuing the documents. SpecificationsWriter Production of specifications.Coordination of drawings and specifications with Project Architect. Addresses QC comments on the specifications. Each Project Team Member Each team member checks his or her own work. BIM Management. Presentation at Mid-Phase Reviews. Completion of QA Checklists. Addresses QC comments. Alerts the PA and PM to QC comments that require additional discussion and/or resolution. QC reviewer Advisory role Triages documents to verify that the Design Team performed Quality Assurance. Reviews the project deliverables on the schedule established by the project Manager, using the QC topics list as a guide. Meets with the PA before the QC review to understand the project scope, schedule, goals, and document structure. Meets with the managing principal, PM, PA, and technical director if issues or concerns are raised that would suggest delaying the document issuance.

title: Introduction UK
content: The Project Manual and Construction Drawings together comprise Perkins&Will’s Contract Documents, which are to be coordinated with the owner’s construction contract with the contractor. The Project Manual, which includes the specifications, is the responsibility of the project team and the Specifications Writer. The Project Manual is the part of the Contract Documents that defines:1. How the project will be bid/procured.a. Instructions to the bidders as to the procedures for preparing and submitting bids. (These Procurement and Contracting Requirements are not Contract Documents but are included in the Project Manual).2. How the Contract will be administered.a. The Contracting requirements include Instructions to the Contractor on procedures to be followed during Construction, including the processes for submittals, review, and recordkeeping, and the information to be transferred to the owner upon completion of construction.3. The physical qualities and characteristics of construction products, components, equipment, and materials, including fabrication, installation, testing, cleaning, and protection.Specifications and Construction Drawings are legally binding Contract Documents, so it is imperative that we produce them so they are unambiguous in defining our design intent. Specification contents, particularly terminology, products, or materials, must be tightly coordinated with the Contract Drawings to avoid conflicts or a lack of clarity. Conflicts in our Contract Documents will result in Requests for Interpretation (RFIs) and Change Orders. This chapter of the Project Delivery Manual describes Perkins&Will’s approach to developing Project Manuals for our projects.See the Signing and Sealing Documents section in Chapter 5 for information and responsibilities related to the project's Architect of Record. Hub (perkinswill.com) https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/lvdZEv0ElhCM3A9 auto false 14px Link to full size Specifications Process Map - Executive Summary https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/MSgUjZiErEz0e7z auto false 14px Link to Full Size Specifications Process Map - Detailed

title: Project Manual Components UK
content: The Project Manual is composed of Procurement Requirements, Contracting Requirements, and Specifications.Procurement (Bidding) RequirementsThis section generally includes the Invitation to Bid (or Advertisement for Bids), Instructions to Bidders, the bid form, other sample forms, and portions of Addenda pertaining to the procurement (bidding)requirements. By definition, the procurement (bidding) requirements address matters pertaining to the procurement process and should not contain Contract Document related provisions (such as thoseaddressed in the Contracting Requirements portion of Division 00 and Division 01). Unless specifically directed by the owner and its legal counsel, or defined in the Owner-Architect Agreement, the procurement (bidding) requirements are not Contract Documents. Coordination with Division 00The Procurement Requirements are not typically a part of the Contract Documents, since they pertain to and govern the Procurement process only, not the construction process. As such, Procurement and Contracting requirements should not be discussed in the Specifications. Do not include references to submission of bids, site visits, bonds and insurance, or pre-bid approvals of substitutions in the Specifications.Contracting Requirements Owner-Contractor AgreementThis document is the agreement (legally binding contract) between the owner and the contractor for construction. AIA’s A-101-2017 (CCDC-2-2020 in Canada) is often used for this agreement, but owners may prefer their own form of agreement.Perkins&Will may assist the owner’s legal counsel in the preparation of this document. At a minimum, we should be aware of any provisions in this agreement that impose an obligation upon Perkins&Will that may not be consistent with the Scope of Services in the Owner-Architect Agreement for the project.Conditions of the ContractThe conditions of the contract detail contractual provisions describing the rights, responsibilities, and relationships of the parties to the Owner-Contractor Agreement.General Conditions of the Contract for ConstructionAn integral part of the Owner-Contractor Agreement setting forth the rights, responsibilities, and relationships of the owner, contractor, and architect. AIA’s A201-2017 (CCDC-2-2020 General Conditions of the Stipulated Price Contract for Canada) is the commonly used standard. The architect is not a party to the Owner-Contractor Agreement. The architect’s duties are defined in the Owner-Architect Agreement and are provided as information in the General Conditions.Supplementary ConditionsProject-specific modifications or additions to the General Conditions of the Contract for Construction. AIA’s A503-2017 (CCDC 20A Guide to Supplementary Conditions or owner Supplementary Conditions for Canada) provides guidance to developing the Supplementary Conditions. This document may be prepared by the architect, the owner, the program manager, or the construction manager but the final supplementary conditions of the contract must be reviewed by the Owner and their legal counsel.Coordination with Division 01The Project Manager, Project Architect,and Consultants should read Division 01 carefully, as it defines Perkins&Will’s work during the Construction Contract Administration phase of the project. Division 01 “General Requirements” specify the administrative and procedural requirements for the following items:1. Allowances, alternates, and unit prices2. Payment and modification procedures3. Substitutions4. Scheduling5. Project management and coordination, including meeting and RFI procedures6. Actionable submittals including shop drawings, product data, and samples7. Mockups and testing requirements8. Temporary facilities, including utility services and facilities to be provided by the contactor on site, as well as who pays for those utilities and facilities9. Product requirements10. Project closeout11. Project record documents, operations and maintenance manuals, warranties, and spare parts (“attic stock”)12. Additional sections required to address sustainability requirements during construction, including construction waste management and disposal, protection, and commissioningSpecificationsThe specifications are written descriptions of products, materials, and systems, defining the quality, performance, installation, and procedural requirements of the work to be incorporated into the project.MasterFormat 2010 defines 50 major divisions (Divisions 00–49), with 35 divisions populated by Construction Specifications Institute (CSI) at this time. Within each division, a specification section is generally identified by three pairs of numbers, with the first pair being the division number. Procurement and Contracting Requirements GroupDivision 00Procurement and Contracting Requirements Specifications GroupGeneral Requirements SubgroupDivision 01 General RequirementsFacility Construction SubgroupDivision 02Existing ConditionsDivision 03 ConcreteDivision 04 MasonryDivision 05 MetalsDivision 06 Wood, Plastics, and CompositesDivision 07 Thermal and Moisture ProtectionDivision 08 OpeningsDivision 09 FinishesDivision 10 SpecialtiesDivision 11 EquipmentDivision 12 FurnishingsDivision 13 Special ConstructionDivision 14 Conveying EquipmentDivision 15 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 16 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 17 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 18 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 19 Reserved for Future ExpansionFacility Services SubgroupDivision 20 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 21 Fire SuppressionDivision 22 PlumbingDivision 23 HVACDivision 24 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 25 Integrated AutomationDivision 26 ElectricalDivision 27 CommunicationsDivision 28 Electronic Safety and SecurityDivision 29 Reserved for Future ExpansionSite and Infrastructure SubgroupDivision 30 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 31 EarthworkDivision 32 Exterior ImprovementsDivision 33 UtilitiesDivision 34 TransportationDivision 35 Waterway and MarineDivision 36 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 37 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 38 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 39 Reserved for Future ExpansionProcess Equipment SubgroupDivision 40 Process IntegrationDivision 41 Material Processing and Handling EquipmentDivision 42 Process Heating, Cooling, and Drying EquipmentDivision 43 Process Gas and Liquid Handling, Purification and Storage EquipmentDivision 44 Pollution Control EquipmentDivision 45 Industry-specific Manufacturing EquipmentDivision 46 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 47 Reserved for Future ExpansionDivision 48 Electrical Power GenerationDivision 49 Reserved for FutureExpansionMasterFormat is the standard six-digit organizational structure for construction-related specifications in the US and Canada. It is used by AIA MasterSpec, SpecLink, SpecText, the US Department of Defense, Unified Facility Guide Specifications, and most major private and government master guide specification providers in the US and Canada. It is the basis for Perkins&Will’s Master Specifications.Every specification section is typically arranged according to SectionFormat and is divided into three parts:Part 1 GENERALPart 1 describes administrative, procedural, and temporary requirements unique to the section. Part 1 is an extension of subjects covered in Division 01 “General Requirements” to address information specific to the section.Part 2 PRODUCTSPart 2 describes products, materials, equipment, fabrications, mixes, systems, and assemblies that are required for incorporation into the project. Materials and products are included with their quality requirements.Part 3 EXECUTIONPart 3 describes installation or application procedures, including preparatory actions and post- installation cleaning and protection. Site-built assemblies and site-manufactured products or systems are included.Furniture SpecificationsWhen included in Perkins&Will’s Scope of Services, furniture specifications, in conjunction with furniture plans, are intended to describe specified products and their locations for procurement by the owner or contractor. The furniture specifications may be bound into the Project Manual or be a separate, stand-alone document, depending upon the Owner-Contractor Agreement. Information that should appear in thefurniture specifications includes:1. Item Description (Generally select from the following categories: chair, table, workstation, private office, storage, miscellaneous)2. Manufacturer3. Model4. Project Name5. Perkins&Will Project Number6. Code (Generally select from the following codes: C-xx (chair), T-xx (table), WK-xx (workstation), OF-xx (private office), ST-xx (storage), MS-xx (miscellaneous))7. Issue Date and Issue Name8. Contact Information9. Style Number10. Description11. Sustainable Criteria12. Fire-resistance Criteria13. Dimensions14. Finish15. Image of Item16. Image of Finish(es)Determining quantities of each product is the responsibility of the contractor, furniture dealer, or other party contracted to procure furniture for the project. Furniture quantities are not to be included on any furniture specification sheets or summaries prepared by Perkins&Will.Room numbers are not to be included on any furniture specification sheets or summaries prepared by Perkins&Will. Bidders or procurement organizations should be referred to the Contract Drawings for locations of furniture items.When manufacturers’ or dealers’ drawings are included as part of the furniture specification, refer the bidders or procurement organizations to these drawings for dimensions and additional required components.Design and Engineering Consultant Specification RequirementsPerkins&Will’s Project Manager will schedule delivery of design consultants’ specification sections for review and coordination prior to their incorporation into the Project Manual. Consultants must comply with the design team’s schedule and Perkins&Will’s Quality Program. Consultants are required to deliver to Perkins&Will either Microsoft Word files or PDF files, or both, based on project and owner requirements. This requirement should be stipulated in the agreement between Perkins&Will and our consultants.Table of Contents: Within two weeks of the start of each design phase, each consultant shall prepare and deliver to Perkins&Will an electronic file listing those specification sections they intend to prepare for that phase of the project. Perkins&Will’s Specifications Writer will then prepare a consolidated Table of Contents for all disciplines and distribute it to all consultants for coordination purposes.When required by the authorities having jurisdiction (AHJ) to secure project approvals and/or permits, each consultant shall print out its table of contents sheet; sign, seal, and date that sheet only; and return it to Perkins&Will as a PDF file or original hard copy. In the case of jurisdictions that require embossed seals, return only the original; the PDF file will not be required. Otherwise, consultants are not to provide a signed, sealed copy of a table of contents for their sections.If there are any questions or uncertainty regarding the above requirements, the consultant shall seek clarification from the Perkins&Will Project Manager.

title: General Specifications Information UK
content: Specifications ProcessPerkins&Will’s policy is to use internal specification writers for all projects. A Specifications Writer will be assigned by the Spec Team based on individual Specification Writers’ expertise and availability in coordination with the project’s schedule. Use of an external specifications writer rather than an internal specifications writer must be approved by the Technical Director and Operations Director of the office.Coordinate with your Project Manager, Operations Director, and the Specifications Writer to identify the budget and schedule for the specifications.Design Team ResponsibilitiesThe Perkins&Will design team is responsible for the development of the Project Manual and is accountable for the content. This policy will accomplish the following:1. Having the team members most familiar with the project’s design intent actively participate in the preparation of the written specifications.2. Distributing responsibility for the production of the Project Manual at each phase of the project among the appropriate team members.3. Increasing the coordination of the drawings with the specifications.4. Increasing the familiarity of the project team with project requirements in preparation for the construction contract administration phase.5. Providing the opportunity for team members to improve their knowledge and skills as design professionals.Naming Document IssuancesDocument issuances during the design phase should match the contractual language in the Owner-Architect Agreement (e.g. Design Development, Stage 4 Tender). Percentage complete should not be used in issuance names (e.g. 50% Design Development, 100% Construction Documents) unless specifically required by the contract. The use of percentages can be misleading and arbitrary, as it is impossible to accurately determine percentage completion of a document set, and it gives the false impression that a "100%" set is perfectly complete. Instead, issuances should include terms like "Mid-Phase", "In Progress" or similar.Specification Deliverables by Project PhaseSchematic Design Phase: Based upon information contained in the Owner-Architect Agreement,the project team, including the Specifications Writer, will prepare a Narrative based on information in the Basis of Design or Conceptual Design documents.The SpecificationsWriter will prepare a preliminary Table of Contents (using Master Format) or an outline specification (by editing Master Guide Outline Specifications sections).Design Development Phase: The Specifications Writer will provide a preliminary edit of the Perkins&Will master specifications based on Schematic Design documents.Construction Documents Phase: Based upon information contained in the Design Development specifications and the Basis of Design, the Specifications Writer and the project team will prepare, review, and edit Perkins&Will master specifications.Specification Writers’Roles and Responsibilities in Preparing Specifications1. Becoming familiar with the project’s design intent.2. Asking questions when the design intent is not clear in the drawings.3. Offering advice and guidance to the project team.4. Reviewing and advising the project team regarding materials and products selected by the project team.5. Preparing the Project Manual (including integration of consultant-prepared divisions when required by the Owner–Architect agreement).6. Assisting the project team in establishing performance requirements for project elements.7. Leading the design team in preparing the Narrative during Schematic Design before its release as a deliverable.8. Leading the team in preparing Design Development outline specifications before their release as a deliverable.9. Leading the team in preparing the Contract Document Phase specifications.10. Assembling the Project Manual for each design phase.Design team members’ roles and responsibilities in preparing specifications:1. Establishing requirements for materials, products, components, systems, and assemblies.2. Providing the Specifications Writer with a copy of the project’s master schedule including all revisions.3. Selecting specific Basis of Design or Conceptual Design products, materials, and systems, along with acceptable alternative selections, based on history of performance in similar applications, compatibility with other selected products, contribution to sustainable design, and life cycle value.4. Identifying project regulatory, functional, sustainability, aesthetic, constructability, budgetary, client- specific, and other requirements.5. Evaluating material, product, component, system, and assembly performance attributes based upon requirements, criteria, and tests.6. Selecting acceptable Basis of Design or Conceptual Design alternate materials, products, components, systems, and assemblies.7. Obtaining product information from product representatives.8. Documenting material and product selections by assembling manufacturers’ product data sheets (cut sheets) for standard and custom products and for proprietary products/systems.9. Ensuring the terminology used on drawings matches terminology used in the specifications, and vice versa.10. Delivering information to Specifications Writer according to agreed-upon interim deadlines.11. Reviewing drafts of specifications for content and providing comments to the Specifications Writer.Language and TerminologyThe design team and Specifications Writer are to coordinate terms used in the Contract Documents to ensure that contractually defined terms used throughout the drawings are the same as those used in the Project Manual. The following terms shall be capitalized when used in Contract Documents:1. Owner (do not use “Owner’s Representative”),2. Architect (do not use “Consultant”),3. Contractor (do not use “General Contractor” or “Construction Manager”)4. Contract Documents (do not use “Construction Documents”),5. Drawings (do not use “Contract Drawings”),6. Specifications,7. Site,8. Project,9. Project Manual,10. Work,11. Contract Sum,12. Contract Time, and13. Change OrderFurnish/Install/ProvideThe design team, including the Specifications Writer, is to ensure proper use of the terms “furnish,” “install,” and “provide.” These terms are contractually defined in the supplementary conditions as follows:1. The term “furnish” means to purchase, supply, and deliver to the project site the specified products or items, which may then be consigned to another party.2. The term “install” is used to describe operations at the project site including receiving, unloading, storing, protecting, assembling, erecting, placing, anchoring, applying, finishing, and curing such products or items. Installation shall also include all final mechanical and electrical connections, unless such work is specifically excluded.3. The term “provide” or “providing” as used in the Project Manual means to furnish and install, complete and ready for the intended use.Improper use of these terms could cause confusion by contractor and owner’s vendors, and may result in disputes and potential additional costs.Definition of the Term “Architect”The term “Architect,” as used in the specifications, is the designated entity with whom the owner has contracted to prepare Contract Documents for the project. The Architect delegates parts of the work to others, such as civilengineer, landscape architect, structural engineer, mechanical engineer, electrical engineer, etc., but the Architect is the entity under contract with, and responsible to, the owner.Contractor TerminologyRefrain from using terms such as “Mechanical Contractor,” “Electrical Contractor,” Plumbing Contractor,” “Controls Contractor,” “Roofing Contractor,” “Kitchen Equipment Contractor,” “Security Contractor,” “each contractor,” “this contractor,” etc., unless the project is specifically being awarded to separate prime contractors with those specific designations. Otherwise, these entities are only subcontractors or installers, performing work under a subcontractor agreement with the “Contractor” (always capitalized, and omitting the word “General”).Unless the project has been specifically designated to be awarded under separate multiple prime contracts, there is only one Contractor under contract with the Owner.Since the Contract Documents are addressed to the contractor, designating or mandating that a particular portion or item of work is to be performed by a specific subcontractor or trade is not appropriate, may be in direct conflict with the terms of the general conditions, and is to be avoided. Similarly, do not use the phrase “Contractor shall…” as it is already inherent in the documents.

title: Drawing Organization UK
content: Drawing Organization-GeneralSheet Numbering Use the following pattern for numbering sheets: A 11 - 01 A Discipline CodeSee Discipline Codes below Group NumberSee Drawing Order and Types below Dash Sheet Number Sheet Number Suffix (Optional) Create a master sheet index at the start of each phase, including all consultants’ sheets. Disciplines not needed for the project can be removed. Keep sets of drawings organized and well planned to avoid rework in subsequent phases.Where sheets are bound into multiple volumes, the sheet index for each volume should include all the sheets of all volumes.Building Level TerminologyAll building levels, including mezzanines, basements, penthouses and roofs, should be called “Level,” not “Floor” or “Story”, unless this is a jurisdictional requirement.Levels are to be designated with a number rather than words (e.g., “Level 01 Floor Plan” not “Level One Floor Plan” “First Floor Plan” or “Ground Floor Plan”).Level 01 is the lowest level that provides fire department access to the building. Levels below the lowest level of fire department access should be called “Lower Level 01,” “Lower Level 02,” etc. with the lower level numberincreasing as the levels go down.Coordinate with the consultants for consistency in terminology. See below Sheet Organization-Architectural and Interiors Sheets for sheet numbering standards for building level plans.Consultants’ SheetsSheets prepared by consultants under contract to Perkins&Will are to be included with our sheets in the drawing set. Sheets by consultants not under contract to Perkins&Will can be bound into our set (and sometimes they need to be for AHJ review); if they are, the Perkins&Willlogo and any other reference to Perkins&Will must be removed from those sheets. Consultant sheets must follow the Perkins&Will sheet numbering system, as defined in below Sheet Numbering. Discipline CodesThe following is a list of the standard discipline codes, in order of appearance in a drawing set: Code Discipline G General C Civil L Landscape A Architectural (and interiors, if prepared by Perkins&Will) I Interiors (if not prepared by Perkins&Will) Q Specialty Equipment (Medical or Lab) (if not prepared by Perkins&Will) S Structural F Fire Protection P Plumbing M Mechanical E Electrical T Telecommunications W Wayfinding, Signage, and Graphics (if not prepared by Perkins&Will) K Kitchen/Food Service V Vertical Transportation X Other Disciplines Sheet Organization-Architectural and Interiors SheetsSheet OrganizationThe following is the standard framework for Perkins&Will sheet numbering.This sheet organization is intended to be applicable to most Perkins&Will projects, unless a different system is required by the Client or AHJ. For very large projects, multi-building projects, and very small projects, modifications may be appropriate. The decision to vary from this sheet organization must be approved by your office’s Technical Director.Please note that the intent is not that every project uses every category of sheet identified below. Sheets used in a project will vary by project phase and scope. See below for typical minimum expectations by phase. Deliverables Checklist by Phase(Example drawing types can also be found in SharePoint: LINK)The 'x' marks in the charts below identify the minimum drawing expectations for a 'typical' project.Acknowledging that all projects are unique and have unique requirements, project teams can include more than the minimum, or combine or split drawing information, to convey the project-specific information necessary at each phase. Please review deviations from this list with your Technical Director prior to proceeding.
