x Drawing Order and Types (Example drawings can be found in SharePoint: LINK)The drawing order is based on national standards with refinements intended to respond to our extended practice areas. The basic sheet groups are:1. General Reference (All-Project Data Only) includes reference information that applies to the entire project. Indexes, code compliance and zoning data, andphasing.2. Architectural Reference includes abbreviations, symbols and general notes that pertain to the architectural drawings only.3. Architectural Site includes plans and details of architecturally designed items outside of the building.4. Architectural Demolition includes plans, elevation, sections, and details necessary to describe architectural demolition.5. Architectural Plans typically include floor plans, reflected ceiling plans, finish plans and may include Furniture and Equipment plans when done in-house.6. Exterior Elevations and Building Sections include exterior building elevations, and overall building sections. Interior elevations are in Group 8 with enlarged interior plans.7. Wall Sections and Related Details include exterior wall sections, enlarged partial elevations and exterior envelope details.8. Large Scale Views and Related Details include 1/4"=1’-0” (or larger) enlarged plans of cores, stairs, elevators, toilet rooms, and other areas, along with related interior RCP’s and elevations (optional), as well as stair and elevator plans, sections and details and other details specific to core construction.9. Interior Details include millwork, ceiling, and miscellaneous interior details.10. Schedules and Related Details include partitions, doors, interior finish schedules, and furniture and equipment schedules when done in-house.11. Unassigned Group 1 - General Reference (All-Project Data Only) Discipline Code Sheet Type Dash Sheet Numbers Sheet Title Content Description Cover Sheet Design Perspective, Project Name, Design Phase, Issue Name, Issue Date, Perkins&Will Logo, Volume Number. G 00 - 01 to 99 Index of DrawingsCode Compliance Data Index of Sheets, Location Maps, Project Data (including area and height limitations, construction type, occupancy type), General Notes (that pertain to multiple disciplines). G 01 - 01 to 99 Code Compliance Plans Plans must be scaled such that the entire level fits on a single sheet. If the building configuration allows, multiple levels may be included on a single sheet. G 02 - 01 to 99 Code Compliance Details and Diagrams Code Compliance Details, Zoning Compliance Diagrams (as required by jurisdiction). G 03 - 01 to 99 Phasing Diagrams Avoid subdividing phasing plans into partial area plans. Group 2 - Architectural Reference Discipline Coed Sheet Type Dash Sheet Numbers Sheet Title Content Description A 00 - 01 General Notes, Symbols and Abbreviations Architectural Notes, Symbols and Abbreviations (that apply to all architectural sheet types). Group 3 - Architectural Site Discipline Code Sheet Type Dash Sheet Numbers Sheet Title Content Description A 01 - 01 to 99 Architectural Site Plan For identifying site elements designed by architect and to coordinate scope of site work among disciplines. A 02 - 01 to 99 Architectural Site Details e.g. Fenced or walled enclosures, stairs, ramps, covered walks, fountains, pylon signage, bollards, decorative walls, retaining walls, etc. Do not duplicate Civil or Landscape work. A 03 - 01 to 99 Site Parking Structures e.g. Control gates, control booths, pay stations, directional signage, etc. if designed and specified by Perkins&Will. Group 4 - Architectural Demolition Discipline Code Sheet Type Dash Sheet Numbers Sheet Title Content Description A 04 – 01 to 99 Demolition Plan Level X, [Area X] Do not display new construction on plans. Do not identify new work in notation. A 05 – 01 to 99 Demolition Reflected Ceiling Plan Level X, [Area X] Follow Demolition Plan organization. The preference is to denote demolition of ceilings on Demolition Plans whenever possible. A 06 – 01 to 99 Demolition Elevations and Photos Elevations and annotated photos as required to clearly show scope of demolition work. A 07 – 01 to 99 Demolition Wall Sections Demolition Wall Sections A 08 – 01 to 99 Demolition Details Demolition Details Group 5 - Architectural Plans Discipline Code Sheet Type Dash Sheet Numbers Sheet Title Content Description A 10 – 01 to 99 Overall Floor Plan Level X Overall Floor and Roof Plans. The entire level should be scaled to fit on one sheet. Use as a Key Plan for targeting floor plan ‘Areas’, exterior elevations, and building sections. Use match-lines to subdivide overall plans and identify related A11 through A17 series sheets. The A10 series is not required if each level of the building can fit on one sheet at a minimum 1/8” scale. A 11 – 01 to 99 Floor Plan Level X, [Area X] Floor Plans and/or Floor Plan areas as identified in A10 series. 1/8” scale for plans is preferred. 1/4” plans are acceptable if the entire plan fits a single sheet. A 12 – 01 to 99 Reflected Ceiling Plan Level X, [Area X] Match organization of Floor Plan areas. A 13 – 01 to 99 Finish Plan Level X, [Area X] Match organization of Floor Plan areas. A 14 – 01 to 99 Floor Pattern Plan Level X, [Area X] Use only if the patterns do not read clearly on Finish Plans. Match organization of Floor Plan areas. A 15 – 01 to 99 Furniture Plan Level X, [Area X] Only use Furniture Plans when furniture is in Perkins&Will contract. Match organization of Floor Plan areas.Furniture designed and documented by others should be located in their specific discipline series. A 16 – 01 to 99 Equipment Plan Level X, [Area X] For the identification of fixed and movable equipment. Definition of equipment may vary by practice area. Only use Equipment Plans when equipment is in Perkins&Will contract.Indicate OFCI and CFCI in equipment schedule.Match organization of Floor Plan areas. Equipment designed and documented by others should be located in their specific discipline series. Group 6 – Exterior Elevations and Building Sections Discipline Code Sheet Type Dash Sheet Numbers Sheet Title Content Description A 20 – 01 to 99 Exterior Elevations Scale to match A10 Series plans or A11 series plans when no A10 series is required. A 21 – 01 to 99 Building Sections Scale to match A10 Series plans or A11 series plans when no A10 series is required. Group 7 – Wall Sections and Related Details Discipline Code Sheet Type Dash Sheet Numbers Sheet Title Content Description A 30 – 01 to 99 Enlarged, Partial Building Elevations, Wall Sections [and Partial Plans] Enlarged partial building elevations together with corresponding sections and plans. Use only if required by exterior design complexity. A 31 – 01 to 99 Exterior Wall Sections Wall sections at 1/4” scale that are not in the Enlarged Partial Building Elevations sheets. Use where exterior wall complexity does not warrant A30 series documentation. A 32 – 01 to 10 Typical Exterior Assemblies Typical opaque exterior assemblies. A 32 – 11 to 99 Exterior Envelope Details Typical opaque exterior assembly details as well as door and exterior window head, jamb and sill details. Includes plan and section details targeted on the A30 and A31 series sheets. A 33 – 01 to 99 Exterior Glazing System Types Exterior glazing system elevations. Includes storefront, curtain wall window wall and punched openings. Tag glazing types. Group 8 - Large Scale Views and Related Details Discipline Code Sheet Type Dash Sheet Numbers Sheet Title Content Description A 40 – 01 to 99 Enlarged Core Plans Not required if Floor Plans are at 1/4” scale. A 41 – 01 to 49 Stair X Plans and Sections Stair plans are not required if regular Floor Plans are at 1/4” scale and/or if stairs are part of the Enlarged Core Plans. Only include stair wall sections that are not documented with the Exterior Wall Sections (A30 and A31 series). A 41 – 50 to 99 Stair Details Includes details targeted on the A40 and A41 series sheets. A 42 – 01 to 49 Elevator X [Lift] [Escalator] [Dumbwaiter] Plans and Sections Elevator plans are not required if regular Floor Plans are at 1/4” scale and/or if elevators are included in the Enlarged Core Plans. Only include elevator wall sections that are not documented in with the Exterior Wall Sections (A30 and A31 series). Include all kinds of vertical transportation devices such as lifts, escalators and dumbwaiters in this series. A 42 – 50 to 99 Elevator [Lift] [Escalator] [Dumbwaiter] Details Includes details targeted on the A40 and A42 series sheets. A 43 – 01 to 99 Enlarged Toilet Plans [and Interior Elevations] Include Toilet Accessory tags and legend.Toilet Plans are not required if regular Floor Plans are at 1/4” scale and/or if toilet rooms are a part of the Enlarged Core Plans and/or if the project is using the Plumbing Lay-out system. Include typical mounting height sheets for plumbing fixtures and toilet accessories. A 44 – 01 to 99 Miscellaneous Enlarged Plans, Enlarged Reflected Ceiling Plans [and Interior Elevations] Miscellaneous Interior Enlarged Plans (and Interior Elevations if combined).Not required if Floor Plans at 1/4” scale. Typical plans and RCP’s for repetitious rooms and plans and RCP’s for complex spaces would be documented in this series. A 45 – 01 to 99 Miscellaneous, Interior Elevations Include typical mounting heights and organization of wall mounted devices and equipment. Eliminate if combining 1/4” Floor Plans with 1/4” Interior Elevations. Group 9 - Interior Details Discipline Code Sheet Type Dash Sheet Numbers Sheet Title Content Description A 50 – 01 to 99 Architectural Woodwork Architectural Woodwork enlarged plans, sections and details. A 51 – 01 to 99 Ceiling Details Ceiling Details A 52 – 01 to 99 Miscellaneous Interior Details Include all required interior details not included in the A50 and A51 series. Group 10 - Schedules and Related Details Discipline Code Sheet Type Dash Sheet Numbers Sheet Title Content Description A 60 – 01 to 99 Reserved Reserved A 61 – 01 to 49 Interior Partition Type Charts Interior Partition Type Schedule/Charts. A 61 – 50 to 99 Interior Partition Details Interior Partition Details. Do not duplicate details included in the G02 series. A 62 – 01 to 49 Door Schedule [and Details] Door Schedule with door and frame type designations, legends, notes and glazing legend. Door and frame details may be included here if space allows. A 62 – 50 to 99 Interior Door and Frame Details Interior door and frame details (exterior door details are in the A32 Exterior Details series). A 63 – 01 to 49 Interior Borrowed Lite Types [and Details] Interior borrowed lite type designations, legends, notes and glazing legend. Interior borrowed lite details may be included here if space allows. A 63 – 50 to 99 Interior Borrowed Lite Details Interior borrowed lite details. (Exterior window details are in the A32 Exterior Details series). A 64 – 01 to 99 Interior Finish Schedule and/or Legend Interior Finish Schedule and/or Legend A 65 – 01 to 99 Furniture Schedule Furniture Schedule A 66 – 01 to 99 Equipment Schedule Include equipment mounting heights and responsibilities (OFCI and CFCI). A 67 – 01 to 99 Prefabricated Casework Schedule Coordinate wording with specifications This sheet series is not for Architectural Woodwork. Group 11 - Unassigned Discipline Code Sheet Type Dash Sheet Numbers Sheet Title Content Description A 70 to 79 – 01 to 99 Not assigned A 80 to 89 – 01 to 99 Not assigned A 90 to 99 – 01 to 99 Not assigned Notes on plan sheet numbering:1. Items in brackets [ ] are to be used as appropriate for each project.2. Typically, for all plan groups (A10 through A17), begin sheet numbering at the lowest level of the project whether above or below grade. Plan sheets showing mezzanines and penthouses should be numbered in sequence with all other levels. When plan size permits, multiple plans may be shown on a single sheet.3. For plan groups A11 through A17, if multiple sheets are needed to show an entire level, repeat the sheet number and use letters (except “I” or “O”) as suffixes to distinguish each area of the level. For example, A11-01A is the Partial Floor Plan for the lowest level of the building - Area A and A17–22D is the Partial Equipment Plan for the 22nd sequential level - Area D.4. For projects where an entire floor plan can be drawn at 1/8"=1'-0" scale (or 1/4"=1'-0" if needed) on a single sheet, the A10 sheet series of Overall Plans are not required. For small projects where multiple levels can be drawn on a single sheet, use the appropriate sheet group (A03, A05, A06, A11, A12, etc.) and number the sheets sequentially beginning with 01.5. Identify all plan views with a view title even if there is only one plan per sheet.6. Title the plan sheets to reflect the Level Name and the Plan Area Alpha ID (If it exists) that are shown on that sheet.7. On plan sheets, it may be desirable to replicate the floor number within each discipline with the sheet number. For example, sheet A10-01 would become the Overall Level 01 Floor Plan and A12-22 would become the Level 22 Reflected Ceiling Plan. This system may become cumbersome when basements and mezzanines or split-level plans are involved. Evaluate each project carefully before deciding to implement this option.8. For projects with levels below grade begin the plan sheets with the lowest level.9. Plans in all plan groups (A11 through A16) should be at the same scale, the same orientation, and the same position on the sheet, and should have consistent sheet numbering. For example, A11–22B shows the same plan area as A12–22B and A16–22B.10. Partial roof levels should not be identified separately but should be shown with the floor that they are concurrent with or immediately below.11. Phasing Plans (sheet group G03) should be numbered in a way that makes sense for the project. For example, if all the phasing occurs on one level, then G03-01 could represent Phase 1, G03-02 could represent Phase 2, etc. When multiple levels are part of multiple phases, follow the standard numbering system for plans (as defined above). This determination should be made such that the drawing organization conveys the most important information in the clearest manner.12. Sheets that are not a part of the contract (such as surveys) should not include the Perkins&Will title block and do not have to follow Perkins&Will standards.13. The window and door schedules shall be separate butmay be on the same sheet for small projects.14. If a door has sidelights, include them in the door schedule and not in the window schedule.Drawing TitlesIdentify each Plan, Elevation, Section and Detail drawing on a sheet with a title that includes:· The descriptive name of the drawing (e.g., “LEVEL 01 FLOOR PLAN,” “NORTH ELEVATION,” “EXTERIOR WALL SECTION,” “DOOR JAMB DETAIL,” etc.)· A number based on the appropriate diagram below· The scale of the drawing (e.g., 1/8? = 1’-0“, 1/4“ = 1’-0“, 1:50, etc.)Avoid abbreviated titles.All titles should be left-justifiedto the drawing that they name.Drawing Title NumberingUse the following numbering pattern for all drawings. https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/XJJSVoGYI7VWfAQ auto false 14px

title: Types of Drawings UK
content: There are seven basic types of drawings: plans, elevations, sections, details, 3D views, schedules, and diagrams. All Perkins&Will drawings fall within one of these categories. Not all drawing types will be required by every project. Refer to the QA checklist templates in Microsoft Word for information on Perkins&Will expectations for each drawing type.PlansPlans make up the largest category of drawings in a typical set. The purpose of all plans is to convey information on a horizontal plane. Note that floor plans are typically cut at 4’-0”(1220 mm) above the finished floor and should show information at that elevation and below. If there are features that are above that elevation, make sure that they appear in another drawing (the reflected ceiling plan, for instance) or are clearly identified as “above.”Code Compliance PlansPlans that demonstrate how the design complies with the applicable codes, particularly regarding life-safety issues. These plans usually use the Architectural Site Plan and the Overall Floor Plans as backgrounds, at the same scale.Architectural Site PlansThese plans show the organization of the major site elements, which may include curbs, paving, site walls, site structures, planting areas, site utilities, and site lighting and include the entire project site, showing all major architectural and site elements. Coordinate with other disciplines, particularly Civil (to locate the building) and Landscape, to avoid duplication or omission of information. Information that is typically shown on civil, landscape or electrical drawings includes grade and paving elevations, horizontal locations of site elements, detailed planting layouts and site utility information. The scale depends on the sheet size and the actual dimensions of the site, and should be the same as the civil plans; for most projects, the scale should be no smaller than 1”=20’-0” (1:200). If, at this scale, the site is too large to fit on one sheet, it can be broken up for multiple sheets; the break lines should be in the same location(s) as on the civil plans. Note that the architectural site plan should only be included in the CD phase when it includes work not covered by the civil or landscape scope, which is atypical for our work.Enlarged Architectural Site PlansThe plans that show detailed parts of the site. The scale will depend on the nature of the design; for most projects, 1/8”=1’-0” (1:100) or 1/4'=1’-0” (1:50) will suffice. These plans should have the same orientation as the Architectural Site Plan. Note that enlarged architectural site plans should only be included in the CD phase when it includes work not covered by the civil or landscape scope, which is atypical for our work.Overall Floor PlansIf, at 1/8”=1’-0” (1:100) or 1/4"=1’-0” (1:50), the entire plan of the largest floor plate cannot fit comfortably on a single drawing sheet (allowing enough space for column bubbles, dimensions, etc.), then a separate series of plans—the Overall Floor Plans—is needed. The scale of the Overall Floor Plans will depend on the sheet size and the actual dimensions of the largest floor plate; for most projects, 1/32”=1’-0” (1:400) or 1/16”=1’-0” (1:200) will suffice.Floor PlansThe plans that show every part of the project at either1/8”=1’-0” (1:100) or 1/4"=1’-0” (1:50). (Note: For Schematic Design, the Floor Plans should be at a scale that allows each floor to be shown entirely on one sheet. If that results in a scale smaller than 1/8”=1’-0” (1:100), then only Overall Floor Plans (see Overall Floor Plans above) should be issued for Schematic Design, with the larger scale (1/8”=1’-0”or1/4"=1’-0” (1:100 or 1:50)) Floor Plans being started in Design Development.Demolition PlansThe plans that show the extent of demolition for each floor, typically at the same scale as the Floor Plans (and at the same location on the sheet).Roof PlansPlans showing each level of roof in a building. Roof Plans should be at the same scale as the Floor Plans (and at the same location on the sheet). Where a roof is at (approximately) the same level as a floor, the Roof Plan for that area should be incorporated into that Floor Plan.Enlarged Partial PlansUnless the Floor Plans are at 1/4"=1’-0” (1:50), areas that are too detailed or complex to be clearly shown on the Floor Plans should be enlarged to not less than 1/4"=1’-0” (1:50). These areas can include toilet rooms, nurse stations, patient rooms, stairs, elevators, and lobbies. The orientation of the Enlarged Plans should be the same as the Floor Plans.Reflected Ceiling Plans (RCPs)Plans showing all ceiling areas of the project, at the same scale as the Floor Plans (and at the same location on the sheet).Finish PlansPlans showing floor finishes, floor patterns, and room finish tags. If the wall, floor and base finishes can be completely described using room finish tags on the Floor Plans, or using a finish schedule, then Finish Plans are not necessary. The Finish Plans should be at the same scale as the Floor Plans, unless a larger scale is necessary in some areas for clarity, and at the same location on the sheet.Furniture, Fixtures and Equipment (FF&E) PlansPlans showing loose furniture and equipment (but not specialty equipment, such as medical, laboratory, kitchen or fitness). The FF&E Plans should be at the same scale as the Floor Plans, unless a larger scale is necessary in some areas for clarity, and at the same location on the sheet.Equipment PlansPlans showing program-driven equipment, such as laboratory, medical, kitchen and fitness equipment. Building systems, such as mechanical, plumbing, electrical or fire protection, should not be included. If the equipment plans are prepared by either Perkins&Will’s or the Client’s consultant, those sheets should be designated with a “Q” prefix, instead of an “A.” The Equipment Plans should be at the same scale as the Floor Plans, unless a larger scale is necessary in some areas for clarity, and at the same location on the sheet.All specialty equipment falls in one of the following groups:· Group 1: Architecturally significant equipment (i.e., it takes up a significant amount of floor space or requires a significant amount of clearance around it) that has utility requirements (electrical, water, gas, drainage, etc.). Examples: refrigerators, cage wash machines, sterilizers, blanket warmers, ceiling- mounted exam lights, booms, and MRI and CT machines.· Group 2: Architecturally significant equipment that has no utility requirements. Examples: patient beds (because they take up space and have clearance requirements), wall-mounted blood pressure monitors (because they need blocking in the wall), stretchers, linen carts (because they take up space), and laboratory benches.· Group 3: Equipment located on countertops and requiring utility connections. Examples: microwave ovens, ice machines.Power and Communication (Utility) CoordinationPlansPlans showing locations of power and telecommunication outlets. Note: Power and Communication (Utility) Coordination Plans should not be included in the drawing set unless there is specific Perkins&Will information (e.g. device location) information that is added. Consultant coordination is critical to the use of Power and Communication (Utility) Coordination Plans because the majority of the information included comes directly from our consultants. The Power and Communication(Utility) Coordination Plans should be at the same scale as the Floor Plans, unless a larger scale is necessary in some areas for clarity, and at the same location on the sheet.Slab Edge PlansThese architectural plans may be used for internal coordination purposes with the structural engineer to give dimensions to all slab edges, at the building perimeter and floor openings; to all slab depressions; and to all raised concrete pads and curbs. For the construction drawings, this information should be shown in the structuraldrawings. Slab edge plans should not be issued as architectural sheets in the Contract Documents.ElevationsThe purpose of elevations is to show information on exterior and interior vertical surfaces.Overall Exterior ElevationsThe series of exterior elevations that shows every building façade. For most projects, the scale should not be the less than 1/16”=1’-0“ (1:100). To accommodate this, some elevations may need to be broken into two or more segments on a sheet, with match lines connecting them.Enlarged Exterior ElevationsAll typical and non-typical portions of the exterior wall should be represented by an Enlarged Building Elevation, at1/4"=1’-0” (1:50). These elevations can be composed on a sheet adjacent to the related 1/4"=1’-0” (1:50) scale wall sections and above the related 1/4"=1’-0” (1:50) when this arrangement is beneficial for explaining complex 3D building conditions.Interior ElevationsElevations of interior walls. Only show elevations of walls with elements that can be identified, dimensioned or described only on an elevation and that are not shown elsewhere in the drawings. Interior elevations should generally be drawn at 1/4”=1’-0” (1:50) scale, unless a larger scale is necessary for clarity.Interior elevations are needed to show design elements other than doors and windows (they are covered inschedules), outlets (unless a precise dimension is required), standard wall bases (described under room finishes), wall finishes (unless the extent of a material can only be made clear in elevation), and loose equipment (covered in schedules).SectionsThe purpose of sections is to show the vertical relationships of floors, spaces, materials and building systems.Overall Building SectionsThese sections cut through the entire building; longitudinal sections cut through the longer building dimension, transverse sections cut through the shorter dimension. Make section cuts that show as many major building features as possible: atriums, high-volume spaces, changes in grade, changes in floor or elevations, and connections to existing buildings. The scale should be the same as the Overall Exterior Elevations; if breaks are needed to fit the section on one sheet, they should be located in the same places as on the elevations.Wall SectionsThese are sections cut through the building’s exterior walls. Generally, wall sections extend through the full height of an exterior wall, with break lines as needed, although partial-height wall sections can also be used. Thescale should be no smaller than 1/4”=1’-0” (1:50) and no larger than 3/4”=1’-0” (1:15). When the wall sections are at the same scale as the Enlarged Elevations they should be shown on the same sheet as, and adjacent to, the relevant Enlarged Elevations and/or plans.Vertical Transportation (Stair, Elevator and Escalator) SectionsThese sections are cut through the stair, escalator and elevator enclosures and extend the entire height of the enclosure, including machine rooms, pits and overruns, using break lines to show the top and bottom of the stair run or hoistway, and a typical intermediate portion. They also include unenclosed or monumental stairs. The scale should be 1/4"=1’-0” (1:50).Partial SectionsThere are often instances where the only way to show a condition on the project is by drawing a partial section at that condition. Partial sections are usually used as a coordination tool, showing conditions such as tight above- ceiling spaces. The scale for partial sections should be whatever is appropriate to illustrate the conditions, but it shouldn’t be smaller than 1/4"=1’-0” (1:50) or larger than 1”=1’-0” (1:10). Partial sections should provide the same information as wall sections.DetailsDetails are large-scale drawings that show how materials and systems are put together. For all types of detail drawings, you should group related details on a sheet to make the building systems as understandable as possible. The positions of related details on a sheet should correspond to their relationships with each other in the actual building.Details are typically drawn at 1 1/2"=1’-0” (1:10) or 3”=1’-0” (1:5), depending on the complexity of the detail.Show only enough detail to sufficiently describe the design intent. Don’t show information, such as screw threads or aluminum mullion extrusion details, that is more appropriate for shop drawings or fabrication drawings.LegendsLegends have two distinct forms in Perkins&Will drawings: graphic and tabular.Graphic legends appear on drawing sheets to define the meaning of graphic symbols that appear on that sheet. This could include tags, drawing components, line types, hatches, colors and combinations of these. Many drawing sheets in Firmwide and local templates have graphic legends included. It is the team's responsibility to make sure that the graphic symbols in the legend match those used in the drawing,Tabular legends are a matrix format of verbal and numeric information used to identify materials, systems, components or other items that are annotated in the drawings. Note that tabular legends do not list each instance of a material, system, component or other item, they list each type of material, system, component or other item. For example, a Finish Legend would list the paint with designation PNT-1 only once even when it is used multiple times in a project.SchedulesThe purpose of schedules is to show, in a matrix format, defining information about, and location of, systems and components. While much of the information on schedules could be shown graphically, a schedule is the preferred method of documentation when the information is presented more efficiently and is more easily understood.Schedules are used to define work by space and list each instance. rather than each type. For example, every door would appear on the Door Schedule even when multiple doors are identical.For standard templates for all schedules, see the Digital Practice Website for specific Revit instructions.Partition ScheduleA graphic representation of each type of interior partition construction, with a matrix identifying the variations within each partition type. The Perkins&Will Partition Type Schedule should be used.Door ScheduleA matrix identifying the dimensions, materials, finishes, detail references, fire resistance rating, glazing materials and hardware for each door on the project. The Perkins&Will Door Schedule template should be used. Refer to the Digital Practice Website for specific Revit instructions.Window ScheduleWindow schedules identify the materials, dimensions, and details for the exterior and interior windows and borrowed lights. The project team can select from two window schedule formats: matrix or graphic.· Matrix Window Schedule:A matrix identifying the dimensions, materials, finishes, fire resistance ratings, glazing materials and detail references for each exterior and interior window and borrowed light in the project. Transoms and sidelights that are integral with door frames are scheduled on the Door Schedule· Graphic Window Schedule:Elevations of each window/borrowed light type, with dimensions and detail targets.Room Finish ScheduleA matrix identifying the wall, floor, ceiling, wall base, and, in some cases, millwork or casework materials and finishes. A Room Finish Schedule may not be needed for every project if room finish tags on the floor plans can adequately describe the finishes. The Perkins&Will Room Finish Schedule template should be used, see the Digital Practice Website for specific Revit instructions.Casework ScheduleElevations of each type of casework with dimensions and detail targets. The Perkins&Will standard casework system should be utilized. See the Digital Practice Website and MicroKnowledge card for more information.Equipment ScheduleA matrix identifying the dimensions, weight, mounting conditions, and power and water requirements for Group 1, Group 2 and Group 3 equipment (see above Equipment Plans).Signage ScheduleA matrix identifying the dimensions, materials, finishes, and message for each interior and exterior sign, including those required for life safety, on the project.DiagramsDiagrams are graphic illustrations, simplified so that only the pertinent information is shown. Diagrams are used most often by the engineering disciplines (e.g., mechanical, plumbing and riser diagrams; electrical one-line diagrams). However, there are a few typical types of Architectural and Interiors diagrams.Zoning Compliance DiagramsDiagrams, in plan and/or section, showing the land use limitations imposed on the site by the local jurisdiction.Code Compliance DiagramsGraphic representations illustrating how the design complies with the applicable codes, particularly regarding life safety issues. These diagrams should be on the same sheets as the related Code Compliance Plans, but do not have to be drawn to scale. They are used to supplement the Code Compliance Plans.Accessibility DiagramsA summary of the applicable accessibility requirements (ADAAG, ICC A117.1, and/or local accessibility code as required by the AHJ) for the project.Phasing DiagramsDiagrammatic plans showing the order in which portions of the project are to be completed. These diagrams can also illustrate the intended sequence of how portions of the project will be occupied. They are not the same as construction sequencing plans, which are the contractor’s responsibility. Depending on the level of detail needed, Phasing Diagrams can use the Architectural Site Plan or the Overall Floor Plans as backgrounds or can be diagrams not drawn to scale.Three-Dimensional ViewsTwo-dimensional views are the primary means of graphically communicating information in the Contract Documents. Three-dimensional views should be used whenever they can provide additional information or clarity. It can be helpful, particularly with complex building designs, to include 3-dimensional views of a building, space, or detail in the design and construction drawings. These drawings can make it easier for consultants, cost estimators and contractors to visualize the project and understand the design intent. If 3-D views are included in the construction drawings, they must be generated from the Revit model and not constructed independently. The views should be labeled to identify what is being viewed, and they should be targeted on plans or elevations. Notations and dimensions should be provided where possible.

title: Drawing Standards UK
content: Drawing Issuance and RevisionsContractual Issuances of Sheets:When sheets are issued to the outside world (as opposed to internal coordination, interdisciplinary team coordination, check sets, etc.), it is for one of these four contractually defined reasons: client approvals, regulatory approvals, bidding/pricing, or construction.Client approval issuances are typically identified in the Owner-Architect Agreement as deliverables, such as “schematic design” or “design development.” In most cases, changes on drawings issued for client approval are not clouded. These issuances are included in the revision block during the design phases, but are removed from the revision block when the sheet is first issued for regulatory approval, bidding/pricing or construction.Regulatory approvals are issued to authorities having jurisdiction (AHJ). The first issuance of sheets to a regulatory agency is typically unclouded, but listed in the revision table; subsequent issues are clouded and listed in the table.Bidding/pricing issuances would include any instance that the sheets are issued for the development of formal constructor price (e.g. GMP) or bid, including addenda.Construction issuances include the initial “issued for construction,” if required by contract, as well as contractually identified revisions (Architect’s Supplemental Instructions, Construction Change Directives, or Change Orders—see 07-Construction Contract Administration).Naming Drawing Issuances:Drawing issuances during the design phase should match the contractual language in the Owner-Architect Agreement (e.g. Design Development, Stage 4 Tender). Percentage complete should not be used in issuances (e.g. 50% Design Development, 100%Construction Documents) unless specifically required by the contract. The use of percentages can be misleading and arbitrary, as it is impossible to accurately determine percentage completion of a document set, and it gives the false impression that a "100%" set is perfectly complete. Instead, issuances should include terms like "Mid-Phase", "In Progress" or similar.Drawing issuances for regulatory approvals, bidding/pricing and construction should be named to reflect all intended uses. So, if a set is being issued for both bidding and permit, it should be named "Bid and Permit."Drawing issuances during the construction phase should be named to reflect the method of issuance; ASI 04 or CCD 01.Identifying Drawing Revisions:There are three basic elements in identifying revisions: clouds (or bubbles) around each change in a drawing, a “delta” identifier for each change, and the revision table within the title block.Clouds:Changes must be clouded for any sheet revisions issued to an AHJ, to bidders during the bidding period, and to the contractor during the construction phase.When a change needs to be identified on a sheet, the part of the sheet that has been changed must be completely surrounded by a cloud. Do not include unchanged parts of a sheet within a cloud. If the changes to a sheet are so extensive that very little would not be clouded, then a note should be added under the Sheet Title saying, “ENTIRE SHEET REVISED,” surrounded by a cloud. If the sheet has been newly added to the set, the sheet title and number should be clouded. All changes that appear in the drawings, including spelling corrections, relocated dimensions strings, etc., no matter how insignificant, must be clouded. When the sheet is next issued, the clouds from the preceding issue are to be removed.Deltas:All clouded changes on a sheet are to be identified by a number that reflects the number of times the sheet has been contractually issued. The issue number is to be surrounded by a triangular delta symbol, with a leader connecting the delta to one or more clouds. Typically, deltas, with their issue numbers, remain on the sheet for all subsequent issues, while the clouds and their leaders are removed.Revision Table:The standard Perkins+Will sheet layout includes a revision table in the lower right-hand corner of the sheet. All contractually required issuances of each sheet to the owner, contractor, or AHJ need to be recorded in the revision table, starting with the earliest at the bottom of the table. These official issuances are to be reflected in the delta numbers. The issuance number and the date of the issuance are to be recorded in the table. Each entry in the revision table should reflect the history of the individual sheet, not the entire set of sheets. As a result, sheets issued for the same revision may have different delta numbers.Dates in the revision table will be expressed as DDMMMYY, so 1 January 2024 would be written as “01JAN24.” Room Numbering GuidelinesThe room numbering system is intended to simplify finding rooms on a plan. Use the following guidelines when developing the room numbering strategy for your project:1. Use as few characters as possible to clearly identify each room or space.2. Number all rooms and spaces, including corridors, alcoves, atriums, stairs, stair vestibules, etc.3. If there is a series of primary programmatic spaces, such as classrooms, patient rooms or laboratories, start the sequential numbering with them.4. If there is a small accessory room, such as patient toilets or storage rooms, associated with each of the primary programmatic spaces, apply an “A,” “B,” etc., suffix to the room number.5. The first digit(s) of the room number identifies the floor level. If the project has more than nine floor levels, room numbers for Levels 01 to 09 should start with “0” (i.e., 0101, 0102, etc. at Level 01).6. If any level on a project contains more than 90 rooms, use four- or five- digit numbers with the first digit (or first two digits) still being the level number. For example, for a building with less than ten levels, 1001, 1002, etc. For a building with ten or more levels, 01001, 01002, etc.7. In projects with multiple buildings, a prefix (letters or numbers) identifies the building name or number. For example, AD101 could be room 101 in the Administration Building and CL101 could be room 101 (1st level room) in the Classroom Building.8. Stairs (exit, communicating and ornamental) get stair numbers (ST- ), not room numbers.9. Elevators (including dumbwaiters, food service lifts, blood lifts) get elevator numbers (ELEV- ), not room numbers.10. Room numbers on the drawings are for use during design and construction. The permanent numbers that the client uses when the space is occupied will be determined in conjunction with the client, usually when the signage program is developed. Final room numbers need to be coordinated with the building automation system and the fire department command panels.11. If the client or project has an existing room numbering system, use it.12. Number rooms in a consistent direction (right-to-left, clockwise, etc.).13. Where floors with similar room layouts are stacked, give stacked rooms similar numbers. For example, if the 3rd and 4th floors of a patient bed tower have the same floor plans, then the patient room on Level 4 that is immediately above Patient Room 304 would be called Patient Room 404.Planning/Structural GridsPlan drawings shall include a planning or structural grid.The grid shall be developed based upon columns, bearing walls, building or site geometry or simply a logical planning grid on a selected spacing when that is appropriate to the project,The grid is a planning and coordination tool during design, a location aid to the Contractor during construction and a means to relate different views to each other in the drawings (e.g. Elevation and Section views to the Plans, Consultant drawings to the Architectural drawings). it is, therefore, critical that a grid be established as early in the design process as possible.For typical new construction and addition projects, grid identifiers shall:Be a circle with an alpha-numeric characterBe alphabetic for east to west grids and numeric for north to south gridsBegin with A for lettered grids and 1 for numbered gridsStart at the northwestern-most portion of the building or addition, at whichever level that occursNote that this system can be modified to accommodate geometrically complexity, multi-building or multi-phase projects and other project-specific conditions that arise.For renovation or adaptive reuse work the grid identifiers shall:Be a hexagon with an alpha-numeric characterFollow the pattern and numbering/lettering of the existing buildingDoor Identification on PlansEach door and cased opening shall be individually identified on the A11 series of floor plans, using the method included in the sample diagrams below. Do not tag existing doors unless some change is being made to the door, frame or hardware as part of the project. A pair of doors in a single frame receives one door tag. Doors are identified by the room number for which the door provides exiting. https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/NeBkIqEIbnUdtvu auto false Example: Room with multiple doors 14px https://files.hub.perkinswill.com/download/SQCL3PDVbcfhlRu auto false Example: Room with one door 14px All doors are labeled sequentially in each room. The tags include the room number followed by a letter designation, beginning with A at the first door. Do not use spaces, dashes or hyphens between the room numbers and the door designations. To identify the first door (and the subsequent door order), start at the north end of the east wall and move in a clockwise direction.Dimensioning StandardsDimensions are required to define sizes and locations of building elements and their relationships to each other. The Perkins&Will General Notes state that the drawings are not to be scaled. Dimensions found by querying the building information model are not an acceptable substitute for writing dimensions on the drawings. The dimensions written on the drawings are critical to communicating the building’s geometry to clients, consultants and contractors.General Dimensioning StandardsFor legibility in plans, the dimensioning scheme should start with the largest overall dimensions and proceed to the smallest dimensions. The following are general principles for dimensioning:1. Dimension to the ¼ inch (5mm) or greater. Dimensions less than ¼ inch (5mm) are almost always inappropriate and should only be used when circumstances necessitate (e.g., vertical brick dimensions, orthogonal dimensions of non-orthogonal elements, large-scale detail drawings of precision systems like curtain wall). Never dimension to 1/16” or less. Try to use whole-inch numbers whenever possible. Other than for large-scale (1 ½”=1’-0” or 3”=1’-0”) details, a general rule is if you are getting dimensions to 1/8” or 1/16”, you are probably dimensioning to the wrong thing or have modeled incorrectly.2. When setting column grid dimensions at the beginning of a project, you should try to organize the column grid so they are spaced on whole inch dimensions.3. Do not use decimal Imperial dimensions on architectural or interiors drawings (e.g., 4.33 feet or 5.5 inches).4. Metric dimensions are always given in millimeters and do not require the mm designation.5. Leave dimension strings “open” to accommodate construction tolerances and the differences between actual and nominal dimensions. On an open dimension string, the dimension string is tied to only one fixed point, such as a column grid line. Locate the open end of the string in the least critical area. Make sure that all required minimum clearances (corridor widths, ADA clearances, etc.) are specifically dimensioned.6. The term “equal” in lieu of a numeric dimension is appropriate where components are evenly spaced between two located fixed points. “Equal” can also be used to dimension both ends of an open dimension string.7. The following dimensions are to be placed between the building footprint and the column bubbles:1. Overall building dimensions2. Structural bay dimensions, including dimensions from the outermost column lines to exterior faces of the building. These dimensions should add up to the overall building dimension.3. Grid dimensions, where column lines are not available.4. Exterior envelope offsets and openings8. The following dimensions are to be placed inside the building footprint:1. Interior partition location dimensions2. Slab opening, curb and floor depression dimensions (unless shown elsewhere, such as on structural drawings)3. Critical clear dimensions, such as corridor and stair widths4. Dimension to the centerlines of plumbing and light fixtures. Place dimensions within the building footprint.9. Do not use indefinite terms such as “NTS” (not to scale), “+/-,” and “TYP.”10. Express dimensions less than one foot in inches (11 ½”, not 0’-11 ½”), and over one foot as feet and inches (1’-3 ¼”). Express dimensions less than one inch in fractions of an inch (½”, not 0’-½”).11. Where elements of manufactured components, such as steel shapes or AWI (American Woodwork Institute) casework, are identified with manufacturer or standards organization nomenclature that includes the size, do not provide additional dimensions.12. Alignment notation in lieu of dimensions may be used where applicable (“Align with center of mullion,” “align with face of column,” etc.)13. Use the note “Verify in Field” (“VIF”) only where dimensions of existing conditions are not precisely known.Dimensioning on Plans (Horizontal Dimensions)1. Use the planning grid for placing dimensions. Tie each dimension string to only one grid line.2. Dimension all exterior elements, including fenestration, openings, changes in plane and major building features.3. Exterior building dimension strings should follow the following hierarchy:1. The outermost dimension string is the overall building dimension.2. The next dimension string is the spacing of column grid lines.3. The innermost dimension string(s) dimension exterior building elements, such as windows, doors, and changes in wall plane, to the nearest column grid line. When the exterior finish material is CMU, brick, or stone, these openings should be denoted with “MO” for masonry opening.4. Use building grid line intersections to locate the building relative to the site property boundaries on the Civil sheets.5. Horizontal dimensions should generally appear only on plan drawings.6. Building grid dimensions should be complete and continuous between outermost grid lines.7. For modifications to or within existing buildings, where grid centerlines may be unavailable, critical dimensions should be tied to existing fixed building elements with known locations, e.g., inside face of exterior wall, face of column enclosure, or face of core wall.8. Unless noted otherwise, dimensions to partitions should be from face to face of gypsum board or CMU, excluding applied finishes or panels.9. Do not dimension to both sides of a partition (i.e., don’t dimension partition thicknesses).10. Where clear or minimum dimensions are required for building code, zoning, or accessibility compliance, such as hallway or corridor widths and dimensions related to furniture systems, dimension to the face of applied finish rather than to the face of gypsum board or CMU. Add a note to the dimension saying that it is to face of finish. Examples:1. If the code requires a corridor to be a minimum of eight feet wide, the dimension should read, “8’-0” Clear Face of Finish.”2. For accessibility compliance, dimensions from lavatory centerlines to adjacent partitions should read, “1’-6” to Face of Finish.”11. Doors can be dimensioned in any of the following ways, depending on what is critical for ADA compliance or design:12. If ADA clearance is critical, dimension from strike face of the jamb to the closest obstruction (partition, equipment, etc.).13. If the door is located in a long wall and ADA clearances do not control the door location, dimension from the closest grid line or partition to outside edge of one jamb. If, and only if, the door needs to be centered on some other element, dimension to the centerline of the door assembly.14. If the jamb detail of the door provides the dimension between the jamb and the adjacent partition, no other dimension on the plans is needed.15. Enlarged stair plans should show the following dimensions: total dimension of each run of treads (e.g., 8T@11”=7’-4”), width and length of landings, and handrail extensions.Dimensioning on Elevations and Sections (Vertical Dimensions)1. Identify floor levels with elevation datum tags and tie all vertical dimensions to floor datum elevations. Provide datum elevations at the tops of major building elements.2. Assign Level 01 elevation datum as 100’-0”. Only once—in the General Notes—should the actual Level 01 USGS (United States Geological Survey) datum elevation be identified. Actual USGS datum elevations are only to be used on civil drawings.3. Datum elevations shall be written in architectural style—feet and inches (i.e., 100’-0”, not 100.00?).4. Vertical dimension strings should be placed to the right of the elevation or section.5. Tie all vertical dimensions to the nearest floor level.6. Horizontal dimensions should generally not be shown on elevations or sections.1. Exception 1: If there is no practical way of showing them on a plan (e.g., control joints in walls). Horizontal spacing of control joints may not be needed when they are shown as being aligned with other building elements (e.g., the jamb of a window or door).2. Exception 2: On wall sections, provide the horizontal dimensions between the column grid and the exterior face of wall and major protrusions (canopies, “eyebrows,” etc.).Dimensioning on Details (Horizontal and Vertical Dimensions)1. Provide the datum elevations for floor level, top of steel at roof, and top of masonry at parapet when in the view of the section detail.2. Tie dimensions to the nearest grid line or a floor level datum.3. For items that are specified by product number, do not provide size dimensions on the drawings of the product. But draw the item to scale.4. Dimension the top or bottom of horizontal reveals, projections, depressions and changes of exterior veneer material from the nearest floor level.Notation GuidelinesGeneral Notes:The Perkins&Will standard General Notes are to be included with every set of drawings issued. The intent is that General Notes be consistent from project to project. The General Notes clarify issues that apply to the architecture and interiors drawings and are not covered in the Project Manual. Delete General Notes that do not apply to your project. If a General Note is added or modified, the change should be reviewed with the specifications writer and your Studio’s Technical Director. Notes that are specific to individual sheets or groups of sheets should be located on those sheets rather than the General Notes sheet.The General Notes should be located in the A series of sheets (see Drawing Order and Types).Sheet Group NotesSheet Group Notes apply to all sheets within a group of drawings (e.g., the A10 sheets) or a sequence of related sheets within a group (e.g., A12–20 to A12–29). Sheet Group Notes are placed on the first sheet of the sequence and clearly note the sequence of sheets to which they apply.Sheet NotesSheet Notes clarify information on an individual sheet.Reference and Target Notes1. Generally avoid notes that refer to another discipline's sheets (like "See Structural".) Most project General Conditions will include a complementarity provision that makes such notes unnecessary, like AIA A201 1.2.1: "...The Contract Documents are complementary, and what is required by one shall be as binding as if required by all..." An exception is for complex conditions or those that require extraordinary multi-disciplinary coordination to achieve a specific design or performance result. In these cases, it is imperative that the project team verify that the coordinated work is properly documented in the other discipline's documents. In these cases, it may be helpful to reference specific drawings or sheets for clarity (like "See 1/S20-02".) The specific language used will be determined by the Project Architect/Designer.2. Take care when using terms such as “TYP,” “OPP HD,” or “SIM” on targets.3. “TYP” should mean that the referenced drawing applies to all similar-looking conditions, and that a person not familiar with the design intent would readily understand that the similar-looking conditions are covered by the “typical” target.4. “OPH” means that the targeted drawing is identical to the referenced drawing in every way except that it is a mirror-image, reversed version of the detail as drawn.5. “SIM” means that the referenced drawing is essentially the same as the targeted condition; the differences between them must be insignificant. Two details might have minor dimensional or finish material differences that are too dissimilar for “SIM” to apply, yet are too similar to warrant two separate drawings. In that case, draw the basic detail, add the variations to the detail, and designate the variations by letters after the target number. In this way, a single detail drawing can use multiple numbers to reference more than one condition: 4/A11-51 and 4A/A11-51, as an example. The differences between the two versions of the detail must be noted in the detail drawing.AbbreviationsWith the use of BIM, and keynotes, the difference in time between typing a complete word and an abbreviation is trivial, while the potential for confusion is high. Therefore, the use of abbreviations is not encouraged and should be avoided.The following guidelines are to be used in determining legitimate instances where abbreviations are appropriate:1. When the full room name will not comfortably fit within the room on a plan, use an abbreviation, provided it is on the abbreviations list. Examples: DEPT for DEPARTMENT, EMER for EMERGENCY, HSKPG for HOUSEKEEPING. If a room name needs to be abbreviated and is not on the standard abbreviation list, customize the list with project-specific abbreviations.2. Abbreviations that are commonly understood throughout the construction industry are generally acceptable. Examples: T/SLAB for TOP OF SLAB, AFF for ABOVE FINISH FLOOR, HP for HIGH POINT.Do not use periods, commas, or other punctuation for abbreviations.Abbreviations should never be used in general, sheet series or sheet notes, or in specifications.Consultants’ General NotesPerkins&Will is responsible for the content of our consultants’ drawings, including their General Notes, which are often boilerplate notes that have not been tailored for your project. It is essential that the project architect read every general note on consultants’ sheets.The following notes and information should be avoided:1. Notes that say “The Contractor shall do [something],” or “The Contractor shall comply with [something],” or similar words. The Contract Documents as a whole are intended to address the contractor. The notes should simply say “Do [something]” or “Comply with [something].”2. Notes that refer to another discipline's sheets (like "See Architectural".) Most project General Conditions will include a complementarity provision that makes such notes unnecessary, like AIA A201 1.2.1: "...The Contract Documents are complementary, and what is required by one shall be as binding as if required by all..." An exception is for complex conditions or those that require extraordinary multi-disciplinary coordination to achieve a specific design or performance result. In these cases, it is imperative that the project team verify that the coordinated work is properly documented in the other discipline's documents. In these cases, it may be helpful to reference specific drawings or sheets for clarity (like "See 1/A32-02".) The specific language used will be determined by the Project Architect/Designer and coordinated with the consultants.3. Notes related to the bidding process, such as “Include in this bid all premium time …” Information such as this should be in the Instructions to Bidders.4. Notes related to construction safety, such as “These drawings and specifications do not include necessary components for construction safety.”5. Notes that should be in the specifications: “Equipment shall be provided with a permanent label affixed to the equipment …”6. Notes that say that something is “NIC” (not in contract) or “by others.”Room Finish DesignatorsWhen identifying the colors and finishes of interior wall, ceiling, flooring, wall base, and casework materials, use Room Finish Designators. A Room Finish Designator consists of a two- to four-character Identifier (see list of identifiers below) and a Number, connected by a hyphen. These designators can be used on Finish Plans, Room Finish Schedules, Finish Legends, or Room Finish Tags (see Revit Guidelines)APC Acoustical Panel CeilingCPT CarpetCT Ceramic TileCMU Concrete Masonry UnitEXP Exposed ConstructionFWP Fabric Wrapped PanelPT Porcelain TilePNT PaintPLAM Plastic LaminateQT Quarry TileRB Resilient BaseRF Resinous FlooringRSF Resilient Sheet FlooringRTF Resilient Tile FloorSSM Solid Surface MaterialSST Stainless SteelST Stone TileTER TerrazzoWC Wall coveringWF Wood FlooringA drawing set that uses these designators must include a legend that defines each designator. The legend must include the specification section that covers each material, the material color and a brief description.Do not provide the names of manufacturers or specific model numbers on architectural sheets. This information will be included in the specifications. The following are the only exceptions to this rule:1. Equipment manufacturers and model numbers in the Equipment Schedules2. Manufacturers and product numbers for finish materials where the Finish Legend included in the drawing set is used exclusively for this information and it is not included in the specifications.Accessory TagsWhen identifying accessories, use Accessory Tags. An Accessory Tag consists of a two- to four-character Identifier and a number, connected by a hyphen and enclosed by a rectangle. These designators can be used for Floor Plans and Interior Elevations.Each sheet that uses designators must include a legend that defines each designator. The legend must include the specification section that covers each material.Coordination Between Drawings and SpecificationsNotations (i.e., notes and keynotes) are required on drawings to identify components, products, materials, instructions, or supplemental information. They should be succinct, limited in content to essential information, and coordinated with the specifications. By using project-specific keynotes, the project specifier and the project architect will maximize coordination between drawings and specifications.KeynotesPerkins&Will uses a keynoting system as part of our drawing annotation process. Keynotes significantly improve the quality, coordination and efficiency of our drawings by providing consistency in annotation and, in some instances, a direct correlation between drawing annotations and specifications. Some Studios maintain a keynote template that can be used as a starting point for individual projects. Discuss your Studio's process with your Technical Director.A keynote list must be made for each project and edited/updated in conjunction with the creation and editing of the specifications. The project architect and specification writer share responsibility for coordinating, editing, and maintaining the project keynote list, with the help of the rest of the project team.Specifics of the Keynoting System can be found on the General channel of the Technical Design Community MS Team: 2024 Keynoting ProtocolThe edited keynote list must be linked to the project’s Revit model and refreshed as new edits are made. Refer to Revit Guidelines for instructions on the application of keynotes types and the use of the keynote system.

title: Introduction UK
content: The Bidding & Negotiation (also known as Procurement) Phase of the project is the transition from design to construction. During this phase, the owner makes procurement documents available to prospective builders, who submit bids or proposals for price and schedule to complete the documented work. For thorough descriptions of typical architectural services during this phase, refer (for U.S. projects) toThe Architect’s Handbook of Professional Practice, published by the AIA, or the Project Delivery Practice Guide, published by CSI, or (for Canadian projects) to the Canadian Handbook of Practice for Architects. For Perkins&Will’s responsibilities during bidding and negotiation for your project, refer to your project’s owner-architect agreement.

title: Pre-bid Meeting UK
content: The pre-bid meeting is usually held at or near the site, so that prospective bidders have the opportunity to see the actual site conditions. Check your project’sowner-architect agreement to confirm specifically what is included in our scope of services. If we are responsible for preparing the agenda,use the template in Microsoft Word.The architect may be responsible for preparing and distributingmeeting minutes to all participants (check the owner-architect agreement). Because the minutes are not part of the procurement documents, nothing said at the meeting is binding until it has been issued as an addendum.

title: Requests for Information (RFI) UK
content: The protocols for the bidders to ask the owner or architect questions (RFIs) are established in the instructions to bidders (Division 00) and often further clarified in the pre-bid meeting. RFIs should all be addressed to a single person on the owner/architect team (usually an owner’s representative or a construction manager, but sometimes the architect). Responses should be issued to all the bidders, not just the ones who asked the questions. RFI responses are not contract documents until they are issued as part of an addendum.

title: Substitution Requests UK
content: The procedures for bidders to propose substitutions and for architects to evaluate and approve or reject them are defined in Divisions 00 and 01 of the project’s project manual. These procedures identify the information that the bidders need to provide that will allow the architect to evaluate the proposal. It is strongly preferred that Perkins&Will’s standard Substitution Request Form be used by bidders in requesting substitutions.As with RFIs, responses to substitution requests, either acceptance or rejection, must be distributed to all bidders and issued in an addendum to become part of the contract documents.

title: Addenda UK
content: Addenda are contract documents issued during the bid period (or, in some cases, up until the owner-contractor agreement is executed) that provide new or updated information to the bidders. Accepted substitution requests, RFI responses that impact the procurement documents, and revisions generated by the owner/architect team must be included in addenda to be considered contract documents. On the bid form, bidders must acknowledge receipt of all addenda in order to have a responsive bid.

title: Terminology UK
content: This phase has traditionally been known as “Construction Administration” (CA). However, the architect does not administer the construction of the project; that is the responsibility of the contractor. What the architect administers, as required by the typical Owner-Architect Agreement, are the provisions of the construction contract between the owner and the contractor. CSI has adopted the term “Construction Contract Administration” (CCA) in its Project Resource Manual and for its CCCA certification program. The AIA also uses CCA (as in Construction Contract Administration Knowledge Community). But if you still find yourself saying “CA,” you know that it stands for “Contract Administration.”To be consistent with the AIA and CSI, the Project Delivery Manual uses “Construction Contract Administration”(CCA).

title: CCA Startup Tasks UK
content: This checklist identifies the tasks that are typically performed by the CCA team at the start of construction. These tasks should be completed before the first application for payment is submitted by the contractor. The contractor and Owner should be engaged in the Pre-Construction Meeting. title: Ongoing CCA Tasks UK
content: This checklist identifies the weekly or monthly tasks that are routinely performed by the CCA team. This list should be reviewed by the CCA team on a weekly basis, at a regularly scheduled internal team meeting. SubmittalsReview submittal log for submittals due this weekTriage submittals for contractor approval and general completenessIdentify “high-priority” submittals due this weekReview and return all submittals due this weekFold and file all returned submittalsReview contractor’s submittal schedule for upcoming submittalsRFIsReview RFI log for outstanding RFIsIdentify “high-priority” RFIsIdentify non-compliant RFIsRespond to RFIs due this weekContractor’s Application for PaymentReview the “pencil draft” during site visitRespond with comments on the “pencil draft”Review revised application for paymentReview lien waiversCertify,issue and file the application for paymentSite ObservationVisit site (if required this week)Issue notices of non-compliance (if necessary)Review contractor’s record documents (monthly)Give file names to saved photographsIssue and file field observation reportChange OrdersReview outstanding change order request logReview current change order requestsGive change order requests to ownerPrepare and execute change ordersReview outstanding change order request logDocument ModificationsPrepare and issue ASIs (if required). Are specs required?Prepare and issue CCDs (if required). Are specs required?Review ASIs and CCDs with AHJs (if required)Construction Progress MeetingIdentify who from A/E team needs to attend this weekPrepare list of issues to discussWrite and distribute minutes/ Review minutes prepared by othersRespond to minutes with comments (if required)Construction ScheduleReview updated progress schedule from contractorProvide comments to the contractorInvoicingReview monthly invoice prepared by accountingIdentify potential additional servicesNotify owner of potential additional servicesReceive owner approval before proceeding with additional servicesLEED CertificationAttend LEED meetingReview LEED-required submittalsMaintain USGBC project siteTesting and InspectionReview testing to be performed this weekObserve tests (if required)Review testing and inspection reports received this weekReview contractor’s proposed corrective actions (if required)Prepare response to contractor’s proposed corrective actionsSubstitution RequestsIdentify reason for request (cause or convenience)Verify that request is compliant with contract documentsReview Substitution Request Form submitted by contractorRecommend approval or rejection to ownerOtherReview contractor’s request for a reduction of retainage (if applicable)Update project directory

content: This list identifies the tasks typically required by the CCA team to close out the construction contract. The team should start reviewing this list on a monthly basis as early as the midpoint of construction.
