title: Submittal Management UK
content: Submittal TriageThe project architect should triage, or preview, the submittal within a day of it being received. The purpose of the submittal triage is to determine the following:· Does the submittal comply with the requirements of the Contract Documents?Verify that the submittal has been stamped to show that it was reviewed and approved by the contractor. Make sure that the submittal does not include reissued Contract Documents. Make sure the submittal contents match the contractor’s letter of transmittal. If the submittal does not comply with the Contract Documents, stamp it with a “D-Rejected” action (see below Stamping Submittals) and return it immediately.· Have Safety Data Sheets (SDS) been submitted without being specifically required by the Contract Documents?They should only be required by the Contract Documents to verify sustainability compliance or VOC content. If they were not required, return them to the contractor with no action, and say on the transmittal they were not reviewed.· Does the submittal need to be reviewed by a consultant or by the owner?If so, forward it to the appropriate party promptly (see below Consultant Review of Submittals). Determine whether the review is primarily the responsibility of the consultant (e.g., catalog cuts of mechanical pumps), or if the architectural team needs to perform a simultaneous review for design intent or coordination (e.g., structural steel drawings or plumbing fixture cuts).· Is the contractor requesting an expedited review?Expedited review of submittals must be agreed upon by Perkins&Will before they are submitted.· Did the contractor or subcontractor reissue our Contract Documents as their shop drawing?Generally, our specifications require that theprepare project-specific information, drawn accurately to scale and do not allow shop drawings based on reproductions of the Contract Documents or standard printed data. If this is the case, stamp it with a “D-Rejected” action (see below Stamping Submittals) and return it immediately.The project team should be familiar with the submittal requirements described in Division 01.Consultant-Reviewed Submittal Triage· Has the consultant indicated a review action on the submittal?If not, contact the consultant to alert them of the oversight and immediately return the submittal to the consultant. The consultant’s action descriptions do not have to correspond to Perkins&Will’s action on the submittal (see below Stamping of Submittals Reviewed by Consultants). Please note that as primary holder of the Contract between the Design Team and Owner, Perkins&Will’s action on submittals governs/overrides the action of a consultant.· Are the consultant’s remarks and comments clear, understandable, and clouded?If not,contact the consultant to alert them of the issue and immediately return the submittal to the consultant.· Has the consultant made comments that are inconsistent with the Contract Documents?If so,contact the consultant to alert them of the issue and immediately return the submittal to the consultant.· Has the consultant made comments such as “architect to verify” on items that are not our responsibility to verify (e.g., field dimensions)?If so, contact the consultant to alert them of the issue and immediately return the submittal to the consultant.Stamping SubmittalsWhen our review and markups of a submittal is complete, apply the Perkins&Will submittal review stamp to it. If the entire submittal will receive the same action, the Perkins&Will submittal review stamp can be applied to the first page. If individual pages receive different actions, every page must have the Perkins&Will submittal review stamp on it.The primary reviewer should initial and date the review stamp in the “By:” line. Note that the submittal stamp should only be initialed, and NOT signed, by a Perkins&Will employee. Additionally, the primary reviewer is not required to sign, and should not sign, any other portion of a submittal. If the submittal includes language requiring the Architect to sign or 'Approve' the submittal prior to release for production, that text should be stricken as part of the Perkins&Will review.sign the submittalThe primary reviewer can be the Project Architect/Designer or a junior team member supervised by the Project Architect/Designer. Because of their potential effect on our risk management, some submittals should also be reviewed by the Project Architect/Designer. Determination of which submittals require this review is at the discretion of the Project Architect/Designer.Mark reviewed submittals with an appropriate “A,” “B,” “C,” “D,” “E,” or “F” action box on the review stamp. The following is an explanation of the actions used in submittal review:A – NO EXCEPTIONS: Work included in the submittal may proceed. The work generally is in compliance with the Contract Documents.B – EXCEPTIONS AS NOTED: Work included in the submittal may proceed provided the modifications noted are properly incorporated. Resubmission is not required.C – REVISE AND RESUBMIT: Work covered by the submittal cannot proceed until the submittal is revised per the A/E comments and resubmitted to the A/E team for review.D – REJECTED: Work covered by the submittal is not complete or does not conform to the Contract Documents and cannot proceed. A new submittal is required.E – FOR INFORMATION ONLY: Submittal is for record or information only. No approval is required by the A/E team. Compliance with Contract Documents is required.F – NOT REVIEWED: The submittal is not required by the Contract Documents.Resubmittals by the contractor of previously reviewed submittals (or sheets within a submittal) that received either an “A” or “B” action in our previous review shall be returned to the contractor without additional review. In cases where portions of individual submittals received “C” or “D” actions, only those portions should be resubmitted by the contractor. If the entire package is resubmitted, it should be returned without review. This policy should be made clear to the contractor at the outset of the project so that all parties are clear on the process and expectations.When a submittal consists of product brochures or catalogs, the product(s) that is being proposed for the project must be highlighted. If the product is not highlighted, the submittal should be returned with a “D” action.Submittals that are not specifically required in the Contract Documents shall be returned without review.Also see the Canadian Handbook of Practice for Architectsfor additional information on submittals for projects in Canada.Stamping of Consultant-Reviewed SubmittalsWhen the primary review of a submittal is performed by a consultant who is directly contracted to Perkins&Will (or when the consultant is contracted to the owner, but Perkins&Will is contractually obligated to receive submittals and coordinate responses with those of the owner’s consultant for submittal reviews (including submittals required by Specification Divisions 21 (Fire Suppression), 22 (Plumbing), 23 (HVAC), 26 (Electrical), 27 (Communications), 28 (Security), 31 (Earthwork) and 33 (Utilities), as well as some specification sections in Divisions 03 (Concrete), 05 (Metals), 32 (Exterior Improvements) and others), the submittal is to receive the Perkins&Will submittal stamp in addition to the consultant’s submittal stamp.The action marked in the Perkins&Will stamp should be as follows:1. If the consultant submittal action is equivalent to Perkins&Will’s “Not Reviewed,” “For Information Only,” or “Rejected” actions, the action marked on Perkins&Will’s stamp should be the same.2. If the consultant submittal action is equivalent to Perkins&Will’s “Revise and Resubmit” or “Exceptions as Noted” actions, and Perkins&Will has no further comments on the submittal, then the action marked on Perkins&Will’s stamp should bethe same.3. If the consultant submittal action is equivalent to Perkins&Will’s “Exceptions as Noted” or “No Exceptions” actions, and Perkins&Will has added comments significant enough that resubmittal is required, the action marked on Perkins&Will’s stamp should be “Revise and Resubmit.”4. If the consultant submittal action is equivalent to Perkins&Will’s “Exceptions as Noted” or “No Exceptions” actions, and Perkins&Will has added comments that are not significant enough to require a resubmittal, the action marked on Perkins&Will’s stamp should be “Exceptions as Noted.”5. If the consultant submittal action is equivalent to Perkins&Will’s “No Exceptions” action, and Perkins&Will has no additional comments, the action marked on Perkins&Will’s stamp should be “No Exceptions.”When the consultant is not directly contracted to Perkins&Will, and Perkins&Will is not contractually obligated to receive submittals and coordinate its response with that of the owner’s consultant for submittal reviews, the submittal should not be submitted to Perkins&Will. If it is, then the action marked in the Perkins&Will stamp should be “Not Reviewed.”Filing SubmittalsSubmittals are to be filed in the Consultants/07-CCA-Mgmnt/Submittals folder. Each submittal should be filed in a subfolder named using the Contractor-generated submittal name. Only file the submittal that was sent to the contractor with Perkins&Will’s action on it.Physical samples should be filed in a central location in the Studio. When the project is closed out, discard the samples.Also see CCA Automation using PowerAutomate for information on setting up automatic processes that will streamline submittal intake and management.

title: Site Visits and Observations UK
content: Site VisitsFrom AIA Document B101-2017:"Article 3.6.2.1 The Architect shall visit the site at intervals appropriate to the stage of construction, or as otherwise required in Section 4.2.3, to become generally familiar with the progress and quality of the portion of the Work completed, and to determine, in general, if the Work observed is being performed in a manner indicating that the Work, when fully completed, will be in accordance with the Contract Documents. However, the Architect shall not be required to make exhaustive or continuous on-site inspections to check the quality or quantity of the Work. On the basis of the site visits, the Architect shall keep the Owner reasonably informed about the progress and quality of the portion of the Work completed, and promptly report to the Owner (1) known deviations from the Contract Documents, (2) known deviations from the most recent construction schedule submitted by the Contractor, and (3) defects and deficiencies observed in the Work."Additionally, from AIA Document A201-2017:“Article 4.2.2 The Architect will visit the site at intervals appropriate to the stage of construction, or as otherwise agreed with the Owner, to become generally familiar with the progress and quality of the portion of the Work completed, and to determine in general if the Work is being performed in a manner indicating that the Work, when fully completed, will be in accordance with the Contract Documents. However, the Architect will not be required to make exhaustive or continuous on-site inspections to check the quality or quantity of the Work. The Architect will not have control over, charge of, or responsibility for, the construction means, methods, techniques, sequences or procedures, or for the safety precautions and programs in connection with the Work, since these are solely the Contractor’s rights and responsibilities under the Contract Documents, except as provided in Section 3.3.1.”The number or frequency of site visits should be defined in the Owner-Architect Agreement. It is best if a precise frequency can be agreed upon.The A/E team may confer with the contractor to provide clarifications to the Contract Documents. If the contractor requests an interpretation, it can be discussed, but the formal question and response must be done via the RFI process. However, the main purpose of the site visit is to determine the progress and quality of the work.Field Observation Reports: The “Field Observation Report” is an instrument of service to fulfill the architect’s responsibilities defined in the Owner-Architect Agreement. The A/E team shall observe the work during each site visit and document the progress and quality of the work as indicated in the General Conditions. See Record Keeping and Reporting below for more information.Other than at Substantial and Final Completion, the architect is not required to make inspections of the work. The architect is not responsible for construction means, methods, site safety precautions or procedures.Also see the Canadian Handbook of Practice for Architects for additional information on projects in Canada.Non-Conforming WorkArticle 4.2.2 of the AIA Document A201-2017, General Conditions of the Contract for Construction, requires the architect to determine if the work is being performed in accordance with the Contract Documents.In the performance of this duty the architect has the responsibility to notify the owner and contractor of all nonconforming activities and installations in the work.The instrument of service for reporting all nonconforming work is the Notice of Noncompliance, which is available as a template in Microsoft Word.The Notice of Noncompliance will specifically address the drawing or project manual section that is the subject of noncompliance.On-Site ConductWhen on site, Perkins&Will staff must comply with all contractor safety procedures and must wear personal protective equipment (PPE), at a minimum, a construction helmet, safety glasses, and a safety vest. Staff must also comply with any additional PPE rules and procedures required by the Contractor.While on site, Perkins&Will staff should maintain a professional demeanor, be polite and courteous, show respect, follow the proper lines of communication, and follow the contractor’s protocols for site visitors.Record Keeping and ReportingIn the Field Observation Report, the architect documents the work performed and items observed during the site visit. Reports are to be distributed to the owner, contractor, and consultants, and to the Perkins&Will project team. Reports are to be completed and distributed promptly after the site visit. They are to be objective and factual.The preferred method for creating Field Observation Reports is to use BIM360 on a tablet, or other mobile device, while on the project site. Instructions on setting up the template, creating field observations and exporting a report can be found in the CCA Modernization QuickStart Guide and this Core eXchange Presentation.Field Observation Reports created using BIM360 should be exported as PDFs and then transmitted to the Owner, Contractor, Consultants and the Perkins&Will project team via e-mail, the Contractor's PIMS or whatever method the broader project team has agreed upon. The open Issues can also be exported to an .xlsx (MS Excel) file and transmitted to the Contractor for input into the PIMS. See the CCA Modernization QuickStart Guide for more information and instruction.Typically, we should not invite the Contractor to our BIM360 project for the purpose of transmitting FORs or responding to Issues contained in those reports.If requested by the Owner, it is acceptable for our teams to document field observations in the Contractor’s Project Information Management System (PIMS, e.g. Procore, eBuilder, CMIC), as long as:The PIMS allows the inclusion of all information noted as required for FORs belowThe PIMS allows exporting a report that includes only current. Perkins&Will-generated open items (both new and rolled over from previous observations)We export and retain a copy in our project fileWe have a record of transmitting the report to the Owner and ContractorNote that when working int the Contractor's PIMS, in-progress items we create are viewable by the Contractor, so we need to be careful and judicious in what we write, even in draft form. It is recommended that the following hardware be purchased (as a project cost) for each project entering CCA:iPad Air (LINK)Apple Pencil - Generation 2 (LINK)Case with Shoulder Strap (Example)Project team leaders should coordinate with local IT for this purchase.The reports are to include, at a minimum, the following information (input in the "Message" field in the BIM360 template):1.	Project name and number2. Date of visit3. Weather and approximate temperature4. Others present during the visit5. Approximate construction completion percentageIssues:1. Focus on identifying deficiencies and non-conforming work.2. If an Issue includes a 'dropped pin', BIM360 will automatically include a cropped plan showing the area in question. Note that the user cannot control the size or extent of the plan area.3. BIM360 will automatically assign a sequential ID number to each Issue. This numbering is sufficient and does not need to be manually altered with prefixes or suffixes, as it will create a unique identifier for each Issue that can be tracked through the life of the project. Note that a BIM360-generated ID number cannot be deleted once created. Depending on how Issues are created, BIM360 may create gaps in the Issue numbering sequence. These gaps are acceptable and need not be corrected. See the CCA Modernization QuickStart Guide for information on creating Issues.4. Include the location of each Issue.*5. Issues can be used to document general site progress for purposes of recording the percentage of construction completion, certifying applications for payment and demonstrating the contractor's adherence to the published project schedule.**6. Unresolved (Open) Issues from previous Field Observation Reports should appear on successive reports until the Issue has been resolved. Updates to the Issue, including resolution, should be recorded in the Issue's "Description" field.* When setting a project up in BIM360 for use in Field Observation Reporting, teams should take the time to 'draw' regions in BIM360 and assign them to rooms in the project floor plans. While there is setup time required for this step, it will allow BIM360 to automatically correlate the location of each Issue with a room or space in the floor plan, ultimately saving time and increasing the accuracy of the FORs. See the CCA Modernization QuickStart Guide for information and instructions on this process.** General progress Issues should be closed immediately after generating the FOR so that they do not carry over to the subsequent report. See the CCA Modernization QuickStart Guide for instruction on closing Issues.Photographs:General guidance for taking on-site photographs1. Site photographs should not be taken separate from the FOR process.2. Perkins&Will representatives on construction sites or related out-of-office locations will take photographs of only the following:a. Illustration of narratives in a Field Observation Reportb. General site conditions, but only to document overall progress for the purpose of recording percentage of completion.c. The general state of a specific scope of work, or discipline, for the purpose of recording percentage of completion.d. Conditions that do not comply with the Contract Documents.e. Conditions that require follow-up action by owner/client, constructor, or design team.3. All photographs transferred to and saved in Perkins&Will servers and devices are to be used to illustrate comments in Field Observation Reports or Notices of Noncompliance.4. All photographs transferred to and saved on Perkins&Will servers will be thoroughly reviewed for content prior to completion of the associated field report.5. All photographs transferred to Perkins&Will servers will be named according to Project Delivery Manual Section: Project Document Management.6. Photographs associated with Field Observation Reports created in BIM360 do not need to be downloaded to the project file.Content of PhotographsPhotographs of the construction work in progress can be valuable aids to Perkins&Will’s CCA staff. However, each photograph should be taken only to document one or more of the following:When a representative of Perkins&Will takes a photograph on a construction site or related out-of-office location and saves it on a mobile device (whether company provided or personal) or transfers it to our servers, it can be reasonably assumed that the photographer knows the content and purpose of the image. It is therefore critical that when we deliberately and knowingly photograph site or other construction-related conditions, rather than capture images indiscriminately.Photographs shouldn’t be taken “just in case they might come in handy later.”Review of Image ContentRegardless why a photograph is taken, the photographer must examine the image content. By taking a photograph and saving it on a company-provided device or our server, we make a tacit representation that we are familiar with the content and that we will alert appropriate parties when the recorded conditions require action. If we indiscriminately photograph site conditions and save them on our devices or servers without knowing their content, we risk being accused of negligence if we have inadvertently captured a noncompliant condition without identifying an action on someone’s part.Our having photographed a noncompliant conditioning without realizing it is there leaves us vulnerable to claims of negligence and liability for costs of correction due to a lack of timely action.Photographic File NamingIf photographs are taken outside of BIM360 (discouraged) it is important that they be filed and named according to the protocol described in PDM Chapter 2 section Project Document Management. Project construction can span years, during which we can accumulate hundreds, even thousands, of purposeful photographs. If these photos are filed by date only and with device-default file names (e.g., IMG_1333.jpg, DSCN0026.jpg, etc.), it will be enormously time consuming at best, and nearly impossible at worst, to find an image of a specific condition in the future. Tools are available from our Digital Practice group to batch-change file names to speed the process of renaming, if required. This requirement for naming photographs will also deter excessive site photographing and will help ensure that only deliberate and meaningful images are captured.Use of On-Site Photographs for Marketing and Posting to Social Media Platforms1. When a representative of Perkins&Will takes a photograph on a construction site or related out-of-office location, use of that image for personal social media posting is prohibited.2. The use of on-site photographs for Perkins&Will marketing material (Proposals, Qualifications Statements and similar) and social media (whether for an individual Studio or Firmwide account) is generally discouraged. On-site photographs may be used for these purposes only under the following conditions:a. The image documents a construction milestone (e.g. topping-out ceremony), important client visit (e.g. client's Board site visit) or other significant eventb. The image is first reviewed and approved by Firmwide Marketing and the Global Director of Technical Design.What do we do if the Contractor regularly copies us on their construction progress photos?If we are on a distribution list for Contractor progress photographs, whether we are the sole recipient or one of many, our receipt of these images creates a responsibility to review the content. If you are in this position, please follow one of the following 3 possible courses of action:1. Continue to receive the photographs without defining our role relative to them (discouraged) - If we continue to receive progress photos, and do not define what we will do with them, by default we must review each one critically and carefully and notify the Client and Contractor of anything we have concerns with or that we believe to be nonconforming with the Contract Documents. This is likely to be work we do not get paid for, as it is not a basic service.2. Ask to be removed from the distribution (preferred) - If we aren’t going to look at them critically, we should ask to be removed from the distribution. This may be viewed unfavorably by the Client, so we have to make a careful and considered decision here.3. Continue to receive the photographs, but communicate our role to the Client and Contractor - If we don't want to risk negative perception by asking to be removed from the distribution, we will need to go on record to both the Client and Contractor stating the following (though not necessarily in these words):a. Perkins&Will will not review the photographs for conformance with the contract documents, project schedule or payment applications and will not comment on their content, unless asked to do so on a specific issue or condition.b. Perkins&Will will not retain the email or the images in Outlook or in our project files.What do we do if we have access to a live webcam?It is permissible to view a live webcam for general interest and to keep appraised of progress on site. Do not, however, take screenshots or other stills of the webcam images, or otherwise save webcam information to the project file.What is not to be included in the Field Observation Report:1. Extraneous information2. Sarcastic remarks3. Suppositions, hearsay or conjecture4. Nonfactual judgments or feelings5. Information about individuals or events not related to the project.6. Contractor safety requirements or procedures7. The BIM360 Field Observation Report template includes several unwanted fields in each Issue that cannot be deleted: Due Date, Assigned To, Root Cause. These fields should not be filled in and should be left blank on all Field Observation Reports.

title: Interpretations Modifications and Substitutions UK
content: Requests For Interpretation (RFIs)RFIs clarify the intent of the Contract Documents and should only be used when the contractor discovers one of the following:1. An unforeseeable condition during the course of construction2. A conflict between the various components of the Contract Documents3. The omission of necessary information within the Contract DocumentsThe design team should not accept RFIs if they are used as substitution requests, submittals, requests for corrective action, clarifications of subcontractor’s scope, or claims. Guidelines for RFI procedures should be defined in the Contract Documents to avoid any misunderstandings.The AIA estimates that as a project team, we spend an average of 17-man hours per RFI. It is therefore in the project team’s best interest to minimize the number of RFI’s to only those that are necessary and avoid the submission of open-ended RFI questions. Reviewing any current or pending RFI’s with the on-site project team during visits can help reduce the number of redundant RFI’s and limit the submission of post-visit RFI’s to those for confirmation only.If an RFI asks for information that is already in the Contract Documents, it can be answered directly on the RFI form and returned to the Contractor.For any other situation, RFI responses must be in the form of one of the following: Proposal Request, Architect’s Supplemental Instructions (ASI) or Construction Change Directive (CCD) in order to formally incorporate the response into the Contract Documents. See below Modifications and Changes to the Contract.RFI Management Perkins&Will’s standard specification for project management and coordination (Section 01 31 00) requires the Contractor to utilize a Project Information Management System (PIMS) for the submission, management and receipt of RFIs and their responses. The PIMS should be the primary means of managing and tracking RFIs. Perkins&Will teams should also work with their local Digital Practice team to set up automations to receive, file and manage RFIs internally. See the CCA Modernization QuickStart Guide for more information.Response TimeThe allowable response time is typically defined in specification section 01 31 00 Project Management and Coordination. If the response time is not defined in the specifications, it should be discussed, agreed and documented at the initial meeting with the Owner and Contractor.RFI TriageThe project architect should triage, or preview, the RFI immediately upon its arrival. The purpose of the triage is to determine the following:1. Is the RFI written clearly enough to understand what the question or issue is so you can respond to it?If not, return the RFI to the contractor, requesting clarification.2. Is the question or issue the RFI addressed within Perkins&Will’s contractual scope?If not, return the RFI without response.3. Is it immediately evident that the RFI is requesting information that is addressed in the Contract Documents?If so, include in your response that this was an inappropriate RFI. (These are often called, by architects at least) “frivolous RFIs.” Keep a log of all such RFIs; you’ll be grateful you did when the contractor says that there are too many RFIs on the project.)4. Is the contractor requesting an expedited review, and is it justified by the project schedule?Requests for expedited reviews should be accompanied by the reasons for the request. Expedited review of RFIs must be agreed upon by Perkins&Will before they are issued. If that agreement is not in place, notify the contractor that the RFI will be returned as quickly as possible, but may not be returned before the standards response time limit.5. Does the RFI include a recommendation from the Contractor/Subcontractor?If not, return as an incomplete requesting the Contractor’s recommended solution.Consultant-Reviewed RFI Triage Immediately upon receiving a response from the consultant, check the response to determine the following:1. Is the consultant’s response clear, understandable and complete?If not, call the consultant to find out what was intended.2. Does the consultant’s response answer the contractor’s question?If not, return the RFI to the consultant for a better response.3. Has the consultant made comments that are inconsistent with the Contract Documents?If so, return the RFI to the consultant for a better response.Filing RFIsRFIs are to be filed in the project file: Consultants/07-CCA-Mgmnt/RFIs Only file the version of the RFI that was sent to the contractor with Perkins&Will’s response.Also see CCA Automation using PowerAutomate for information on setting up automatic processes that will streamline RFI intake and management.Modifications and Changes to the ContractProposal RequestsProposal Requests are written documents prepared by the architect describing a proposed change to the project. Based on the Proposal Request, the contractor will evaluate how the proposed change will affect the project cost and schedule and will submit a change proposal for Owner and Architect review. The work described will not proceed until the proposal is accepted by the Owner. The Proposal Request is available as a template in Microsoft Word in the pwConstructionAdmin folder.The signatory on a Proposal Request is signing on behalf of Perkins&Will, and the Architect of Record as a designee and would not generally be considered to be acting as a licensed architect. As such, most jurisdictions would permit any employee to sign Proposal Requests. Be sure to review the laws and rules of the project's jurisdiction to confirm.Architect’s Supplemental InstructionsArchitect’s Supplemental Instructions (ASI) are minor changes not affecting contract cost or schedule. If the contractor believes that the ASI will involve changes in cost or time, a change order request may be submitted.The work described in theASI is to proceed immediately. If the Contractor proceeds with the work without notifying the Owner and Architect that they believe it constitutes a change to the cost or schedule, they waive the right to request additional time or fee later. TheASI is available as a template in Microsoft Word in the pwConstructionAdmin folder.Construction Change DirectivesConstruction Change Directives (CCD) direct the contractor to make a change with the understanding that an agreement on cost and/or schedule impact will be made later. Under a CCD, the contractor performs the work whether or not an agreement has been reached before the start of work incorporating the proposed changes.Construction change directive process:1. Architect prepares CCD and reviews it with the owner2. Owner signs CCD3. Architect issues the CCD to the contractor4. Contractor proceeds with the changes to the work.5. Time and cost changes are negotiated and agreed upon by all parties before becoming a Change OrderBecause the contractor is at risk when performing work under a CCD, the owner and architect need to make every effort to fairly evaluate the contractor’s compensation for the work. CCDs should be used only when required by the project schedule, not routinely for every change.The CCD is available as a template in Microsoft Word in the pwConstructionAdmin folder.The signatory on a CCD is signing on behalf of Perkins&Will, and the Architect of Record as a designee and would not generally be considered to be acting as a licensed architect. As such, most jurisdictions would permit any employee to sign CCDs. Be sure to review the laws and rules of the project's jurisdiction to confirm.Change OrdersA Change Order providesdirection to the contractor to make a change with an agreed upon adjustment to the project cost and/or schedule.Roles in the change order process:1. The contractor starts the process by submitting a change order request (COR) to the owner and architect for review. The COR can be limited to a single issue or combine multiple cost and schedule issues.2. Perkins&Will analyzes the contractor’s proposed changes to contract sum and/or time and documents reasons for recommendation for acceptance, modification or rejection of the COR.3. If we recommend modifications or rejection of the COR, negotiations among the owner, contractor and Perkins&Will will result in a COR that can be approved.4. If the owner accepts the proposed changes, the contractor prepares the change order form, stipulating the approved changes in cost and/or schedule.5. Perkins&Will signs the change order and forwards to the contractor for signature.a. Legally, the signatory on a Change Order is signing on behalf of Perkins&Will, and the Architect of Record as a designee and would not generally be considered to be acting as a licensed architect. As such, most jurisdictions would permit any employee to sign Change Orders. Be sure to review the laws and rules of the project's jurisdiction to confirm.b. Perkins&Will's policy is that Change Orders are signed by the project's Senior Project Architect/Designer/Manager or Project Architect/Designer/Manager.6 The contractor reviews and signs all copies of accepted change orders7. The owner signs and executes all copies of the change order; it then becomes part of the contract betweenthe owner and contractorSubstitutionsSubstitution requests are proposals from the contractor to use a manufacturer or product different from that required in the Contract Documents. The project requirements governing the submission of substitutions are defined in specification section 01 25 00 Substitution Procedures. Substitutions must be submitted independently of the submittal or RFI processes, and as a stand-alone process. Perkins&Will’s services to evaluate substitutions and to make revisions to the Contract Documents for accepted substitutions should be considered additional services.The burden of proof to demonstrate that the proposed substitution meets the requirements of the Contract Documents rests with the contractor.However, once a substitution is accepted, Perkins&Will (as the Architect of Record) assumes all responsibility for the suitability, performance and compatibility of the substituted product.There are two basic types of substitution request:Substitutions for Convenience: The contractor may submit as a substitution request a product or system that potentially could benefit the project or the owner. The Project Manual will define under which conditions a Substitution for Convenience may be allowed. Substitutions for Convenience typically may be submitted within specified time periods during bidding and following the signing of the Owner-Contractor Agreement. In some cases, the specifications may stipulate that no Substitutions for Convenience will be considered at any time.In reviewing Substitutions for Convenience, the architect must be assured that the proposed substitution demonstrably gives the owner greater value, in cost, time, or quality, than the specified product or system. This could mean that the proposed product will perform better at equal cost, or perform equally well at a lower cost, or perform better at a lower cost. In evaluating potential cost savings, any collateral costs, such as the architect’s additional service fee for reviewing the substitution and modifying the Contract Documents, need to be considered.Substitutions for Cause: If a specified product is no longer manufactured or available, or cannot reasonably be brought to the site in the time required by the project schedule, or is not appropriate for its intended use or location on the project, the contractor may request a Substitution for Cause. Unlike Substitutions for Convenience, there are usually no time restrictions for when Substitutions for Cause can be submitted.In reviewing Substitutions for Cause, the architect and owner have to be assured of the validity of the cause. As part of the substitution review, the architect needs to determine if the real cause of the substitution is the result of the contractor’s failure to perform in accordance with the Contract Documents. If this is the case, the contractor is to bear all additional costs for providing products or systems that are equal to or better than the specified products or systems.Evaluation of Substitution RequestsItems to consider in evaluating proposed substitutions:1. Reason for unavailability of the specified item2. Benefit to the Owner in terms of cost savings or schedule reduction3. Total cost impact, including owner costs, such as architect’s additional services resulting from the substitution4. Potential maintenance issues/long term impacts to the Owner5. Performance of the proposed product6. Consistency with project criteria7. Impact on project schedule8. Impact on project coordination9. Impact on other building systems, components, and materials10. Acceptance of manufacturer11. Compliance with codes12. Warranties13. Operational characteristics14. Finishes consistent with design intent15. Qualifications of fabricator and installer16. Installation requirements consistent with project conditionsThe substitution request must contain the following information:1. Name, address, and phone number of person making request2. Name and location of project3. Specification section in which the product is specified4. Generic title of product5. Manufacturer, brand, and model number of proposed product6. List of projects where product has been used7. List of references familiar with the product8. Product data and (where appropriate) samples9. Test data establishing performance equality10. Side-by-side comparisons of original and proposed products11. List of ways in which proposed product deviates from the Contract Documents12. Anticipated cost impact, with $0 being explicitly listed if no impact. Do not accept a blank line for cost impact.13. Anticipated schedule impact, with 0 days being explicitly listed if no impact. Do not accept a blank line for schedule impact.Acceptance or Rejection of Substitution RequestsSubstitution requests must be submitted by the Contractor on the Substitution Request Form issued by Perkins&Will as part of the project manual. Substitutions submitted on any other form should be returned to the Contractor without review.All substitution requests should be reviewed with the Studio's Technical Director and the project's Managing Principal. Following the substitution request review, the architect will recommend that the owner either accept or reject the request by completing and signing the "Architect's Review and Action" section of the substitution request form.Legally, the signatory on a Substitution Request is signing on behalf of Perkins&Will, and the Architect of Record as a designee and would not generally be considered to be acting as a licensed architect. As such, most jurisdictions would permit any employee to sign Substitution Requests. Be sure to review the laws and rules of the project's jurisdiction to confirm.Perkins&Will's Policy is that only the project's Senior Project Architect/Designer or Project Architect/Designer may sign the substitution request.When the substitution request form is completed and signed, it is sent to the Owner for their review and action. Only the owner can accept or reject a substitution.The standard AIA Owner-Architect Agreements include substitution review as a “pre-approved” additional service. However, it is advisable to alert the owner before starting the review that it will be an additional service.

title: Construction Contract Closeout UK
content: Contract CloseoutContract Closeout comprises the activities to be performed by contractors, subcontractors, suppliers, manufacturers, architects, consultants, and owners related to completing the work as defined in the Owner- Contractor Agreement. Performing these activities is necessary for occupancy of the work by the owner, Final Completion, final payment to the contractor, and normal termination of the contract. On phased-occupancy projects, contract closeout occurs at different times as portions of the project are completed and occupied at different times.The requirements for Contract Closeout are primarily defined in Division 01 of the Project Manual. The most relevant sections are 01 77 00 Closeout Procedures, 01 78 23 Operation and Maintenance Data, 01 78 39 ProjectRecord Documents, 01 79 00 Demonstration and Training, 01 81 13 Sustainable Design Requirements, and 01 91 13 General Commissioning Requirements. Individual specification sections may have additional closeout requirements that apply to only the products specified in that section. The General Conditions and SupplementaryConditions contain information about fulfilling contract requirements.Closeout MeetingsWell before substantial completion, regular meetings should be held to specifically address closeout issues. Participants should include the owner, contractor, architect, consultants, and facility manager.Substantial CompletionSubstantial Completion is defined as the point where the project is sufficiently completed so that the owner can occupy and use the building/space/phase for its intended purpose. The time of Substantial Completion is established when the architect issues the Certificate of Substantial Completion (AIA G704) following an inspection of the work.The process for Substantial Completion begins when the contractor submits the initial list documenting items needing to be completed or corrected, to the architect (commonly, though not officially, known as a ‘punch list’). The architect then inspects the project to determine if the work is substantially complete and supplements the contractor’s ‘punch list’ with additional items that need to be completed or corrected. During the inspection, the architect should have available a list of deficiencies noted on observation reports and a copy of the Contract Documents.If it is determined that Substantial Completion has not been achieved, the inspection should be terminated. The architect may be entitled to additional fee for additional inspections.Following the inspection, the architect issues a Certificate of Substantial Completion. The certificate must identify the date of Substantial Completion. The ‘punch list’ for incomplete or incorrect items should be attached to the form, and a time period in which the punch list items need to be completed or corrected should be identified. The certificate is to be signed by, and copies distributed to, the owner, contractor, and architect.The date of Substantial Completion has long been accepted as the base date for all time-related considerations of the contract, including the owner’s occupancy, payment for fuels and services, and responsibility for operating building systems, insurance changeover, starting date for warranties and the start of the owner’s responsibility for security and maintenance.Certificate of OccupancyThe contractor must submit a Certificate of Occupancy or other documents issued by the appropriate building official confirming that the required inspections have been completed and that the project meets the requirements of the authorities. Note that the Certificate of Substantial Completion (a contractual process) and the Certificate of Occupancy (a regulatory process) are separate processes and are not co-dependent. Perkins&Will is not typically involved in the Certificate of Occupancy process.Final Inspection and Final PaymentFor Final Completion, as for Substantial Completion, the contractor requests the architect to make an inspection to determine if the work is “acceptable under the Contract Documents and the contract fully performed.” If it is, the architect issues a final Certificate for Payment. Again, many items must be submitted to establish that obligations have been satisfied before the contractor can request the inspection for Final Completion, including evidence that the Substantial Completion inspection list (‘punch list’) of items has been completed or corrected.Final payment by the owner represents final acceptance of the work. The owner waives all claims except liens, claims, security interests or encumbrances arising out of the contract and unsettled; failure of the work to comply with the Contract Documents; and terms of special warranties required by the Contract Documents. In accepting the final payment, the contractor waives all claims except those previously made in writing and identified as unsettled.Closeout SubmittalsThe specifications define the required closeout submittals due from the Contractor. These often include:1. O&M data for systems/equipment2. Contractor-produced record documents3. Spare parts and extra stock materials4. Special warranties and bonds5. Certificates and affidavits6. KeyingUnless a project's Contract Documents state otherwise, closeout submittals should be treated as Informational Submittals, reviewed for conformance with the Contract Documents and stamped with one of the following actions:C – REVISE AND RESUBMIT: The submission is partially, but not completely, in conformance with the contract documents and must be resubmitted to the A/E team for review.D – REJECTED: The submittal is not complete or does not conform to the Contract Documents. A new submittal is required.E – FOR INFORMATION ONLY: Submittal is in conformance with the requirements of the Contract Documents.F – NOT REVIEWED: The submittal is not required by the Contract Documents.Perkins&Will is also typically responsible for verifying that all required closeout documents have been submitted by the Contractor. Once all required closeout submittals have been submitted and have received E-action, Perkins&Will should inform the Contractor that they can be transmitted to the Owner.Note that submission of closeout submittals is a requirement of the Contract Documents. If they have not been submitted, reviewed and transmitted to the Owner at the time of Substantial Completion (see above) that should be included on the 'punch list' of incomplete items that accompanies the Certificate of Substantial Completion and their value should be included in the cost estimate of work to be completed or corrected.Perkins&Will-produced Record DocumentsThe architect’s responsibility with regards to record documents can vary from project to project; make sure you know what your Owner-Architect Agreement says. If you are using the AIA B101-2017 Standard Form of Agreement Between Owner and Architect, the architect’s role in producing record documents is an additional service. However, when we are working under an owner-written contract, or a modified B101, it is not unusual for record document preparation to be included under basic services. In that case, our responsibility, as well as our consultants’ responsibilities, are usually limited to incorporating the Contractor-produced record drawing information into the Contract Documents. Because we are basing our record documents on information provided by the Contractor, we should not certify or otherwise state that our record documents represent the as-built conditions.If we are required to prepare record documents, we should use the most recent version of the Contract Documents as their basis. All previous revision clouds and deltas should be removed, and all new changes based on Contractor-provided information should be identified by clouds and deltas. The deltas should be tied to the revision block of each sheet, and identified as “Record Documents.” If editable electronic copies (e.g. .dwg or .rvt files) of these documents are given to the owner, the Perkins&Will name and logo, and the names of our consultants, should be removed from the title blocks of each sheet.It is recommended that each sheet of the record drawing set include the following statement:This record drawing has been prepared, in part, based upon information furnished by others. While this information is believed to be reliable, Perkins&Will cannot assure its accuracy, and thus is not responsible for the accuracy of this record drawing. Those relying on this record document are advised to obtain independent verification of its accuracy before applying it for any purpose.Record documents should not be called “as-built documents.” Neither the AIA nor CSI recognize the term and it implies a level of site verification that we are not responsible for.Record documents should not be sealed by Perkins&Will or our consultants unless it is specifically required by either the Owner-Architect Agreement or the Authority Having Jurisdiction. If a seal is required, the recommended statement above is required.Warranties and Correction PeriodTypically, within one year of the date of Substantial Completion, the contractor is required to correct any work not in conformance with the Contract Documents upon written notice of the owner. The Owner-Architect Agreement may require the architect to perform a site walk to identify any nonconforming work prior to the termination of the warranty period.Post-Occupancy Services:Post-occupancy services are typically additional services not specifically addressed in the Owner-Architect agreement. These services can include assisting the owner in the initial occupancy and ongoing operation of the project, or a post-occupancy evaluation.
